name: Anonymous, CPA, Corporate Accounting, Retail Investor
role: CPA, retail investor; Big 4 + Fortune 500 background
summary: Self-described conservative retail investor and CPA with Big 4 and Fortune 500 experience. Generally favors lighter-touch regulation but strongly opposes this specific proposal. Frames it as political cronyism following Trump's call to end quarterly reporting and Chair Atkins's fast-tracking of it. Argues 10-S election will benefit insiders, invite earnings management and delayed bad news, and disillusion the next generation of finance and accounting professionals.
rationale_evidence: blatant political cronyism; permitting 10-S election will benefit insiders, invite earnings management and delayed bad news

name: Fardeen Irani
summary: One of the few clearly supportive letters. Argues the 10-Q has become a 'compliance tax' of 1,000+ burden hours per filing, and that smaller issuers can reclaim 2,000-3,000 executive hours per year for R&D and long-term growth. Suggests pairing with rigorous 8-K material-event reporting and letting the market price transparency through opt-in cost of capital signals.
rationale_evidence: 1,000+ burden hours per 10-Q; 90-day cycle fuels short-termism; consistent with UK/EU standards

name: Anonymous
summary: Argues quarterly reporting drives CEO short-termism and bad behaviors that he witnessed first-hand at a large bank during the 2016 (Wells Fargo) cross-selling scandal. Frames the proposal as a way to push corporate America back toward durable products and services, and away from layoffs and cheap materials done to hit quarterly numbers.
rationale_evidence: Quarterly reporting drives short-term results; witnessed Wells Fargo 2016 scandal first-hand

name: Anonymous
summary: One sentence: 'This is a really bad idea.'
rationale_evidence: 'This is a really bad idea'

name: Arturo Zuniga
summary: Notes the proposal aligns the U.S. with international standards but argues U.S. tax structure is different and the 10-Q serves needs international peers' reports do not. Concludes that if burden reduction is the goal, this is the wrong way and the change would 'leave us in the dark for half a year.'
rationale_evidence: leaves us in the dark for half a year; aligning with international standards is wrong

name: Derek White
role: Individual investor; former hotel real-estate manager (sole-income from portfolio)
summary: Strongly opposes; wants MORE frequent reporting (monthly or weekly). Cites his prior experience managing several billion dollars of hotel real estate, where he received daily, weekly, and monthly reports as a matter of course. Argues any well-managed company already has the data, and reducing public reporting puts retail investors at a huge disadvantage. His sole income is from his investment portfolio.
rationale_evidence: retail investors at HUGE DISADVANTAGE; asymmetry of data with insiders/employees/regulators worsens

name: Jason Couchman
role: ISC2 CISSP; IT/IS audit and assurance professional
summary: Argues quarterly testing of pervasive IT controls is essential governance and costs only 1-3% of profit, so the cost-burden argument is weak. Warns of a market-wide race to the bottom: if SEC sets a 'low bar' on cadence, competing public companies will both reduce compliance to keep parity, degrading governance broadly. Worries semiannual cadence will surface failing controls only late in the annual cycle.
rationale_evidence: competitive race-to-bottom: 'if SEC shifts to semi-annual, neither company has incentive to do more work; standards will degrade market-wide'

name: Jeanne James
summary: One sentence supporting the proposal because it allows companies to focus on long-term growth rather than short-term quarterly targets.
rationale_evidence: allows companies to focus on long-term growth rather than short-term quarterly targets

name: L. Abott
summary: Itemized rebuttal of the typical pro-arguments. (1) Cost argument is spurious: reporting infrastructure already exists and AI is making it cheaper. (2) Long-term-planning argument: if leadership does not like the cadence, they can take the company private. Frames public-company status as carrying a transparency obligation. Worries semiannual filing creates more cover for insider trading.
rationale_evidence: compliance infrastructure exists and gets cheaper with AI; less frequency provides cover for insider trading

name: Michael Gower, CPA
summary: Strongly opposed. Frames short-termism as a management issue, not a reporting issue: if leadership provides reasonable long-term guidance and meets it, investors will follow. Argues semiannual filing only benefits unethical companies by helping them 'kick the can' of bad news down the road.
rationale_evidence: change will cause companies to hide critical data; gives incentive to 'kick the can' on bad news

name: REITup Advisers
role: Former CFO, CAO and partner at firm leading SEC reporting
summary: Pragmatic, mechanics-focused. Asks what Form 10-S will actually require (suggests something between 10-Q and 10-K, perhaps modeled on N-CSRS). Concerned that large filers will continue to release earnings data quarterly without an accompanying GAAP filing, leaving the data without disclosure context. Recommends that any final rule require a quarterly filing if the issuer also releases earnings, and points to N-PORT as a possible interim model.
rationale_evidence: asks for quarterly form requirement if issuer reports earnings; cites Form N-PORT model

name: Roy Howard
summary: Less transparency will increase fraud and magnify volatility. Quarterly disclosures keep companies honest and are what analysts rely on to understand actual performance. Reminds the SEC of its investor-protection mandate.
rationale_evidence: less transparency leads to fraud; less frequency magnifies volatility; takes investor protection away

name: Skyler Mathis
role: Texas CPA, Financial Reporting Manager at Par Pacific Holdings
summary: Practitioner critique from a public-company financial reporting manager. Worries the proposal will gut dedicated SEC reporting teams and push companies toward contractor-heavy models that lack institutional knowledge, hurting reporting quality. Argues investors rely on consistent cadence and that compensating with detailed interim disclosures would erode the cost-saving rationale anyway.
rationale_evidence: reduction in reporting teams; revolving contractors lack institutional knowledge; would necessitate more detailed interim reports

name: Stephen Bainbridge
role: William D. Warren Distinguished Professor of Law, UCLA School of Law (individual capacity)
summary: Substantive scholarly support letter from William D. Warren Distinguished Professor of Law at UCLA (writing in individual capacity), based on decades of research on corporate governance and the mandatory-disclosure system. Supports the optional 10-S framework. Core arguments: (1) the theoretical foundation for mandatory quarterly reporting is weak — the public-goods, externalities, and information-asymmetry rationales are all contested; (2) market and reputational forces produce substantial voluntary disclosure even without mandates (private placements, foreign issuers); (3) the Adyen case demonstrates that companies whose investors genuinely want quarterly information will provide it voluntarily — investor pressure forced Adyen back from semi-annual to quarterly updates without any mandate; (4) a universal semi-annual mandate would override that market signal and impose investor welfare losses; (5) European semi-annual experience does not show the stigma or adverse-selection signaling effect critics warn about; (6) optionality functions as a useful natural experiment for the Commission to observe.
rationale_evidence: UK natural experiment shows little effect; supports optionality and ICs; engages spillover/externalities and short-termism

rfc_questions.note: Law professor (Bainbridge). Names and answers Q1, Q2, Q14, Q17, Q32 with quoted question text.

name: Aidan
summary: Argues quarterly reporting is a public good for individual and professional investors. Invokes Enron and the 2008 financial crisis as cautionary examples of what longer reporting gaps would enable. Says semiannual reporting will widen the knowledge gap between retail and institutional investors and reduce corporate accountability. Notes longer-period statements become less intelligible.
body: Quarterly reporting creates a public good for individual and professional investors. What would have happened if Enron only had to cook their books semiannually? What would have happened during the financial crisis? Public investors deserve timely updates on public companies. This will widen the knowledge gap between individuals and institutional investors and reduce accountability for public companies. The longer period of time these financial statements cover, the more unintelligible the information will become.
rationale_evidence: what would have happened if Enron only had to cook books semiannually? Widens gap between individuals and institutional investors

name: Anonymous
summary: Frames the issue as retail investor protection. Argues that retail investors lack the visibility and connections of hedge funds and high-net-worth individuals, and that quarterly reports are how they get insight into company performance. Reducing cadence makes performance less transparent.
body: Hi, thanks for the proposal. The market is made of several retail investors and common people who do not have visibility or connections that hedge funds and high net worth individuals have. Quarterly reports provide retail investors insight into the company performance. Reducing the cadence of these reports will make company performance less transparent
rationale_evidence: retail investors don't have visibility hedge funds have; quarterly reports give insight into performance

name: Anonymous
summary: Supports flexibility in reporting cadence but warns that limited-assurance review designed for 10-Q is misaligned with a 6-month reporting period. Recommends pairing the 10-S option with enhanced auditor assurance: filing the auditor review report, expanded review procedures, explicit GAAP-conformity attestation, or disclosure of any scope differences between 10-S and 10-Q reviews. Argues this preserves investor confidence without requiring a full interim audit.
body:
Chair Atkins and Commissioners:
I appreciate the opportunity to comment on the Commission's proposed amendments that would permit registrants to elect semiannual reporting on new Form 10-S in lieu of quarterly reporting on Form 10-Q.

I support the Commission's objective of providing flexibility in interim reporting cadence and acknowledge that mandatory quarterly reporting may not optimally serve all issuers or investors. However, I respectfully recommend that, if adopted, the optional semiannual reporting framework be paired with an enhanced level of independent auditor assurance over the Form 10-S financial statements, beyond the level currently associated with quarterly Form 10-Q reporting.

Moving from three quarterly interim reports to a single semiannual report represents a material reduction in the frequency of standardized, periodic financial disclosure. While the proposal contemplates continued disclosure through Form 8-K and other channels, those mechanisms are episodic and event-driven and do not substitute for comprehensive interim financial statements subject to uniform presentation, comparability, and internal control discipline.

From an investor perspective, less frequent mandatory reporting inherently increases estimation uncertainty, lag in visibility into operating trends, liquidity, and capital structure, and the risk that adverse developments accumulate before being reflected in filed financial statements. A corresponding increase in assurance would help preserve investor confidence while allowing issuers to realize the compliance benefits of semiannual reporting.

Under the existing quarterly reporting regime, Form 10-Q financial statements are subject to a limited-assurance review by the independent auditor rather than a full audit. This framework was designed for shorter measurement periods and more frequent investor updates.

Applying the same limited-assurance review standard to a six-month reporting period creates a different risk profile. A semiannual period necessarily embeds more judgmental estimates, greater cumulative accrual activity, increased exposure to the risk of undetected misstatements, and longer periods between auditor involvement in interim results. As a result, the assurance framework that is acceptable for quarterly reporting may be misaligned with investor expectations when reporting frequency is reduced.

Importantly, I am not suggesting that the Commission require a full audit opinion on Form 10-S financial statements. Instead, the Commission could consider incremental and targeted enhancements that strike an appropriate balance between cost, feasibility, and investor protection.

Potential approaches include, for example, requiring the filing of an auditor's review report with Form 10-S (rather than permitting the review to be performed but not disclosed), improving transparency about the scope and outcome of auditor involvement. Mandating an expanded review standard for semiannual periods (e.g., enhanced procedures around risk assessment, significant estimates, and material changes in internal control). Requiring explicit auditor attestation as to whether they are aware of material modifications that should be made for the financial statements to conform with GAAP for a six-month period. Requiring management to disclose whether the scope of auditor review for Form 10-S differs from the scope historically applied to quarterly Form 10-Qs, enabling investors to assess assurance quality. Any of these approaches would represent a measured evolution of the current framework rather than a wholesale redesign.

The proposal envisions that issuers and investors will "determine for themselves" the most appropriate reporting cadence. For that market choice to function effectively, investors must have confidence in the reliability of the information produced under each option.

Requiring enhanced assurance for Form 10-S would reduce concerns that semiannual reporting is perceived as a weakening of investor protections, support comparability between quarterly and semiannual filers, and encourage issuers to make the election based on business suitability rather than perceived reductions in scrutiny. In this sense, enhanced assurance would strengthen, rather than undermine, the Commission's goal of flexibility.

If the Commission adopts an optional semiannual reporting framework, I respectfully recommend that it also require an increased level of independent auditor assurance for Form 10-S financial statements relative to quarterly Form 10-Q reporting, commensurate with the longer reporting period and reduced filing frequency. This could be achieved through enhanced review requirements, increased transparency around auditor involvement, or other targeted attestation mechanisms that do not impose the cost or complexity of a full interim audit.

Thank you for the opportunity to comment on this important proposal and for the Commission's continued focus on balancing regulatory flexibility with robust investor protection.
rationale_evidence: limited-assurance review misaligned with 6-month period; recommends enhanced auditor assurance for Form 10-S

name: Anonymous
summary: Concrete-example argument: a lot of bad operating news (subscriber drops, traffic declines, fuel-cost shocks) can develop in 6 months that quarterly reporting would surface in 3. Concludes the proposal is not in the public interest.
body: This is not in the public's interest. A lot of BAD can happen in 6 months that is caught faster in 3. For Examples: Subscribers drop 5%? that'd be caught faster in 3 months. Diners decrease 5% due to recession? That would be caught faster in 3 months. Fuel costs cause a loss on earnings? People need to know this in 3 months not 6. A lot of BAD can happen in 6 months that is better caught in 3 months. This is not in the public's interest.
rationale_evidence: a lot of BAD can happen in 6 months caught faster in 3

name: Chris Ridder
role: Investment firm (uses quarterly reports for analysis)
summary: Brief, on behalf of an investment firm. States the firm uses quarterly reports for investment analysis and that no change should be made to 4-times-per-year reporting. Opposes both the cadence change and the new Form 10-S.
body:
May 6, 2026

Our firm uses quarterly reports for investment analysis, and it is critical that no change is made to the financial reporting period of 4 times each year.

We are against any change to Semi-Annual reporting and the use of form 10-S!
rationale_evidence: firm uses quarterly reports for investment analysis; critical that no change is made

name: Conor M.
summary: Two-sentence comment. Argues the rule reduces information available to investors, contrary to financial reporting standards, and that halving disclosure frequency will likely increase fraudulent activity.
body:
This proposed change in reporting requirements, if elected, will reduce information available to investors. This is directly opposed to any financial reporting standards framework.
Halving required reporting will almost certainly lead to an increase in fraudulent activity.
rationale_evidence: reduce information available; halving will lead to increase in fraudulent activity

name: Dakshesh Patel
summary: Says the change is acceptable only if paired with a Taiwan-style monthly operating-revenue disclosure (filed by the 10th of the following month). Argues investors do not need full quarterly statements but must be able to verify whether companies are hitting their semiannual revenue guidance.
body:
Hello,

This rule change is fine but requires an additional change similar to what Taiwan requires of all of it's publicly listed companies.

"Listed companies in Taiwan must file monthly operating revenue reports by the 10th day of each calendar month for the preceding month. These reports must include operating turnover and self-assessed income." This information is posted without any commentary. Strictly facts of the business.

The market doesn't need to know the quarterly statements and cash flows but it needs to know whether the companies are collecting revenues as they projected during their last semi-annual guidance.

This is not an onerous requirement in the 21st century.

Thank you
rationale_evidence: proposes Taiwan-style monthly revenue floor

name: Daniel Gopalan, CPA
summary: Two-sentence CPA comment. Less information means less price discovery, which reduces market efficiency and creates more room for accounting fraud.
body: I am against removing quarterly reporting. The financial markets move on information. Less information means less price discovery. This will reduce the efficiency of the markets and open the door for more accounting fraud to fester in the markets.
rationale_evidence: less information = less price discovery; reduces efficiency; opens door for accounting fraud

name: Daniel O'Donnell
summary: Writes as investor and former strategic planner. Less frequent reporting gives management more room to manage data and cover issues. Quarterly reporting also benefits companies by letting them present positive results or rational explanations on an ongoing basis. Reducing frequency is a bad idea.
body: As an investor and former strategic planning executive, understanding the financial position of any company is paramount to successful investing. Reducing the frequency of reported financial details allows business to manage its data to cover potential issues. In addition, quarterly reporting allows any company to present the market with positive performance data or rational explanations for changing market conditions on an ongoing basis. In my opinion, reducing the frequency of reporting is a bad idea.
rationale_evidence: reducing frequency allows business to manage data to cover potential issues

name: Eugene Stranch
summary: Brief private-investor comment. The current cadence is balanced between investor and company interests. The proposal lessens transparency and tilts the balance toward management.
body: I'm against this rule! The current reporting requirement has worked well and is balanced between the Investor and reporting Company's benefits. This rule proposal will lessen transparency and favor Company's Management interests over Investor's interest.
rationale_evidence: lessens transparency; favors Company Management interests over Investor's interest

name: Isaiah Owolabi
role: Founder and CEO, ESGine; LL.M. (FinTech Law & Regulation); Originator, ESG-as-Code
summary: Supports flexibility but flags that reporting frequency and disclosure obligations are independent: substantive obligations under federal securities laws do not move with the calendar. Asks the Commission to include explicit language in the final rule clarifying that election of 10-S does not reduce, defer, or modify substantive disclosure obligations. Pitches his ESG-as-Code traceability product as compliance infrastructure.
rationale_evidence: step in right direction; flexibility makes sense; clarifies disclosure obligations vs frequency are distinct

name: J R
summary: Long structured comment. Five concerns: timeliness loss, increased volatility and information asymmetry, weakened governance discipline, compressed audit/review cycles, and erosion of the U.S. global benchmark for transparency. Suggests directing burden-reduction efforts at simplification or smaller-issuer relief instead of cadence.
body:
I respectfully oppose the proposal to reduce the frequency of required reporting for public issuers from quarterly to semi-annual. While I appreciate the objective of easing compliance burdens, I believe the proposal would have several unintended consequences that could ultimately undermine the transparency, efficiency, and integrity of U.S. capital markets, which are currently the world standard.

First, reducing the cadence of financial reporting would materially diminish the timeliness of information available to investors. Quarterly reporting plays a critical role in providing up-to-date insight into an issuer's financial performance, liquidity, and emerging risks. Moving to a semi-annual model would lengthen the information gap between disclosures, increasing uncertainty and potentially impairing investors' ability to make informed decisions. In volatile or rapidly evolving industries this loss of timely visibility could be particularly significant.

Second, the proposal may lead to increased market volatility and information asymmetry. In the absence of regular, structured reporting, market participants may rely more heavily on informal disclosures, selective communications, or non-GAAP metrics, which may not be subject to the same level of rigor or internal control. This dynamic could disproportionately benefit well-connected or institutional investors, to the detriment of broader market fairness.

Third, quarterly reporting serves as a key discipline mechanism for issuers, reinforcing the importance of robust internal controls, close processes, and ongoing financial oversight. Reducing reporting frequency could inadvertently weaken these governance practices, leading to less frequent monitoring of financial trends, delayed identification of issues, and increased risk of error or misstatement accumulating over longer reporting periods.

Additionally, from an audit and assurance perspective, interim reporting supports continuous engagement between auditors and management, facilitating earlier identification and resolution of complex accounting issues. A shift to semi-annual reporting could compress review timelines and increase the complexity and risk associated with interim reviews and annual audits.

Finally, the U.S. reporting framework has long been viewed as a global benchmark for transparency and investor protection. A move away from quarterly reporting could represent a step backward relative to this standard and may negatively impact investor confidence in U.S. capital markets.

For these reasons, I believe the current quarterly reporting framework strikes an appropriate balance between transparency and compliance burden and should be retained. Any efforts to reduce regulatory burden would be better directed toward simplifying disclosures, modernizing reporting requirements, or providing targeted relief for smaller issuers rather than reducing the frequency of financial reporting.
rationale_evidence: lengthen information gap; institutional advantage; weakened governance; semiannual a step backward from US global benchmark

name: Jack Pieper
summary: Strongly urges reconsideration. As a shareholder he demands transparency on the financial situation of his portfolio companies. Says he would be more impartial if the proposal mandated biannual audits, but as written it provides no additional verification while reducing public transparency.
rationale_evidence: would be more impartial if biannual audits mandated; no additional verification

name: James Hastings
summary: Small investor strongly opposed. In an era of fast information and AI in corporate accounting, less disclosure is the wrong direction. Would prefer monthly reporting. Notes a company can sink in 6 months and harm investor portfolios.
body:
May 6, 2026

I cannot begin to express how opposed I am as a small investor to the proposal of changing public company reporting from quarterly to semi-annual.

In this day of lightning speed info and adoption of AI in corporate accounting offices why propose less info being available?  If anything I'd like to see MONTHLY information.  A company can sink in 6 months causing catastrophic harm to investor's portfolios.

This cannot stand. Please reconsider.

Thank you for you attention to this matter.

James T Hastings
rationale_evidence: in this day of lightning speed info, why propose less? would like to see monthly

name: Jennifer R
summary: Long opposition letter. Markets exist for the broad public, not the convenience of issuers. Cites information-asymmetry harms, increased insider-trading risk, loss of comparability if filers split between cadences, and the SEC's investor-protection mission. Urges the Commission to keep quarterly 10-Q as the baseline.
body:
To the Securities and Exchange Commission:

I am writing to oppose the Commission's proposed rule permitting public companies to replace quarterly Form 10-Q reporting with semiannual reporting on new Form 10-S.
I understand the stated goal of reducing compliance burdens and giving companies more flexibility. However, the public markets do not exist only for the convenience of reporting companies. They exist because investors, employees, retirees, pension funds, analysts, and the broader public can rely on timely, standardized, and comparable information. Reducing mandatory reporting from quarterly to semiannual would weaken that foundation.

Quarterly reporting is not just an administrative exercise. It is one of the core transparency mechanisms that allows investors to evaluate company performance, monitor risk, compare companies, and detect changes before they become larger problems. Moving to semiannual reporting would create longer periods where material business trends, financial deterioration, operational risks, liquidity concerns, or governance issues may remain less visible to the public.

This proposal would also increase information asymmetry. Large institutional investors, insiders, sophisticated analysts, and market participants with access to alternative data may still find ways to monitor company performance between reports. Ordinary investors will not have the same access. A system that gives public companies less frequent disclosure obligations risks widening the gap between those with privileged or expensive information channels and those relying on public filings.

I am also concerned that reducing reporting frequency could increase the risk of insider trading or perceived unfairness in the market. When companies go six months between required interim reports, insiders and select market participants may have access to meaningful performance information long before the public does. Even if existing rules still require disclosure of certain material events, semiannual reporting would reduce the regular cadence of structured, comprehensive financial disclosure.

The proposal also raises concerns about comparability. If some companies continue quarterly reporting while others move to semiannual reporting, investors may lose a consistent basis for comparing companies across industries. Optional reporting flexibility may sound reasonable, but in practice it could create a fragmented disclosure landscape where companies with weaker performance have an incentive to disclose less frequently.

The Commission's mission includes protecting investors, maintaining fair and efficient markets, and facilitating capital formation. This proposal may reduce costs for some registrants, but those savings should not come at the expense of transparency, investor protection, market confidence, and fair access to information. For these reasons, I urge the Commission not to adopt the proposed semiannual reporting option. Public companies that access U.S. capital markets should continue to provide quarterly Form 10-Q reports as a baseline obligation of public ownership.
rationale_evidence: info asymmetry with sophisticated participants; insider trading risk; loss of comparability; companies with weaker performance have incentive to disclose less; fragmented landscape

name: Jennifer Robespierre
summary: Practitioner letter from a financial advisor. Quarterly earnings are central to client portfolio rebalancing, client communication, and explaining decisions during volatility. Halving the cadence will make advisors' work harder and add to skepticism of the financial-services industry. Asks why the SEC would hold advisors to high standards but not issuers.
body:
I find this proposal by the SEC troubling and feel the need to express why I feel this is a bad idea and a disservice to the finance sector. Quarterly earnings are an essential part of how I'm rebalancing the portfolios of my clients. Not only is this time an opportunity for me to engage with my clients and provide an overview of sector movement, but the transparency provided by earnings confirms the reasoning behind my decisions. While the majority of my clients work within the finance sector and are actively engaged in the markets, many of my clients rely on me to explain market shifts and provide calm when things are volatile.
By reducing earnings report from 4 times a year to 2, you not only make my job and the job of everyone who provides financial advisory services more difficult, you will only add to the cloud of skepticism around the financial services industry. Our industry has a lot of regulation, and rightly so. We are held accountable for the financial future of our clients, so we need to know how companies are performing so that we can make the best decisions and recommendations. How can you continue to hold financial services professionals to a high standard and not those listed on the exchange?
rationale_evidence: quarterly earnings essential for rebalancing client portfolios; engages clients and confirms reasoning

name: Joseph Adair
summary: Individual opposing the change. Argues quarterly reports allow individuals with limited resources to decide whether to buy or sell a stock. Frames a return to semiannual as 'returning to the days before computers' when investors had to track stocks via newspaper and library archives.
body:
Subject: File No. S7-2026-15
From: Joseph Adair

I am AGAINST the proposed change that would allow companies that issue stock to submit financial reports on a semi-annual basis.

The quarterly reports allow individuals, with limited resources, to review the quarterly data to determine if a Company stock should be bought or sold.

This would be like returning to the days before computers when you had to track the stock's price using the newspaper and when I had to go to the library archives section to see how a Company was performing.
rationale_evidence: quarterly reports allow individuals with limited resources to review data

name: Joshua Maddox, CPA
summary: Support letter from a CPA. Argues 10-Q creates undue burden on smaller filers and disincentivizes going public, pushing more businesses into private equity. Proposes a hybrid: keep the 10-K, drop the 10-Q, but FORMALLY require quarterly earnings releases that include at minimum a balance sheet and income statement. Frames it as balancing investor information needs against management burden.
body:
Subject: S7-2026-15: Webform Comments from Joshua Maddox
From: Joshua Maddox
Affiliation: CPA

I am strongly in favor of reducing the frequency of extensive financial reporting for publicly traded companies. Lengthy quarterly filings create an undue burden on smaller publicly traded companies and disincentivize business participation in stock markets, leading to fewer stocks available to the public and more businesses owned by private equity.

To balance the informational needs of investors with the administrative burden placed on management, I propose the following: retaining the 10K in its current form, removing the requirements for 10Q, and formally requiring the quarterly earnings releases investors actually depend upon. These earnings releases would be required to include, at minimum, a balance sheet and income statement. This regulatory update should satisfy investor concerns without the excessive reporting required under the current rules.
rationale_evidence: retain 10-K, remove 10-Q, formally require quarterly earnings releases with balance sheet/income statement

name: Kent Slocum
summary: Brief, sharp comment. Modern corporations need more oversight, not less. Reducing cadence will hurt investors and damage public goodwill. Frames the proposal as evidence the SEC serves business over ordinary Americans.
body: Modern corporations need more oversight and accountability, not less. Reducing annual reporting requirements for companies from quarterly to biannually will only hurt investors and damage what little goodwill remains between the American public and the corporate sector. US regulators are already seen as extremely partisan and highly corrupt; taking this action will only serve to remove all doubt that the SEC serves business interests, not the interests of ordinary, hard-working, blue-collar Americans.
rationale_evidence: more oversight not less; hurts investors and US public-corporate goodwill

name: Lee Michael Tomko
summary: One-line opposition. Twice-a-year is too slow; would prefer monthly. Quarterly is fine.
body: The rule change would be worse for investors because there would be less available information due to delay. I use this information. Quarterly is fine, twice a year is too slow and monthly would be preferred.
rationale_evidence: less available information due to delay; quarterly is fine, twice a year too slow

name: Manish D
summary: Argues quarterly reporting keeps managers focused on efficient resource use and is the foundation of efficient U.S. markets. Contrasts with European markets, which the commenter views as less efficient. Transparency is what makes U.S. capital markets unique.
body: This is not a good idea. Quarterly reporting is what keeps the managers focused on efficient utilization of resources and is the bulwark of efficient markets which Europe' s market are not. This level of transparency is what makes US capital market unique.
rationale_evidence: quarterly reporting is the bulwark of efficient markets which Europe's are not; makes US capital market unique

name: Matthew Truty
summary: Frames Buffett/Dimon support for less-frequent reporting as elite-investor concerns irrelevant to retail investors the SEC must protect. Cites investor protection as the SEC's first mission pillar and uses Enron's $60B in investor losses as a counterweight to flexibility arguments.
body:
I write this as an individual investor and compliance professional that stands strongly against this proposal. Although we see elite investors like Warren Buffett and Jamie Dimon comment how quarterly reporting has "contributed to the decline in the number of public companies in America over the past two decades," of what relevance is this to the individual investor, to which the Commission is responsible for protecting? On the SEC's website, the mission statement is clear that its pillars are protecting investors, maintaining fair, orderly and efficient markets, and facilitating capital formation.

I don't believe it's any coincidence that the protection of investors is the number one priority listed in the mission statement, and here's a great opportunity to assert that.

Lastly, does the expansion of publicly traded companies make up for the possibility of an Enron-like disaster repeating? Is $60B in investor losses a sufficient tradeoff for more lax reporting standards? Why give publicly traded companies any more opportunity to dupe the individual investor?

Let's prioritize what helps American citizens and the retail investor, not the agenda of the elite investor.

Thank you for your time and consideration.
rationale_evidence: Enron-like disaster; SEC mission to protect investors comes first; don't give companies more opportunity to dupe individual investor

name: Mike Sumrow
summary: Strong opposition. Says the burden of quarterly reporting is small in the age of business software and that he will sell out of any company that elects semiannual reporting if the rule passes.
body: I strongly oppose the proposed SEC rule change to reduce public company reporting requirements from quarterly to semiannually. As a private investor, I insist on transparency of companies through quarterly fiscal reporting. The burden of quarterly reporting is small today, in this age of computers and business software. I will check my investments and sell any company that adopts the semiannual reporting rule, should the SEC establish such a rule.
rationale_evidence: burden is small in age of computers and business software; will sell any company adopting semiannual

name: Morten Hansen
role: Software developer (financial transparency infrastructure), Tampere, Finland
summary: Technical opposition. Argues the cost-burden rationale is obsolete because modern accounting is digital and continuous, so the marginal cost of a quarterly filing is small. Notes blockchain-based real-time disclosure infrastructure already exists, so halving cadence moves backward. Cites European reversal of semiannual reporting after evidence of overreaction to peer disclosures, more volatile prices, and less efficient markets. Urges the Commission to modernize toward higher-frequency disclosure instead.
body:
Public Comment on Proposed Rule S7-2026-15 - Semiannual Reporting

To the Securities and Exchange Commission,

I am submitting this comment in opposition to the proposed amendments under File Number S7-2026-15, which would allow public companies to file semiannual reports in lieu of quarterly reports.

I am a software developer building financial transparency infrastructure that tracks institutional investment activity using public SEC filings, on-chain blockchain data, and real-time disclosure feeds. My perspective is technical and practical: the cost savings argument underpinning this proposal is obsolete, and the proposal moves in the wrong direction at precisely the wrong moment.

The compliance cost burden cited in support of this rule assumes a world of manual accounting, physical filings, and costly reconciliation processes. That world no longer exists for the vast majority of public companies. Modern financial systems are digital, automated, and continuously maintained. The marginal cost of packaging already-maintained books into a quarterly filing is minimal. The heavy lifting -auditing, internal controls, financial statement preparation - happens on a rolling basis regardless of reporting frequency. Reducing filings from four to two per year does not meaningfully reduce that burden.

More importantly, this proposal ignores where financial markets are heading. Blockchain-based financial infrastructure - already live and operational on networks including XRP Ledger and Stellar - enables continuous, real-time, permissionless disclosure. Institutional transactions are already being recorded on public ledgers with millisecond timestamps. The trajectory of modern capital markets is toward greater transparency and higher frequency disclosure, not less. Proposing to halve reporting frequency in 2026 is a step backward relative to where the technology already is.

Reducing disclosure frequency does not harm all market participants equally. Institutional insiders, large asset managers, and company executives maintain continuous access to operational data regardless of what is filed publicly. Retail investors and independent analysts depend entirely on public filings. Extending the blind spot from 90 days to 180 days widens an already significant information asymmetry - and does so in a way that primarily benefits those who already have the least need for public disclosures.

Academic research supports this concern. Studies of European markets, which moved to semiannual reporting before partially reversing course, found that reduced reporting frequency created information vacuums that caused investors to overreact to peer-firm disclosures and produced more volatile, less efficient stock prices. The U.S. should not repeat that experiment.

I urge the Commission to reject this proposal and instead direct its attention toward modernizing disclosure infrastructure - leveraging existing and emerging technology to make reporting more frequent, more accessible, and more useful to all market participants, not less.

Respectfully submitted,
[Morten Hansen]
[Tampere, Finland]
rationale_evidence: compliance cost burden obsolete with digital systems; studies of European markets found volatility, overreaction, less efficient prices

name: Neil P. Osnato
summary: Frames the issue as a market-function question: when the verified-reporting interval doubles, market reliance shifts to interim guidance, alternative data, and sell-side models. Particularly concerning in capital-intensive sectors (utilities, energy infrastructure, AI infrastructure, data centers, advanced manufacturing, transportation). Asks the Commission to consider sector-sensitive guidance and additional safeguards if it proceeds.
body:
To the Securities and Exchange Commission:

I am submitting this comment regarding Release No. 2026-42, "SEC Proposes Amendments to Permit Optional Semiannual Reporting by Public Companies."

The proposal raises an important question beyond the commonly discussed tradeoff between regulatory flexibility and disclosure frequency. It also raises a market-function question: what happens when the interval between verified financial reporting "reality checks" expands from three months to six months?

Quarterly reporting does not eliminate uncertainty, nor does it prevent markets from relying on estimates, guidance, sell-side models, or alternative data. But it does compress the period during which management assumptions, consensus expectations, and market narratives can drift materially from underlying operating conditions before being reset by standardized financial disclosure.

If public companies are permitted to elect semiannual reporting, investors may place greater reliance on interim guidance, unaudited narratives, alternative data, and model-driven estimates between filings. This may be especially important in capital-intensive sectors where operating risk can form before it appears cleanly in financial statements, including utilities, energy infrastructure, AI infrastructure, data centers, advanced manufacturing, transportation, and other long-lived asset sectors.

In these sectors, material developments often emerge through changes in operating assumptions: demand durability, utilization, construction timelines, project cost escalation, customer commitments, supply-chain constraints, rate recovery expectations, and capital-allocation decisions. Less frequent standardized reporting could increase the duration over which such assumptions remain unverified by formal financial disclosure.

For that reason, I respectfully encourage the Commission to evaluate not only whether optional semiannual reporting reduces compliance burden, but also whether longer reporting intervals may increase market reliance on non-standardized operating signals between filings.

The relevant issue is not simply whether quarterly or semiannual reporting is preferable. It is whether investors retain sufficient decision-grade information to distinguish durable operating performance from assumption-driven performance during longer intervals between required reports.

If the Commission proceeds with an optional semiannual reporting framework, it should consider whether additional safeguards, disclosure expectations, or sector-sensitive guidance may be appropriate where long-lived capital commitments, major infrastructure programs, large customer concentration, or material demand assumptions are central to investor valuation.

Respectfully submitted,
Neil P. Osnato
rationale_evidence: increase market reliance on non-standardized operating signals; sector-sensitive guidance for capital-intensive sectors

name: Omer Yousif, PhD
summary: Long structured opposition with five points: (1) quarterly reporting is the market's 'heartbeat' and semiannual would create informational dead zones, raising volatility; (2) larger gaps advantage insiders/institutions and raise insider-trading risk; (3) the regulatory-burden argument is technologically obsolete in 2026 (generative AI, XBRL automation, real-time ERP); (4) an 'optional' framework bifurcates the market and creates a transparency discount that raises cost of capital for semiannual filers; (5) no empirical evidence that lower cadence improves long-termism — that's driven by corporate culture and exec comp.
body: [PDF letter — content not extracted via fetch.]
rationale_evidence: informational dead zones; insider risk; obsolete compliance burden with AI/XBRL; market fragmentation/comparability; no evidence frequency cures short-termism; US trusted markets

name: Oscar L. Perez
summary: Retail-investor opposition with a 'tone at the top' argument. Concedes there is some short-term incentive baked into quarterly cycles but frames that as a management problem, not an investor problem. If firms dislike quarterly reporting, that itself reveals their own short-term focus and bias. Investor short-termism follows from management short-termism, not from the reporting calendar.
body:
Subject: File No. S7-2026-15
From: Oscar L. Perez

Under no circumstances should this move forward. As a retail investor, it is already difficult to access or contact corporate investor relations people at firms for various reasons.

Although it is true that there is some short-term minded incentive to focus on earnings beats every quarter, which hinders the long term, this is a management issue, not an investor issue. If firms do not like the quarterly reporting system, it shows their own short-term focus and bias.

Management sets the tone at the top. If they focus on the quarters and not the years, investors will focus on the quarters, not the years. This is a self-fulfilling prophecy. A firm culture issue, not a systematic issue.

Sincerely,
Oscar L. Perez
rationale_evidence: as retail investor difficult to access IR; short-termism is management's culture issue, not investor issue

name: Patricia Joseph
role: Former Fortune 500 senior management (ops and finance)
summary: Practitioner rebuttal to the management-burden argument. From senior ops and finance experience, quarterly reporting actually enhances performance: it forces information up and down the chain and acts as an early-warning system. Six months is too long; a lot of bad things can happen.
body: That quarterly reporting takes time away from managing a business is NOT TRUE. In my experience in senior management roles in ops and finance at Fortune 500 companies, quarterly reporting enhances performance. The process of preparing for quarterly reports communicates what's really going on up and down the chain of command and of promotes accountability. Like early warning system if there are problems. Twice a year? A lot of bad things can happen in 6 months.
rationale_evidence: quarterly reporting enhances performance; communicates up/down chain; early warning system

name: Steven A. Collazo
role: BBA Student at UPR Cayey; Individual Investor
summary: Student-investor opposition. Frames the proposal as deregulation that would push companies toward the path of least resistance. Cites recent April market gains tied to Q1 reports as evidence quarterly disclosure stabilizes prices. Suggests incentivizing more frequent reporting (monthly Progressive-style) and notes quarterly reports have helped him avoid imminent bankruptcies.
body:
As far as reporting goes, it seems like this piece of legislation is made to deregulate the stock market further and loosen rules that have already worked well in favor of investors and maintain the trust that helps the stock market outpace the real economy.

Most recently, the gains achieved on April occured thanks to Q1's reports being mostly positive after what seemed to be an uncertain time in March. If companies were given the option to perform semi-annual reporting, they'll go through the path of least resistance and leave investors in the dark as to their daily operations. A better option would be to provide enough incentive to perform quarterly, bimonthly or monthly reporting that the only companies that choose semi-annual reporting are the ones where they see redundancy compared to the benefits provided to them and the shareholders to performing quarterly reports.

Unless the world miraculously stabilized overnight, quarterly reporting keeps all investors up to date with the constant goings of the planet, which helps maintain premiums in stock market prices as any uncertainty is quelled by financial reporting.

Beyond the financial gains, I and other investors have found the quarterly reports quite useful and properly up-to-date. Usually, anything that is more than a few months old will lose its relevance and it will happen much sooner if something major happens like a war, or tariffs, or the many stints of the current administration that have affected business on more than just a quarterly basis. Institutionals and individual investors alike will not only benefit from quarterly reports, but from more frequent reporting the likes Progressive (PGR) have achieved with their monthly updates. There have also been times when I've been able to make use of quarterly reports to avoid entire bankruptcies that would have happened months later due to how quickly some financials have deteriorated. Semi-annual reports put investors at greater risk of financial loss and may erode the trust and goodwill of the US stock market at a global level, as fraud can be more easily achieved with more lax reporting requirements.
rationale_evidence: deregulation; leaves investors in dark; erode trust at global level; fraud more easily achieved

name: Tan Banh
summary: Retail-fairness argument. Hedge funds already use alternative data (satellite parking-lot tracking, shipping manifests, credit-card data) to monitor companies in real time; the 10-Q is what levels the field. A 180-day gap means retail investors see news already 6 months old and traded on. Also argues the longer dark period is a breeding ground for insider trading.
body:
To the Securities and Exchange Commission,
I am writing to express my strong opposition to the proposal to move from quarterly (Form 10-Q) to semiannual (Form 10-S) reporting. While the stated goal is to reduce "short-termism," the actual result will be a dangerous reduction in market transparency that disproportionately harms retail investors. The current quarterly structure is the "gold standard" for a reason. Extending the reporting gap to six months creates a massive "dark period" that gives an unfair advantage to institutional firms and insiders. Large hedge funds and advanced firms already use alternative data such as satellite tracking of retail parking lots, private shipping manifests, and high-frequency credit card data to predict company performance in real time. Retail investors do not have access to these expensive tools; we rely on the 10-Q to level the playing field. By removing the quarterly requirement, the SEC is essentially ensuring that by the time a retail investor sees a semiannual report, the "news" is already six months old and has been fully traded upon by those with better technology. Furthermore, a 180 day gap between financial disclosures creates a breeding ground for insider trading. When material changes in a company's health are not disclosed for half a year, the temptation and opportunity for those with "inside" knowledge to front run the public grow exponentially. I urge the Commission to maintain the current quarterly reporting frequency to ensure the markets remain fair, transparent, and accessible to everyone, not just those with the most advanced tracking technology.

Sincerely,
Tan Banh
rationale_evidence: hedge funds use alt-data (satellite, credit card); retail relies on 10-Q; 180-day gap breeds insider trading

name: Thomas M. Stoops
summary: Two-sentence opposition. Less reporting equals less transparency and more risk shifted to investors who are already taking on the risk inherent to holding equity.
body: Strongly oppose this proposed rulemaking to reduce the frequeny of requirred reporting publicly traded companies. Less reporting means less transparency for the investment community while increasing risk at the same time. A corporation is meant to take on risky business enterpriseand without transparency, the investors will be asked to potentially accept unacceptable risk.
rationale_evidence: less reporting = less transparency for investment community while increasing risk

name: Udayan J. Parikh
summary: Retail investor opposing semiannual but acknowledging the management-cost concern. Proposes a quadrimester (every 4 months) compromise: investors keep enough transparency, management saves cost relative to quarterly. Argues semiannual is unproven territory and risky.
body: As a retail investor, I rely on the quarterly earnings report that are given to me on media by the executives of publicly traded companies. It gives me how the company is performing and what the future holds to my investments. By going to have them go semiannually, it poses risk to investment, as the history shows many companies have gone from top to delisting from stock market in matter of few days. I completely agree that quarterly reporting standards might be overkilled to management of the company and costly; at the same time it is crucial for investors for transperancy. Perhaps better solution is to have Quadrimester (every 4 months) rather than every 3 months. Going semianually has a huge risk as it is unchartered territory and do not know what can go wrong for investors. By going every 4 months, investor still get enough transparency and management can save cost and focus more on running a company that worry about what to report to investor, as it does take quite some management time on preparing for report every 3 months.
rationale_evidence: proposes quadrimester (every 4 months); semiannual unchartered territory

name: Adam
role: Technical Program Manager, AI & Clinical Technology MBA
summary: Long structured opposition. Six points: SEC mandate is investor protection and the proposal moves the wrong way; less frequency causes overreaction and more volatility (cites academic research); 2008 lessons argue for more disclosure not less; 'optional' becomes the floor as larger firms adopt and smaller firms follow; cost-burden argument shifts risk from issuers to investors; quarterly reporting shortens the window for fraud to go undetected.
body:
I am submitting this comment in strong opposition to the proposed amendments that would allow public companies to file semiannual reports in lieu of quarterly reports on Form 10-Q.

The SEC's mandate is investor protection. This proposal moves in the opposite direction.
Reduced transparency increases investor risk. Quarterly reporting gives investors, analysts, and the market timely signals to price risk accurately.

Moving to semiannual reporting creates a six-month information vacuum. Academic research has documented that reduced reporting frequency leads investors to overreact to peer-company earnings, overcorrect when reports do arrive, and experience greater price volatility — not less. Less transparency does not produce more efficient markets. It produces less efficient ones.

The 2008 financial crisis is not ancient history. The systemic failures that nearly collapsed the global economy were enabled by opacity, inadequate disclosure, and insufficient oversight of corporate financial conditions. The regulatory response to that crisis was built on the premise that investors need more information, more frequently — not less. We have not resolved the structural vulnerabilities that made that crisis possible. Loosening disclosure requirements now is not reform. It is regression.

"Optional" frameworks erode in practice. Framing this as voluntary flexibility understates the real-world dynamics. When large-cap companies adopt semiannual reporting, smaller firms face pressure to follow to reduce compliance costs. What begins as an option becomes the floor. Investor protections are weakened for the entire market, not just the companies that elect the new cadence.

Compliance cost arguments do not justify the tradeoff. The burden-reduction case rests on the costs to companies. The SEC's obligation runs to investors, not issuers. Shifting that cost onto investors — in the form of information asymmetry, increased risk, and reduced ability to make informed decisions — is not a net regulatory benefit. It is a transfer of risk from corporations to the public.

The SEC should be strengthening oversight, not contracting it. Bad actors exist in public markets. They always have. Quarterly reporting does not prevent fraud, but it shortens the window in which fraud can go undetected. Extending that window by six months is a gift to those who exploit information gaps.

I urge the Commission to reject this proposal and reaffirm its commitment to the transparency standards that have distinguished U.S. capital markets for over five decades.
rationale_evidence: Academic research peer overreaction; optional erodes in practice ('option becomes the floor'); compliance cost shifts risk to investors; extends fraud window

name: Andrew Neumeier
summary: Personal-experience opposition. He and his wife sold a stock about a year ago because of information in a single quarterly report, getting ahead of trouble that materialized later. Argues retail investors need more information, not less, and asks the Commission to protect them by keeping the quarterly requirement.
body:
Good Day,

I will be very brief. I have read about the proposed rule change regarding the quarterly reporting requirement by the SEC that would allow companies to file them less often, semi-annually.

My wife and I are individual investors, investing for our future, our retirement and that of our extended family. We rely on Quarterly Reports from the companies whose stock we own. It creates more transparency, and allows us to make investment decisions as we need to. We need more information, not less of it. The SEC proposal gives me less information, exactly what I do not want, the opposite of what we need to monitor our investments.

About a year ago, we sold all stock in one of our companies. We did this because of information they provided in a Quarterly Report. We were months ahead of the curve, we sold it before the trouble started that we feared from the information provided in that single report. Had that company not been required to file those reports we would have been late in our sale of that company. Our investment would have been hurt.

We ask you to leave things as they are. We request that Quarterly Reports as a SEC requirement be continued, for our own good and that of other investors. More information is better, much better. More transparency is better. We think the job of the SEC is to protect investors, you can help to protect us by requiring companies provide Quarterly Reports.

Thank you.
rationale_evidence: rely on quarterly reports for retirement decisions; sold stock based on quarterly info months before trouble

name: Andy Black
summary: Investor opposition. Quarterly reporting promotes transparency, market efficiency, and accountability. Reducing it would increase information asymmetry, hurt retail investors, and erode market confidence. Acknowledges short-termism concerns but argues they should be addressed via better disclosure practices, not less of it.
body:
I respectfully oppose any efforts to loosen or eliminate quarterly reporting requirements for public companies. As an investor, I rely on timely, standardized disclosures to make informed decisions, assess risk, and monitor company performance. Quarterly reporting promotes transparency, market efficiency, and accountability by ensuring that material information is disclosed on a consistent and predictable basis.
Reducing the frequency or rigor of these reports would likely increase information asymmetry, disadvantage retail investors, and diminish confidence in public markets. While concerns about short-termism are valid, they can be addressed through improved disclosure practices and guidance rather than by reducing transparency.
Maintaining robust quarterly reporting standards is essential to preserving investor trust and the integrity of U.S. capital markets.
rationale_evidence: rely on timely standardized disclosures; reducing frequency would increase information asymmetry and disadvantage retail

name: Anonymous
summary: Concrete-example list (subscriber loss, fewer diners, fuel-cost-driven earnings hits) showing material information surfaces sooner under quarterly cadence. Concludes the public interest is served by 3-month reporting.
body:
This is not in the public's interest

A lot of BAD can happen in 6 months which can be caught better in 3.
Subscriber loss? find out better in 3
Fewer Diners? find out better in 3
Fuel costs going to cause a loss next Q? find out better in 3
so on

A lot of BAD can happen over 6 months and it is better to find out in 3. It is in the public's interest to know material information in 3 months over six.
rationale_evidence: a lot of BAD can happen in 6 months better caught in 3

name: Anonymous, Individual Investor
summary: Concedes quarterly reporting is burdensome but argues it is necessary for shareholders to track performance. Asks for at least unaudited quarterly statements. Notes competition is intense and businesses are 'hardly stagnant over 6 months.' Adds a call for the SEC to spend more time prosecuting market manipulation and obvious insider trades.
body:
Subject: File No. S7-2026-15
From: Anonymous

the quarterly reporting frequency, while burdensome, is necessary for shareholders to understand how the business they have invested is doing. We need at least unaudited quarterly statements. Competition is intense in this world and businesses are hardly stagnant over 6 months.

I wish the SEC would spend more time prosecuting market manipulation, insider trades which are very obvious these days.
rationale_evidence: competition is intense, businesses are hardly stagnant over 6 months; spend more time prosecuting insider trades

name: Bill Korn
role: Board Member and Audit Chair, Jerash Holdings (US) Inc.; Board Member and former CFO of CareCloud, Inc.
summary: Notable issuer-side voice — public-company audit chair and former CFO. Acknowledges the appeal of semiannual for many issuers but says investors are better served, and boards do a more effective job, with quarterly. If the rule passes, proposes relaxing the quarterly filing deadline (60 days post-quarter rather than 40-45 days) to encourage smaller filers to stay quarterly rather than elect 10-S.
body:
Subject: File No. S7-2026-15
From: Bill Korn

While I see the appeal for many issuers of only reporting twice a year, investors are better served with quarterly reporting, and the Board can do a more effective job of assessing performance with quarterly reports.

If we allow issuers the option of choosing semi-annual reporting, it would be helpful to relax the timeframe for reporting if they choose to stay with quarterly. I would rather see a quarterly report 60 days after quarter-end then wait for a semiannual report 40 or 45 days after the end of the half. Especially for smaller reporting companies, a little extra time might encourage them to stay with quarterly reports.
rationale_evidence: if option allowed, relax timeframe for quarterly filers (60 days) to encourage them to stay quarterly

name: CH Young
summary: Cites Singapore as a cautionary tale: after implementing semiannual reporting years ago, disclosure quality and transparency suffered, trading volume dwindled, and valuations fell — Singapore is now trying to encourage quarterly updates again. Frames easier reporting as 'a loser mentality' and rejects the framing that companies can't cope. Also flags information-flow asymmetry favoring institutions with direct executive access.
body:
Subject: S7-2026-15: Webform Comments from CH Young
From: CH Young

This is a very bad idea. Singapore implemented semiannual reporting years ago. The result is company's disclosure became worse, transparency suffered, trading volume dwindled and valuations suffered. Now it is trying to encourage quarterly updates. Making it easy for companies is finding a wrong shortcut and a loser mentality. Companies are well aware of all obligations as public companies. They shouldn't list if they find it onerous. Don't come into the game if they can't cope. You don't get good players by making things easier. Also, it will skew the information dissemination to institutional investors who have direct access to key executives of companies, which is to the disadvantage of retail investors.
rationale_evidence: Singapore implemented semiannual; disclosure became worse, transparency suffered, trading dwindled; now trying to encourage quarterly updates

name: Chris Curnan
summary: Brief opposition. Argues the rule will harm small investors and keep them in the dark for extended periods, and that companies electing 10-S will not be held accountable. Frames it as harming trust in U.S. public companies.
body:
Subject: S7-2026-15: Webform Comments from Chris Curnan
From: Chris Curnan

This rule will harm small investors and keep investors in the dark about the company finances for an extended period of time. Companies that choose to do this will not be held accountable to extended periods of time. This is a terrible idea and harms the trust of US public companies.
rationale_evidence: harms small investors; harms trust of US public companies

name: Daniel Plowman
summary: Personal-attention support. Argues twice-a-year reporting frees leaders to do what is best for customers rather than only shareholders, and notes he personally only pays attention to Q2/Q4 earnings anyway (the spring/winter spending seasons). Acknowledges the fraud-opportunity counterargument but supports the change overall.
body:
Subject: File No. S7-2026-15
From: Daniel Plowman

I feel like semiannual reporting is a win for regular people. When companies report twice a year, they are not running each quarter with a focus only on earnings. With fewer mandatory reporting periods, leaders can manage their businesses and do what is best for the people they serve rather than serving only shareholders. I invest and really only pay attention to Q2 and Q4 earnings anyway. Those two quarters are centered on the ideal spending seasons, spring and winter. Preparing for summer and Christmas (holiday) shopping.

However, I can see this going both ways, opening up less oversight and, essentially, creating opportunities for fraud. That being said, I still fully support semiannual reporting and feel it will help people who rely on businesses for services or goods rather than having those businesses operate in a manner that does not treat each customer fairly.
rationale_evidence: twice a year means not running each quarter focused on earnings; only pay attention to Q2/Q4 anyway

name: David Bach
summary: Two-point opposition from a financial-literacy consultant. (1) Less transparency increases uncertainty, leading to less trading and greater volatility, which lowers stock prices and creates a downward cycle. (2) Companies should not bite the hand that feeds them — upset investors may not support future financing needs.
body:
Subject: File No. S7-2026-15
From: David Bach

Sir or Madam: Two reasons to avoid semi-annual reporting. (1) Less transparency leads to more uncertainly, which leads to less trading and greater volatility. This increased risk will lower stock prices. That upsets investors, leading to further less trading. The downward cycle continues. (2) Companies should not bite the hand that feeds them. Upset investors may not support the companies' future funding needs.
rationale_evidence: less transparency leads to less trading and greater volatility; lower stock prices

name: Erik Swanson
summary: Long structured opposition. Argues semiannual cadence would create extended periods where material developments may go unreported, increasing information asymmetry between insiders and the public, undermining market efficiency, and elevating mispricing/volatility risk. Concludes that any marginal compliance benefit is outweighed by costs to market integrity, and that the existing quarterly framework strikes the right balance.
body:
Subject: File No. S7-2026-15
From: Erik Swanson

I am writing to express my strong opposition to the Commission's proposal to permit certain issuers to report on a semiannual basis rather than quarterly. Reducing the frequency of required disclosures would materially weaken the transparency framework that underpins U.S. capital markets.

Quarterly reporting has long served as a foundational mechanism for ensuring that investors receive timely, decision-useful information about an issuer's financial condition, risk exposures, and operational performance. Moving to a semiannual cadence would create extended periods in which material developments may go unreported, increasing information asymmetry between insiders and the investing public. This would undermine market efficiency and elevate the risk of mispricing, volatility, and unequal access to critical data.

The Commission has repeatedly emphasized that transparency is essential to investor protection and to maintaining confidence in U.S. markets. Allowing issuers to report only twice per year runs counter to that principle. In an environment where business conditions, macroeconomic factors, and risk profiles can shift rapidly, less frequent reporting would leave investors with outdated information for longer periods and impair their ability to make informed decisions.

While the proposal suggests that reduced reporting may lessen compliance burdens, any marginal administrative benefit is outweighed by the significant costs to market integrity. The existing quarterly framework strikes an appropriate balance between issuer obligations and investor needs, and it should be preserved.

For these reasons, I respectfully urge the Commission not to adopt the proposed rule permitting semiannual reporting. Maintaining robust, frequent disclosure requirements is essential to protecting investors and sustaining the transparency that has long distinguished U.S. capital markets.

Thank you for considering this comment.
rationale_evidence: extended periods material may go unreported; information asymmetry between insiders and public; transparency distinguished US capital markets

name: Henggao Cai
role: Individual investor and member of the public
summary: Long structured opposition with five points. Frames the proposal as inverting SEC priorities (corporate convenience over investor access). Cites academic research on UK/EU semiannual experiments showing increased volatility, investor overreaction in absence of own-firm disclosures, and overcorrection when reports finally arrive. Argues retail investors bear the cost (institutions have alternative channels). Says Form 8-K is not an adequate substitute (management-discretionary, non-standardized). And argues 'optional' framing obscures aggregate harm — companies most likely to elect 10-S are smaller, less-covered firms where retail exposure is highest. Suggests targeted alternatives like streamlining 10-Q content or extending non-accelerated-filer deadlines.
body:
Subject: S7-2026-15: Webform Comments from Henggao Cai
From: Henggao Cai

I am submitting this comment as an individual investor and member of the public. I write in strong opposition to this proposal. While framed as a modernization of an outdated framework, this rule would, in practice, reduce the flow of standardized financial information that retail investors depend on to make decisions and it would do so for the primary benefit of corporate management teams, not the investors the Commission is charged with protecting.

The SEC's core mandate under the Securities Exchange Act is to protect investors, maintain fair and orderly markets, and facilitate capital formation. This proposal inverts that priority. It privileges corporate convenience over investor access, and it does so without credible evidence that quarterly reporting is actually causing the harms its critics claim.

The short-term argument does not hold up. The central justification for this rule is that quarterly reporting forces managers into short-term decision-making. This argument has been repeated for years by executives and lobbyists, but the empirical record does not support it. Academic research examining companies that shifted to semiannual reporting in the UK and EU found no meaningful improvement in long-term investment behavior. What it did find was increased stock price volatility, investor overreaction in the absence of own-firm disclosures, and overcorrection when semiannual reports finally arrived. The Commission is proposing to restructure a 50-year disclosure regime based on a premise that peer-reviewed research has already substantially undermined.

Retail investors will bear the cost of this rule. Institutional investors, hedge funds, and company insiders do not depend on quarterly 10-Q filings the way retail investors do. They have access to earnings calls, analyst relationships, roadshows, and proprietary data services. Retail investors, the people this agency exists to protect, rely on standardized, mandatory disclosures as their primary window into how a company is actually performing. Doubling the gap between those disclosures from three months to six creates a structural information advantage for sophisticated participants and leaves ordinary investors operating on assumptions for longer periods. This is not a theoretical concern. It is a predictable and documented outcome.

The Form 8-K backstop is not an adequate substitute. The proposal appears to rely on current-event 8-K filings to bridge the information gap between semiannual reports. But 8-K filings are management-discretionary, non-standardized, and cannot substitute for the systematic financial data, such as revenue, earnings, cash flow, balance sheet changes that Form 10-Q requires. A company that elects semiannual reporting could go six months without giving investors a standardized, auditable view of its financial condition, entirely within the rules as proposed.

The "optional" framing obscures the aggregate harm. Because this is presented as a voluntary election, proponents argue that market forces will discipline companies that reduce disclosure. That argument fails for several reasons. First, retail investors often cannot easily identify which companies have switched to semiannual reporting or understand the implications when they do. Second, the companies most likely to elect semiannual reporting are smaller, less-covered firms where information is already scarce, which is precisely where retail investors are most exposed. Third, once semiannual reporting becomes normalized, investor expectations will adjust downward and the transparency premium will erode.

I urge the Commission to withdraw this proposal. If the Commission is genuinely concerned about compliance costs for smaller issuers, there are targeted ways to address that and streamlining the content requirements of Form 10-Q, extending filing deadlines for non-accelerated filers, or providing technical assistance without eliminating the quarterly disclosure cadence that has underpinned market confidence for over half a century.

Flexibility is not a regulatory virtue when the costs fall on those with the least access to alternative information. I ask the Commission to reject this rule and reaffirm its commitment to investor protection as its primary obligation.

Thank you for considering this comment.
Henggao Cai
rationale_evidence: UK/EU studies show no improvement in long-termism but increased volatility and overreaction; smaller less-covered firms most likely to elect = retail most exposed; transparency premium will erode

name: Jeremiah Schuur
summary: Opposition framed around U.S. capital-market dominance and retirement savings. Argues frequent disclosure reduces information asymmetry, improves price discovery, lowers cost of capital, and underpins global investor confidence in U.S. markets. Reducing cadence would disproportionately benefit sophisticated participants and disadvantage individuals saving via 401(k)s, pensions, and mutual funds.
body:
Subject: File No. S7-2026-15
From: Jeremiah Schuur

I strongly oppose the proposed SEC rule allowing semiannual rather than quarterly reporting by public companies. The strength and global dominance of U.S. capital markets rests in large part on the availability of timely, reliable, standardized public information. Frequent disclosure reduces information asymmetry, improves price discovery, lowers the cost of capital, and promotes confidence in the fairness and transparency of our markets.

Quarterly reporting is particularly important for individual investors, who do not have access to the private channels, institutional research, or insider relationships available to large financial firms. Reducing the frequency of mandatory reporting would disproportionately benefit sophisticated market participants while increasing informational disadvantage and uncertainty for ordinary Americans whose retirement savings are invested through 401(k)s, pensions, and mutual funds.

At a time when confidence in markets and institutions is critically important, the SEC should strengthen - not weaken - the flow of accurate public information.
