The transparency and accountability created by regular reporting requirements are a major reason global investors continue to trust and invest in U.S. markets. Moving to semiannual reporting would represent a step backward for market integrity, investor protection, and long-term public confidence.
rationale_evidence: global dominance rests on timely standardized info; quarterly important for individual investors without private channels; cost of capital

name: Joe Beverly
role: Former Business Owner; current investor in publicly owned securities
summary: Operator-perspective opposition. Argues any company should already be preparing and reviewing monthly statements internally; with modern accounting systems, printing quarterly statements is not difficult. Acknowledges there's more work than just printing them but says investors should not have to wait six months for an update from a public company.
body:
Subject: File No. S7-2026-15
From: Joe Beverly

I would be opposed to such a change.

Any company, whether public or private, should be preparing and reviewing monthly financial statements. If monthly statements are being prepared, with modern accounting systems, it is not difficult to print quarterly statements.

I admit there is more work to be done than just printing the statements, but I believe investors in public companies should not have to wait for six months to get an update from a public company.
rationale_evidence: any company should prepare monthly financials; with modern systems, easy to print quarterly; shouldn't wait 6 months

name: John Boyar, CPA
summary: Brief CPA letter. Frames the change as reducing transparency between companies and investors and says he does not support it given his financial-markets experience.
body:
Subject: File No. S7-2026-15
From: John Boyar

I do not support this move. As someone who has experience in the financial markets, we should not be making moves to reduce transparency between companies and their investors.
rationale_evidence: should not reduce transparency between companies and investors

name: John Cheppo
summary: Retired industry veteran. Calls the proposal 'an awful idea' that would not change long-term behavior, would increase non-transparency, and would harm individual investors' ability to identify, assess, and mitigate risks. Quarterly reporting is 'a bedrock of making companies try to do the right thing.'
body:
Subject: S7-2026-15: Webform Comments from John Cheppo
From: John Cheppo

Dear SEC,
This is an awful idea. I have worked in companies for decades and have now retired. This would not change any long term behaviors, and simply just increase non-transparency and further harm individual investors ability to identify, assess and mitigate risks. Quarterly reporting is a bedrock of making companies try to do the right thing, it's not a problem. I cannot even believe this is up for consideration. Do not permit this please.
rationale_evidence: would not change long-term behaviors; just increase non-transparency; harm individual investors' ability to identify risks

name: John Flynn Jr.
summary: Long structured opposition. Five points: increased information asymmetry between insiders and the public; delayed visibility of deteriorating fundamentals; reduced management accountability between cycles; increased volatility from concentrated info dumps; and weakened U.S. market leadership built on rigorous disclosure. Argues the short-termism case is overstated and companies already have tools to guide long-term expectations without reducing transparency.
body:
Subject: File No. S7-2026-15
From: John Flynn Jr.

I am writing to express my strong opposition to the proposal that would allow U.S. public companies to report earnings on a semiannual basis instead of quarterly.

Quarterly reporting is one of the few mechanisms that ensures timely transparency, market discipline, and early detection of financial stress. Reducing reporting frequency would:

Increase information asymmetry between insiders and the investing public
Delay the visibility of deteriorating fundamentals, allowing problems to compound before investors can react
Reduce accountability for management teams who already face limited scrutiny between earnings cycles
Increase volatility, as markets would be forced to digest twice as much information at once
Weaken U.S. market leadership, which has historically been built on rigorous disclosure standards

The argument that quarterly reporting creates "short-termism" is overstated. Companies already have tools to guide long-term expectations without reducing transparency. Investors, analysts, and regulators rely on quarterly data to monitor risk, capital allocation, and operational performance. Semiannual reporting would remove a critical safeguard.

For these reasons, I urge the Commission to withdraw or substantially reconsider this proposal. The costs to market integrity and investor protection far outweigh any claimed benefits.

Thank you for considering this comment.
John Flynn Jr.
rationale_evidence: information asymmetry; delay visibility; volatility from twice-yearly digestion; weaken US market leadership; short-termism argument overstated

name: Jose G. Martinez
summary: Private investor strongly preferring quarterly cadence. Argues quarterly reports provide timely transparency, support informed decisions, and maintain accountability; semiannual would limit access to current information and increase uncertainty for individuals.
body:
Subject: File No. S7-2026-15
From: Jose G. Martinez

As a private investor, I strongly prefer that public companies continue reporting earnings quarterly rather than semiannually. Quarterly reports provide timely transparency, help investors make informed decisions, and maintain accountability in the markets. Reducing the frequency of reporting would limit access to current financial information and could increase uncertainty for individual investors who rely on regular updates to evaluate company performance.
rationale_evidence: quarterly reports provide timely transparency and accountability

name: Ken Stack
summary: Asks why the proposal is all-or-none rather than scaled. Suggests alternatives: scaled-back compliance for Q2/Q3 with comprehensive reporting at start and end of year; or qualifying-criteria framework where only companies that 'earn it' may opt in (with investor trust as the gating signal). Argues existing investors should have a vote before their holdings' reporting cadence drops. Pushes back on the proposal's nod to social media as a primary information source.
body:
Subject: File No. S7-2026-15
From: Ken Stack

Why is this all or none and a scaled back compliance burden is not being considered for companies in say q2 and q3 with start and end year more comprehensive reporting? Or another alternative; a reduced burden where it makes sense across the board with the existing cadence intact.

As the proposal itself noted there are significant differences between public companies. If changing the reporting structure why not have qualification criteria to be met and maintained enabling only qualifying companies earning it along with investor trust the option to reduce their reporting burden? This carrot may incentivize better accounting and reporting behaviors as accounting deviations from accepted standards and outright fraud are not unheard of.

Reducing the compliance burden to encourage companies to go public is attractive but to not do this thoughtfully creates more uncertainty and unknown risk for investors with less public information. It is the latter goal; investor well being that should remain the primary focus here. Existing investors need a vote on their holdings before reporting levels at their investment decrease at a bare minimum. To follow one's investment through social media as a primary source of information as mentioned in the proposal is incredibly discouraging.
rationale_evidence: asks for scaled compliance burden alternative (less Q2/Q3 detail) or qualification criteria to earn relief

name: Lora Jones
summary: Two-sentence support letter from a public-company CFO. Argues the rule gives companies the ability to choose the option appropriate to their shareholders' preference and provides greater ability to manage reporting resources. Notable as the first issuer-side comment in the docket.
body:
Subject: File No. S7-2026-15
From: Lora Jones

We are in favor of the proposed rule. The rule will give companies the ability to choose the option appropriate to their shareholders' preference and provide greater ability to manage reporting resources.
rationale_evidence: rule gives companies ability to choose option appropriate to shareholders' preference

name: Mark Hyman, CPA
summary: CPA-investor opposing on discipline grounds: quarterly reporting forces each company to 'get its house in order' on a regular cycle, and that discipline is useful to outside investors who rely on the higher cadence.
body:
Subject: File No. S7-2026-15
From: Mark Hyman

I do not like this option. I am an investor and require the greater frequency of quarterly reporting vs semi-annual.

Quarterly reporting requires each company to "get their house in order" for the quarterly reporting cycle. That discipline is very useful to outside investors.
rationale_evidence: discipline of preparing each quarter is very useful to outside investors

name: Mark Patterson
summary: One sentence: as a retail trader, opposes the amendment because he uses quarterly reports to adjust investment strategies.
body:
Subject: S7-2026-15: Webform Comments from Mark Patterson
From: Mark Patterson

As a retail trader, I oppose this amendment because I use the quarterly reports to adjust my investment strategies.
rationale_evidence: use quarterly reports to adjust investment strategies

name: Marly Reese
summary: One-line opposition. Frames the proposal as terrible for retail investors and as widening the information and access gap between institutional firms and ordinary citizens.
body:
Subject: File No. S7-2026-15
From: Marly Reese

This is terrible for retail investors and only widens the information and access gap that institutional firms and day to day citizens have when looking at investment opportunities.
rationale_evidence: widens information and access gap between institutional firms and day-to-day citizens

name: Michael Shore
summary: Strong opposition citing centuries of corporate illegality surfaced through quarterly reports. Argues a 6-month gap gives companies more time to escalate problems into catastrophes and increases potential investor harm and damage to the U.S. economy. Closes: 'Protect the markets!'
body:
Subject: File No. S7-2026-15
From: Michael Shore

Under NO conditions should companies be allowed to only report every 6 months instead of quarterly. History, reaching back centuries, is full of companies acting illegally; the only way that behavior comes to light is through their quarterly reporting (and not always then, if they falsify financial records). Leaving companies to only report every 6 months would greatly increase the potential harm to investors with the corresponding damage to the stock markets and the US economy. It is best to catch such illegal behavior earlier, while it's a smaller problem, instead of giving companies more time to create a catastrophe. Protect the markets!
rationale_evidence: companies acting illegally only come to light through quarterly reporting; catch illegal behavior earlier

name: Peter Cwik
summary: Two-sentence vehement opposition. Six months is too long to find out 'if the company is screwing us.' 'Are you out of your mind? Do not change the rule!!!'
body:
Subject: File No. S7-2026-15
From: Peter Cwik

Keep the filing quarterly do not make it semi annual. We won't know for six months of the company is screwing us. Are you out of your mind? Do not change the rule!!!
rationale_evidence: won't know for six months if company is screwing us; do not change the rule

name: Peter Ford
summary: Long structured opposition with four points. (1) Information gaps create pricing inefficiencies and risk; six months without auditor-reviewed data is 'blind flying' for individuals. (2) 'Optional' won't stay optional and the wrong companies will opt in: adverse selection — companies with deteriorating margins or rising debt are most motivated to elect 10-S, skewing the universe toward elevated opacity risk. (3) Undermines earnings-based risk management — working capital trends, debt covenant headroom, segment performance, and management guidance credibility all become harder. (4) Market-efficiency argument cuts the other way: a two-tier system fragments information flow; semiannual filers will likely trade at a permanent information-risk discount.
body:
Subject: File No. S7-2026-15
From: Peter Ford

1. Information gaps create pricing inefficiencies and risk. Quarterly earnings have been the bedrock of fundamental analysis for decades. Cutting reporting to twice a year means investors go six months without an auditor-reviewed financial picture of a company. For individual investors, that's blind flying. For advisors managing client portfolios, it means extended periods where you simply don't know if the thesis on a position has changed. Professional advisors who rely on 10-Q data for position monitoring, valuation updates, and risk flagging would face a material degradation in signal quality.

2. "Optional" won't stay optional for long and the wrong companies will opt in. The framing as optional makes this sound benign. But in practice, companies with something to hide between periods. Those with deteriorating margins, rising debt, operational problems are the most motivated to elect semiannual filing. High-quality, stable businesses have little to gain. This creates adverse selection: the universe of semiannual filers will be skewed toward companies with elevated opacity risk, right at the moment investors have less visibility.

3. It undermines earnings-based risk management. Professional advisors use quarterly filings for far more than earnings: they track working capital trends, debt covenant headroom, revenue seasonality, segment-level performance, and management guidance credibility. A six-month gap makes all of this harder. Sector-sensitive portfolios where conditions shift materially quarter to quarter would be particularly exposed.

4. The market efficiency argument cuts the other way. Proponents (echoing Chairman Atkins' language about companies determining "the interim reporting frequency that best serves their business needs") frame this as reducing burden. But capital markets are efficient because information flows continuously and uniformly. Allowing a two-tier system fragments that. Stocks of semiannual filers would likely trade at a discount — a permanent information-risk premium, which ironically hurts the very companies that elect the option.
rationale_evidence: blind flying for individual investors; adverse selection — opaque companies most motivated to elect; bifurcated market; semiannual filers trade at transparency discount

name: Ronney Davis
summary: One sentence: 'Information is of the most importance and removal of any is very very bad!!!'
body:
Subject: File No. S7-2026-15
From: Ronney Davis

Information is of the most importance and removal of any is very very bad!!!
rationale_evidence: removal of any information is very very bad

name: Sig Hardester
summary: Brief opposition. Argues semiannual would substantially disadvantage retail mom-and-pop investors who rely on quarterly information for personal financial and investment decisions.
body:
Subject: File No. S7-2026-15
From: Sig Hardester

I am against the proposed rule change allowing semiannual financial reporting by public companies. I believe that this proposed change will substantially dis-advantage retail mom and pop investors, who rely on this information to make critical personal financial and investment decisions.
rationale_evidence: disadvantage mom-and-pop retail investors who rely on this info

name: Susan Davis
summary: Quotes the SEC's stated mission verbatim — protect investors, maintain fair and efficient markets, facilitate capital formation, enforce against market manipulation — and concludes 'The stated purpose of the SEC is NOT to make life easier for corporations.'
body:
Subject: File No. S7-2026-15
From: Susan Davis

No, no, a thousand times NO.

Here is the stated purpose of the Securities and Exchange Commission:

The Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) is primarily tasked with protecting investors, maintaining fair and efficient markets, and facilitating capital formation. It enforces laws against market manipulation and ensures that companies provide truthful information about their business and securities.

The stated purpose of the SEC is NOT to make life easier for corporations....
rationale_evidence: SEC's purpose is to protect investors not make life easier for corporations

name: Thomas Lupinacci, Sr.
summary: Experienced individual investor focused on small-cap concerns. Argues semi-annual filings would leave investors 'totally in the dark' on smaller-cap companies between reports because the media does not cover them, and that capital would naturally flow to majors as a result. Proposes a 3x/year compromise.
body:
Subject: S7-2026-15: Webform Comments from Thomas Lupinacci, Sr
From: Thomas Lupinacci, Sr
Affiliation: Individual Investor

As an experienced, individual investor I need as much information as possible. In particular on smaller capitalization companies. Too much can happen in between semi-annual reports that will not, can not be, covered by the media. The minor capitalized companies presently have little coverage, semi-annual filings would leave investors totally in the dark between financial reports. Investor dollars would naturally flow to the major corporations.

I would think reporting 3x a year might be a compromised position.

Sincerely
Tom L.
rationale_evidence: smaller-cap companies have little media coverage; semiannual leaves investors in dark; suggests 3x year as compromise

name: Vincent Jansen
summary: Opposing letter from a small independent RIA. Frames quarterly fundamental data as enabling efficient capital allocation, and warns that low-frequency measurements of the economy will produce pricing inaccuracies and volatility. Public quarterly data are irreplaceable for small RIAs and especially for index investors with no advisor affiliation. Asks rhetorically whether U.S. capitalism has benefitted from efficient markets more than it has suffered from regulatory requirements.
body:
Subject: S7-2026-15: Webform Comments from Artificial Alpha Investments
From: Artificial Alpha Investments / Vincent Jansen

Dear SEC,

My RIA utilizes fundamental data from quarterly filings to actively invest in individual stocks for clients. My work suggests that timely reporting of fundamental data results in more efficient allocation of capital. While I recognize the value of lowering regulatory hurdles, I want to emphasize the pricing inaccuracies and volatility risks that I expect to result from low-frequency measurements of the economy. Is it possible that America's incredible brand of capitalism has benefitted from efficient markets more than it has suffered from regulatory requirements?

Additionally, my RIA is small and independent. Public data like these have no replacement for me. Beyond my dependence on these data, I would suggest that public data are especially irreplaceable for index investors with no advisor affiliation. It seems to me like cohorts dependent on quarterly filings are growing at a fast pace.

Thank you for taking the time to read my perspective.
-Vincent Jansen
rationale_evidence: small independent RIA depends on quarterly fundamental data; America benefits from efficient markets

name: Diane Aaby
summary: Two words: 'Submit quarterly!!'
rationale_evidence: 'Submit quarterly!!'

name: Anonymous
summary: Structured opposition. Five concerns: quarterly cadence enables transparency / accountability / market confidence; 8-K is reactive and cannot substitute for periodic trend data; mixed quarterly/semiannual environment complicates peer analysis; less-resourced investors are disadvantaged by reduced standardization; reduced cadence weakens internal reporting discipline. Suggests streamlining the content of 10-Q rather than reducing its frequency.
rationale_evidence: 8-K backstop insufficient; comparability/info symmetry concerns from mixed environment; weaken internal reporting discipline; delay identification of control issues

name: Bayo Olabisi
role: University of Pennsylvania Wharton School Alumni
summary: Wharton alum opposing the proposal. Argues the quarterly system has worked for decades and isn't broken; AI is making reporting easier, so burden arguments are weak. Worries about volatility from delayed information (analogous to delayed government releases). Suggests if the Commission insists, it should run an experimental trial with a small group of investment companies before opening the option market-wide — without a careful dry run, risks outweigh rewards.
rationale_evidence: US stock market shining example; trial run instead of full market; less inter/intra sector comparability; potential for fraud/malfeasance

name: Marc Steinberg
role: Radford Chair in Law and Professor of Law, SMU Dedman School of Law; former SEC attorney (Division of Enforcement and Office of General Counsel)
summary: Substantive academic letter. Former SEC attorney (Enforcement, Office of General Counsel), Radford Chair in Law at SMU Dedman, author of the 2021 Oxford University Press book Rethinking Securities Law (American Book Fest 'best law book' award). Opposes the proposal outright, but argues if the Commission proceeds in its deregulatory mode, it must — at minimum — amend Form 8-K to require timely disclosure of additional important developments. Specific example: current Form 8-K does not require prompt disclosure of the loss of a material contract; under semiannual reporting that fact would not reach investors for six months, which he calls 'clearly unacceptable.'
rationale_evidence: if SEC adopts, at minimum amend Form 8-K to require more developments (loss of material contract)

name: Anonymous
summary: Anonymous commenter opposes the proposal but offers a fallback. Argues quarterly data is critical for both institutional and retail investors. If changes are made, companies must still provide the core financial statements quarterly: cash flow statement, balance sheet, income statement, share count, segment revenues, and debt disclosures. Rejects the smaller-company carve-out — smaller companies need MORE frequent reporting, not less, because they undergo more rapid structural change. (Docket lists as Anonymous; an early-pass 'Mike Dattilo / NBCUniversal' attribution was removed 2026-05-19 to match the docket and the unsigned body.)
rationale_evidence: if changes deemed necessary, require quarterly key financials (cash flow, balance sheet, income statement, shares outstanding, segment revenues)

name: Vladimir Samarin
summary: One sentence: switching to semiannual reporting will not allow investors to react to changes in business environment and company performance until it is too late.
rationale_evidence: investors cannot react to changes in business environment until too late

name: Adam Sawyers
summary: Notable issuer-side voice: a startup CEO opposing the proposal. Two sentences. Argues this would erode investor confidence in an already fragile system, and frames the change as corporations puppeteering politicians to work in their favor at the expense of democracy.
rationale_evidence: erode investor confidence; corporations puppeteering politicians, attack on democracy

name: Anonymous
summary: Anonymous opposition citing the current insider-trading environment, crypto, and loosely-regulated prediction platforms as reasons to keep quarterly disclosure. Argues quarterly reporting is the minimum standard, and companies seeking public investment should be obligated to release earnings every three months.
rationale_evidence: allegations of insider trading in equity/crypto/prediction platforms; quarterly is absolute minimum standard

name: Anthony Sirica
role: CPA and investor (AAPL, NVDA, BXSL, ARES, JPM, MOB, etc.)
summary: Conditional proposal from a CPA-investor. Suggests structuring the option like SOX Section 404(b): available only to Smaller Reporting Companies (SRCs), not large-cap issuers. Cites Apple as an example — the earnings release has only 4 short paragraphs, but the 10-Q contains substantially more useful information that investors in large-caps would lose under semiannual.
rationale_evidence: structure like SOX 404(b) — available to SRCs only, not large caps

name: Michael Mosquera
role: Private investor (more than half a lifetime)
summary: Thoughtful conditional proposal from a long-tenured private investor. Identifies two systemic problems: short-termism and weak board oversight. Proposes shareholders vote every three years between two options: (1) status quo, or (2) annual reporting paired with significant governance enhancements — CEO/CFO/COO barred from board, personal liability for board negligence, mandatory fiduciary-actions section in annual report, reduced public reporting overall. Trades reporting frequency for stronger board accountability.
rationale_evidence: two options voted by shareholders every 3 years; allow annual reporting only with enhanced board oversight

name: Alex Matache
summary: Frames quarterly reporting as the trust foundation of the American financial system. Argues 50 years of quarterly cadence is an accurate and acceptable barometer; reducing it benefits only insiders, manipulators, and bad actors. Companies wanting less regulation should go private; public-company status carries a transparency obligation.
rationale_evidence: trust born from transparency; only insiders/manipulators benefit from less; private equity is alternative

name: Benjamin Esper
summary: Two-paragraph opposition. Every stock market crash and manipulation traces to a lack of transparency and oversight. Lowering quarterly cadence will increase pump-and-dump schemes, insider trading, and bad business behavior.
rationale_evidence: every market crash from lack of transparency/oversight; only allows more pump-and-dump and insider trading

name: Blake Webb
summary: Retail investor opposition. Argues lowering from 3 interim reports to 1 between annual filings introduces more speculation and disproportionately hurts retail. As a 401(k) holder, he would lose confidence investing in companies that elect 10-S because the reduced transparency makes the asset feel unsafe.
rationale_evidence: would introduce more speculation; hurt retail investors; uninspired to invest in less-transparent market

name: Brian Wang
summary: Three sentences. Argues reporting should be increased, not reduced; AI has reduced the manual effort required for filings, so twice-a-month should not be impossible.
rationale_evidence: reporting should be increased; AI reduces manual effort; twice a month shouldn't be impossible

name: Christine Morton
role: Individual Retail Investor (US citizen residing in UK)
summary: Retail investor opposing the proposal. Acknowledges the compliance burden but asks how investors can hold boards accountable without timely reporting. Frames the proposal as a threat to US global market standing — global investors may pull money out if they cannot verify investment safety via regular reporting.
rationale_evidence: challenges with transparency as it is; affects US standing as global market; global investors may pull money

name: Kenneth I Kornbluh
role: CEO, Kambio Partners LLC (hedge fund managing five funds, hundreds of millions in assets)
summary: Substantive opposition from a hedge fund CEO managing five funds with hundreds of millions in client assets. Quarterly earnings and forward guidance are central to portfolio construction; semiannual would double the information gap and dramatically widen the existing insider-public asymmetry. Argues the Commission should move toward MORE frequent disclosure — proposes mandatory monthly disclosure of key performance metrics — not less. Also emphasizes the irreplaceable value of quarterly earnings calls (tone, confidence, command of detail) for assessing management quality.
rationale_evidence: hedge fund process depends heavily on quarterly reports; earnings calls irreplaceable; US dominance built on robust disclosure

name: Nathan Powers
summary: Small business owner writing on behalf of retail investors. References the canonical large frauds (Enron, WorldCom, Nikola, Wirecard) plus recent smaller shareholder-misleading settlements (Alphabet, Under Armour, Wells Fargo) to argue that a 6-month dark period would let minor fraud grow undetected. Also worries about volatility — concentrated earnings releases would cause share prices to 'collapse or soar.' Asks for status quo.
rationale_evidence: Enron/Worldcom/Nikola/Wirecard/Wells Fargo settlements; small invisible misstatements would grow undetected; extra volatility around twice-yearly reports

name: Oscar Meade
summary: One sentence: In an era of open information, having less data is a step backwards that gives public companies regulatory respite they implicitly agreed to bear when going public.
rationale_evidence: less data to make informed decisions is step backwards; companies agreed when going public

name: Anonymous
summary: Anonymous individual opposing. Investors, customers, and taxpayers deserve more transparency and frequent updates; semi-annual reporting does not serve the public good. Cites a hypothetical 'future Spirit Airlines fiasco' as the kind of event quarterly reporting would surface.
rationale_evidence: investors/customers/taxpayers deserve more transparency; future Spirit Airlines fiasco avoidable

name: Creighton Early
summary: Issuer-side voice (CFO at Willdan Group, a public company). Welcomes regulatory relief but rejects the proposed cadence change as the wrong lever. Argues the actual burden is the depth/quantity of footnotes, acquisitions, tax, and financing disclosures — not the frequency of filing. Says quarterly reporting itself is good for investors and the company; relief should come from reducing arcane disclosure requirements instead.
rationale_evidence: burden is less about frequency, more about depth of disclosure on footnotes/acquisitions/taxes; reduce depth not cadence

name: Lawrence
summary: Individual opposing on transparency and fraud-window grounds. Argues frequent regular updates underpin global investor trust in US markets; semi-annual reporting widens the fraud window and harms public reliance on disclosed information.
rationale_evidence: opens windows into fraud; US market trusted worldwide; switching dilutes trust

name: Andrew Parker
summary: Duplicate (original version) of Andrew Parker's comment — superseded by his amended, longer PDF at #462, which Tzachi kept as the canonical record (Oppose). Same doc-id 2367854. Held out of the count so Andrew Parker is not double-counted. Original #99 was classified Conditional; the amended #462 firms to Oppose. Duplicate (original version) of Andrew Parker's comment — superseded by his amended, longer PDF at #462, which Tzachi kept as the canonical record (Oppose). Same doc-id 2367854. Held out of the count so Andrew Parker is not double-counted. Original #99 was classified Conditional; the amended #462 firms to Oppose. Substantive audit-side conditional letter responding specifically to Request for Comment 18 (investor protection) and Request for Comment 26 (ICFR / auditor strategy). Argues semiannual reporting should be available only to companies that have NOT had material revisions, material misstatements, material period-end financial reporting control failures, or material ICFR significant deficiencies in the past three years. Cites SEC Staff Accounting Bulletin No. 99 on materiality. Provides EGC ICFR-failure statistics (~46% of EGC filers reported material weaknesses in 2017; FY2022 adverse-ICFR disclosure rates 5.8% / 17.7% / 38.0% by filer size). Argues narrowing eligibility increases capital-markets efficiency while reducing administrative burden on companies with proper governance, but acknowledges drawbacks to comparability and consistency.
rationale_evidence: Narrowing scope to exclude companies with material weaknesses / ICFR failures for at least three years; addresses Request for Comment 18 + 26.

name: Davis Sego
summary: One-line opposition. Wants quarterly cadence preserved on trust-in-markets grounds; frames the change as letting Washington further erode market trustworthiness.
rationale_evidence: 'Keep it quarterly, Washington lies and cheats too much already'

name: Jose Sainz
summary: Initially supported, changed mind after research. Concedes quarterly reports may drive layoff-style short-term decisions but trusts companies less. Companies will hide problems and insider-trade if given longer reporting gaps; keep the current system.
rationale_evidence: cannot trust companies to do right; not to hide problems for longer or do insider trading

name: Paul Kalenak
summary: Opposition on accountability grounds. Quarterly reporting keeps companies accountable to shareholders for informed investment decisions; cites monthly government statistics as proof that the burden of frequent reporting is manageable.
rationale_evidence: government statistics reported monthly; not difficult to continue quarterly

name: Robert Burlando
summary: Individual investor opposition with European-comparison argument. Argues semiannual reporting in Europe has hidden corporate malfeasance that earlier quarterly reporting could have surfaced. US capital markets' transparency and liquidity advantage depends on quarterly cadence.
rationale_evidence: semiannual in Europe has hidden corporate malfeasance; US markets most transparent/liquid; quarterly maintains global leadership

name: Rodrigo Cabrera
summary: Investor opposition with insider-information argument. Less frequent public reporting increases the relative value of insider information and erodes market confidence; asks SEC to keep quarterly mandatory.
rationale_evidence: diluting to semiannual makes insider information more valuable; erode confidence

name: Shane Dalton
summary: Retail-investor opposition with information-asymmetry framing. Halving the data hurts retail investors who lack research teams; only large funds benefit. Threatens to sell out of any company that elects semiannual reporting.
rationale_evidence: only large investment funds will benefit; would sell any business that doesn't report quarterly

name: Tommy Brumfield
summary: Three-sentence opposition. Semiannual reporting decreases transparency for investors. Bad idea.
rationale_evidence: decrease transparency for investors; bad idea

name: /r/wallstreetbets
summary: Long structured opposition letter from r/wallstreetbets, framed as the collective voice of retail. Frames the 10-Q as the single most important leveling mechanism between retail and institutional investors — institutions have expert networks, alt-data, satellite imagery, credit-card panels, and direct management access; retail has the 10-Q. Argues the proposal partially unwinds one of the foundational compromises of the 1934 Act. Closes by asking the SEC not to spend this rulemaking making it harder for retail to participate on fair terms.
rationale_evidence: leveling mechanism between retail/institutional (satellite, alt-data); S&P 500 at all-time high files Q every quarter; partial unwinding of 1934 Act foundational compromise

name: Brian Pina
summary: Two-sentence opposition citing harm to retirement accounts and retail investors. Asks SEC to keep quarterly reporting requirement.
rationale_evidence: negative consequences for retirement accounts and retail investors

name: Ian Ross
summary: Opposition on trust grounds. Companies cannot be fully trusted to relay material data voluntarily; less frequent mandatory reporting disserves stakeholders and government decision-making.
rationale_evidence: companies cannot be 100% trusted to relay material data outside mandatory reporting

name: James Mellor
summary: One-line strongly-worded opposition. No supporting argument provided.
rationale_evidence: 'I strongly oppose the proposed SEC Rule change'

name: Jonathin Kennedy
summary: Opposition from a consultant. Frames the proposal as creating 'dark periods' that increase information asymmetry favoring institutional insiders. Suggests the Commission should be moving toward more frequent or real-time disclosures, not less frequent ones.
rationale_evidence: dark periods increasing information asymmetry favoring institutional insiders over retail

name: Kimberly Carnahan
summary: Emphatic opposition. Quarterly reporting has gotten easier over time thanks to technology; the only reason to reduce cadence is to favor insiders and worsen the uneven playing field.
rationale_evidence: creating quarterly reporting has become easier due to tech; only reason to reduce is advantage to insiders

name: Matthew Sing
summary: Short opposition citing free-market reasoning. Free markets depend on consistent reliable data; less information means less informed trading.
rationale_evidence: less informed trading; free markets only work with consistent reliable data

name: Noel King
summary: One-sentence opposition warning the change will drastically reduce individual-investor participation in public markets.
rationale_evidence: will drastically reduce number of individual investors willing to invest

name: Pat Meehan
summary: Administrative-law-flavored opposition. Argues the proposal cannot survive the Supreme Court's Loper-Bright (2024) ruling against agency deference; the SEC's quantitative justification is theoretical and speculative, and the release itself (footnote 332) concedes uncertainty about impacts.
rationale_evidence: won't survive Loper-Bright; agencies need quantifiable justification; footnote 332 admits unknown impacts

name: Patrick Ryan
summary: Opposition framed as cost-benefit asymmetry: insider-trading downside is clear and likely, upside is small and concentrated in a few thousand public companies. Quarterly reporting is a staple of American financial trust; the proposal should be terminated.
rationale_evidence: regulatory burden small and companies well-equipped; avenues for insider trading clear; upsides concentrated in few thousand corps

name: Peter Van Flein
summary: Investment-professional opposition. 'It ain't broke, so don't try to fix it.' Argues the change would widen the information gulf between institutional and retail investors. Asks SEC to reject.
rationale_evidence: create gulf between institutional and retail information

name: Rob Bardy
summary: Three-line opposition from someone affiliated with the Association of Professional Accountants. 'Keep it quarterly. If it ain't broke, don't fix it.'
rationale_evidence: 'Keep it quarterly please. If it ain't broke, don't fix it'

name: Scott Lundstrom
summary: Two-sentence opposition from a self-described market analyst. Investors will be placed at a disadvantage; wants more data, not less.
rationale_evidence: investors at disadvantage; want as much data as possible

name: Sonia Woldow
summary: Retail-investor opposition. SEC filings are the best windows into companies; please keep them quarterly.
rationale_evidence: best windows into companies

name: Suzanne Gallant
summary: Opposition framed as Wall Street vs. average person. The change favors Wall Street at the expense of the average investor; another erosion of standards that made the US great.
rationale_evidence: another way for Wall Street to win and average person to lose

name: Tal Madison
summary: Six-page lawyer-quality letter arguing the proposal will not survive judicial review. Four problems: (1) post-Loper Bright (2024), the SEC bears the full interpretive burden under §13(a) and cannot rely on Chevron deference — the statutory text references 'quarterly reports' in the alternative, posing a serious textual problem; (2) the major-questions doctrine (West Virginia v. EPA; Biden v. Nebraska) applies because eliminating quarterly disclosure is a structural change to ~4,000 reporting companies' information environment, not a technical adjustment; (3) under State Farm and Regents, the Commission has not provided a reasoned explanation for departing from the 1970 quarterly regime, has not credited the contested academic literature on short-termism, and has not catalogued investor reliance interests; (4) the cost-benefit analysis fails the D.C. Circuit standard (Chamber of Commerce v. SEC; Business Roundtable v. SEC) — must quantify issuer savings and investor costs, address bid-ask spreads and cost of equity capital, and consider less restrictive alternatives. Concludes the SEC has not carried the interpretive, statutory, or administrative-law burden required to finalize the rule as proposed.
rationale_evidence: Loper-Bright/major questions/State Farm/D.C. Circuit cost-benefit; reliance interests of analysts, mutual funds, credit committees, rating agencies

name: Anonymous
summary: Strongly worded opposition citing Enron and WorldCom. Frames six-month cadence as 'a gift-wrapped invitation for corporate malfeasance.' Argues retail investors will be left holding the bag while insiders trade on information asymmetry; if a company can't handle quarterly reporting, it shouldn't be publicly traded.
rationale_evidence: 'Enron and WorldCom... insiders feast on information asymmetry' — FR+IA+IP.

name: Christ Fisk
summary: 20-year retail investor. Argues quarterly reports provide 'real truth' versus CEO spin; retail investors don't stand a chance against insiders trading before required disclosure.
rationale_evidence: 'retail investor don't stand a chance to insiders' — IA+FR.

name: David Long
summary: Two sentences. Earnings should stay quarterly. Tangential criticism: SEC should be 'more concerned with dark pools and synthetic shares.'
rationale_evidence: No substantive engagement beyond bare position.

name: James Wiebe
summary: Opposition framed as civic argument ('as an American'). Companies under less scrutiny are less likely to behave according to the law. We all have a stake in the stability of public companies.
rationale_evidence: 'less scrutiny and therefore less likely to behave according to the law' — FR+IP.

name: Lisa Copenhaver
summary: Three-sentence opposition. Fraud can be hidden in six months; investor needs timely updates.
rationale_evidence: 'Fraud can be hidden in 6 months' — explicit FR+IP.

name: Munir Hakim
summary: Retail-investor opposition with seatbelt analogy. Argues 'optional' won't stay optional — if big players switch, smaller ones follow until semiannual becomes the industry standard. Worries about retirement savings; fraud going undetected.
rationale_evidence: 'If all the big players change... smaller players will follow suit and become the standard' — explicit EX (spillover).

name: Theo
summary: Brief opposition citing UK and South Korea as international cautionary examples of semiannual reporting being 'ineffective across the world stage.' Fraud and insider trading concern; reduction in market transparency.
rationale_evidence: 'UK and South Korea... ineffective across the world stage' — ICc (note: South Korea is a new international comparison in the docket).

name: C. Allshouse
summary: One-line opposition from a self-identified attorney. No benefit to investors at 6-month cadence.
rationale_evidence: 'no benefit to investors at 6 months' — IP framing in one line.

name: Katherine Winfield
summary: Argues the proposal dilutes the purpose of the reports and harms 'those sought to be protected.' Companies could use the gap to avoid penalties for disallowed activities, harming consumers.
rationale_evidence: 'companies could avoid penalties for disallowed activities' — fraud/IP framing.

name: Lawrence Rand
role: Visiting Professor of Economics, Brown University (advisor on submitted 2018 undergraduate thesis by Rosen, Shire, Winston)
summary: Submitted as the comment letter is a 28-page December 2018 Brown University undergraduate thesis (authors Theodore Rosen, Ethan Shire, Benjamin Winston; primary advisor Lawrence A. Rand; secondary advisor David Weil). The paper recommends a bifurcated reporting system in which smaller companies would be allowed to report on a semiannual basis while mid- and large-cap companies continue quarterly disclosure — explicitly endorsing the SEC's scaled-relief alternative rather than the proposal's universal optionality. The paper also recommends companies issuing earnings guidance consider ending the practice. Acknowledges the transparency and cost-of-capital benefits of quarterly reporting (Stanford / Arif-De George spillover evidence; Reg FD; Fu-Kraft-Zhang cost-of-equity findings) but argues these are outweighed for small caps by short-termism (Nooyi, Musk, Shaich, Fink, Lutz) and disproportionate compliance costs ($541 vs $3,345 per $1M revenue audit-fee disparity from Audit Analytics; ~4.5 days to close per PwC). Cites Pozen's UK study as evidence that frequency change did not materially affect reinvestment, but flags Pozen's concern that bifurcation could reduce small-cap liquidity. Concludes a one-size-fits-all rule is suboptimal and that scaled relief based on annual revenue (per JOBS Act framework) is the most defensible structure.
rationale_evidence: AL: explicit recommendation of bifurcated/scaled-relief system (small-cap semiannual, mid/large quarterly). ST: short-termism extensively discussed (Nooyi, Musk, Shaich, Fink, Lutz). CB: cost of compliance (Audit Analytics fee disparity; PwC close timeline; small-cap OPEX). IA: institutional-vs-retail reliance on 10-Q (Arif-De George spillovers). FR: insider trading risk if disclosure cadence drops. MF: price-discovery and transparency benefits (Stanford / Beaver-McNichols-Wang). ICs: Pozen UK study cited as supportive of frequency-neutral outcomes.

name: Matt Marino
summary: Investor opposition citing reliability of the quarterly standard and minimal corporate burden. Argues the change would diminish trust, discourage investment, and push investors toward alternatives to public shares.
rationale_evidence: 'trust is diminished with less visibility' — IP+MF+US.

name: MWM
summary: Short opposition. Argues the change 'funnels more power to the powerful and takes away information from the average Joe.'
rationale_evidence: 'funnels more power to the powerful... takes away information from the average Joe' — explicit IA.

name: Nikolay
summary: Investor opposition. Reducing frequency reduces market efficiency and gives insider traders an unfair advantage. Rebuts the burden argument by noting modern compliance costs are 'close to zero.'
rationale_evidence: 'corporate insider trading to gain significantly more unfair advantage' — FR+IA+MF.

name: Noah Kerchner
summary: Long structured opposition (~750 words). Four core objections: (1) reduced transparency / increased information asymmetry favoring insiders, institutions, sophisticated analysts; (2) heightened misconduct risk — earnings management, channel stuffing, delayed problem recognition; (3) damage to market efficiency and liquidity, fragmenting the market into a two-tiered system; (4) weak justification — compliance costs haven't prevented capital formation, 'optional' won't stay optional. Proposes the SEC move in the opposite direction: more frequent disclosures, shorter deadlines, broader auditor review, KPI disclosure, real-time XBRL.
rationale_evidence: Four-pillar structured opposition with explicit AL proposals (monthly updates, shorter deadlines, KPI disclosure).

name: Varad Kandadai
summary: Two-sentence opposition citing transparency as the reason US markets are 'attractive to many countries.'
rationale_evidence: 'transparency is what makes our system... attractive to many countries' — US.

name: Anonymous
summary: Two-sentence opposition. Argues the SEC must keep mandatory quarterly reporting because semiannual reporting would reduce investor access to data.
rationale_evidence: 'reduces investors access to data'

name: David Bolling Wells
role: Former CFO Netflix; Board Chair Wise; Lead Independent Director HIMS
summary: Substantive opposition from a former Netflix CFO and current board chair of Wise (which reported semiannually as a UK company before its 5/11/26 Nasdaq listing). Acknowledges the burden of the four-quarter cycle but argues on balance it is the best compromise: it drives discipline and rigor, gives long investors four annual check-ins, and underpins US capital markets' trust premium and "democratic" playing field versus institutions. Predicts an opt-in would attract a mixed bag of less-disciplined companies, breeding fraud, slower impairments, greater insider information value, and longer-lived bad strategy. Urges the SEC not to confuse the need for securities litigation reform (loser pays) with changes to the reporting cycle.
rationale_evidence: 'US capital markets the envy of the world' (US); 'democratic capital markets' (IP, IA); 'Fraud, slower impairments, increased insider knowledge value' (FR); Wise UK semiannual experience cited as cautionary (ICc); '4 quarter cycle... grinding, demanding... overly short term financial focus' (ST, CB); 'market will be left to sift through this' (MF)

name: Debra Frost
summary: Sharp investor opposition. Argues semiannual reporting would effectively shut out small investors who cannot afford a wealth advisor and is a "financial/power grab by the elite" that ties the hands of middle and lower-class self-directed investors. Closes with "Shame on the SEC, who used to be a reliable watchdog FOR ALL AMERICANS, not corporations."
rationale_evidence: 'shut out small investors who do not have the methods or financial means'; 'power grab by the elite'; 'watchdog FOR ALL AMERICANS'

name: Kyle Hedden
summary: Investor opposition. Calls the proposal "entirely impractical with minimal benefits and more risk to investors," arguing regular pulse on company performance is paramount for educated investment decisions and that the change would give a massive advantage to institutions and insiders, disregarding the general public.
rationale_evidence: 'massive advantage only to institutions and insiders' (IA, FR); 'regular pulse on company performance is paramount' (MF); 'general population of investors' (IP); 'minimal benefits' (CB by implication)

name: Levi Lentz
summary: Investor opposition citing transparency as the key for all investors to make decisions. Argues automation should drive disclosure in the OPPOSITE direction — more frequent (e.g., monthly) reporting, not less.
rationale_evidence: 'Transparency in what is happening in a company' (IP); 'report more often (say monthly), not less' (AL)

name: Masey Moshref
summary: Short investor opposition expressing low confidence in market transparency, referencing "doge cuts on regulators that monitored the economic data." Argues the market should be an instrument for people's livelihoods, not a gambling tool gate-kept by elitists.
rationale_evidence: 'transparency in the market is extremely low'; 'gambling tool that is gate kept by elitist'

name: Chad L.
rationale_evidence: 'Fortune 500 companies are already making so much money off the common man'; 'retail etf investors'; 'total know how is the only thing'
summary: One-liner opposition. Argues retail ETF investors rely on quarterly disclosure as the only check against information advantages held by big-money institutions.

name: David C.
rationale_evidence: 'I do not have the resources that big firms do' (IA); 'long term plan for the company' (ST, engaged-and-pushed-back); 'ability to invest with adequate information' (MF, IP); 'rely more on rumors' (FR by implication)
summary: Retail-investor opposition. Argues less frequent reporting will force reliance on rumors, that long-term focus is already a free choice for any issuer, and that the proposed mindset shift is not guaranteed to materialize.

name: Monica Braman
rationale_evidence: one-line preference for quarterly; Inclusive picks up implicit transparency
summary: One-line opposition: prefers quarterly reporting to stay informed.

name: Andreas Jensen
rationale_evidence: 'public should remain informed' (IP); 'mega corps to hide from the people'; 'insider corruption' (FR); 'everyday man' (IA)
summary: Two-paragraph opposition. Frames the rule as enabling corporate concealment and insider corruption at retail investors' expense, with an analogy to United Healthcare.

name: Brian
rationale_evidence: 'more transparency between companies and consumers not less' (IP); 'expense of the individual' (IA)
summary: One-paragraph opposition. Calls for more transparency, not less; rejects propping up companies at the expense of the individual.

name: David
rationale_evidence: one-line position; no substantive argument
summary: One-line opposition: wants reporting to stay as it is.

name: Edward Dennison
rationale_evidence: 'transparency, accessibility, and depth of its capital markets' (IP); 'United States leads the world' (US); 'no evidence presented that the cost of biannual as opposed to quarterly reporting is materially different' (CB); 'visibility for investors into the conditions and prospects' (MF)
summary: Three-point opposition. Cites current quarterly disclosure visibility, absence of cost-difference evidence, and US capital-market leadership as reasons to keep the status quo.

name: Garrett Beery
rationale_evidence: 'transparency'; 'hurt investors, employees' (IP); 'people commiting crime with insider knowledge' (FR)
summary: Short opposition. Argues quarterly reports build investor trust and employee confidence, and that the change would primarily benefit insider-information actors.

name: Mike Wong
rationale_evidence: 'Keep the public informed' (IP under balanced/inclusive read; Literalist treats as too thin and codes NR)
summary: Two-line opposition. Keep quarterly reporting; keep the public informed.

name: Robert Andrews
rationale_evidence: 'Once one company does it, competitors will' (EX); 'savings are miniscule' (CB); 'small investors' vs 'insiders and large investors' (IA); 'More time for fraud to grow. How much worse would Enron, Tyco, or Worldcom' (FR); engages 'Optional' framing (OP)
summary: Numbered four-point opposition: (1) opt-in becomes de facto mandatory through competitive pressure, (2) savings are trivial, (3) six months is too long for small investors, (4) extending the window enlarges fraud opportunities. Cites Enron, Tyco, Worldcom.

name: Ryan Foli
rationale_evidence: 'every report wil be that much more volatile... more dangerous place to invest' (MF); 'reduce the visibility of operations and allow companies to hide from its owners, its investors' (IP)
summary: Strong opposition. Argues earnings reports would become more volatile with fewer of them, the market would become more dangerous, and reducing visibility is never the right move in finance.

name: Sean Brady
rationale_evidence: 'consumer and investor confidence' (IP); 'engineered to increase corruption' (FR)
summary: Rhetorical opposition. Asks how this increases consumer and investor confidence; frames it as engineering for corruption against a backdrop of weakened enforcement.

name: Steve Coston
rationale_evidence: abstract fairness appeal with no taxonomy-mappable substance
summary: Two-line opposition. Defines fair, argues the rule violates one or both parts of the definition.

name: Antolin (Tony) Saiz
rationale_evidence: 'small investors... no other recourse to protect our investments' (IA, IP); 'must exclusively rely on the 10-Q' (IA)
summary: Retail-investor opposition from a CPA writing as private citizen. Argues small investors lack access to direct company communication and rely exclusively on quarterly reports.

name: Arthur Lyssenko
rationale_evidence: one-line rejection, no substantive argument
summary: One-line opposition ('Stop with the grifting'). No substantive engagement with the proposal.

name: Kimberly O'Brien
rationale_evidence: 'Corporations need to be more transparent, not less' (IP)
summary: Short, emphatic opposition. Argues for more corporate transparency, not less.

name: Dennis Newell
rationale_evidence: Position statement only ('Quarterly is barely frequent enough'); no substantive argument (NR).
summary: One-line opposition. Argues quarterly is already barely sufficient.

name: Desiree
rationale_evidence: 'Insiders quietly sell their shares to you... before the bad news tanks the stock' (IA, FR); 'More time for fraud to grow' (FR); 'Once large companies start filing twice a year, smaller competitors face pressure' (EX, OP); 'The supposed savings are tiny... cost of worse information falls on you' (CB).
summary: Substantive four-point opposition: information gap for retail investors, fraud window, voluntary-becomes-mandatory dynamic, and cost savings flowing to executives rather than investors.

name: J R Merrill
rationale_evidence: 'Absolutely not!' Position only; no substantive argument (NR).
summary: Short, emphatic opposition. No substantive argument.

name: James Medici
rationale_evidence: 'Information should be free and open to all people' (IP, transparency).
summary: Frames opposition in democratic / transparency terms. Information access as a public-interest argument.

name: Jeremy Brown
rationale_evidence: 'I as an investor am 100% opposed.' Position only; no substantive argument (NR).
summary: Investor opposition. No substantive argument beyond the position.

name: Justin Smith
rationale_evidence: 'I am writing in support of semi-annual reporting requirements'; 'forced corporations to focus narrowly on meeting short-term earnings estimates and satisfying Wall Street analysts' (ST).
summary: Substantive Support. The full short-termism argument: quarterly cycle distorts management toward earnings management at the expense of long-range planning.

name: Kerri Pinkston
rationale_evidence: 'Do not change the reporting frequency, citizens have a right to know and understand' (IP). Most of the letter is off-topic political commentary about the President.
summary: Mostly off-topic political commentary on the President; the final sentence asks the SEC not to change reporting frequency, framed as a right-to-know.

name: Michael Turyn
rationale_evidence: 'Corporate fraud and malfeasance are already out-of-control... doubling the period for reporting can only increase them' (FR); 'we ought to keep a keen eye on the beings we create' (IP, oversight).
summary: Anti-proposal on fraud-deterrence and oversight grounds. Frames corporations as creations of society that need close monitoring.

name: Noah G
rationale_evidence: 'Increase corporate greed, and limit accountability' (IP, oversight).
summary: FINRA-registered (operations) opposes. Accountability framing.

name: Rebecca Wieser
rationale_evidence: 'Companies should not go dark for half a year' (IP, transparency).
summary: One-line opposition framed as a transparency / honesty argument.

name: Sherri
rationale_evidence: 'There should absolutely be oversight' (IP).
summary: One-line opposition. Oversight framing.

name: Tim Mulligan
rationale_evidence: 'Keep U.S. markets the world's leaders' (US); cites WSJ that the proposal 'would do nothing to advance listings or improve confidence in the U.S. markets'.
summary: Attorney (former CPA, former hedge fund analyst) opposes on US-competitiveness grounds. Cites a May 13 WSJ op-ed (Spencer Jacob).

name: Angela Schneider
rationale_evidence: 'Citizens must be allowed to participate... not have the laws slanted against them' (IP, fairness / access).
summary: Brief framing of opposition as fairness — citizens should not have laws slanted against them.

name: Anthony C Miller
rationale_evidence: 'Less insight or information on the companies' (IP); 'increase volatility around those periods of reporting rather than a drip or consistent updates' (MF, jump volatility); 'allow companies to obscure or downplay risks' (FR).
summary: Argues less disclosure helps no one, would increase volatility around reporting events, and lets companies obscure or spin risks.

name: Ben Brown
rationale_evidence: 'Widen the information gap between Wall Street insiders and ordinary shareholders' (IA); 'longer windows in which business deterioration, accounting irregularities... can go undetected' (FR, AU); 'increase volatility... raise the cost of capital' (MF); 'evidence for these claims is weak' (CB, ST rebuttal).
summary: Retail investor opposing. Substantive engagement with the full SEC argument map — rebuts both the short-termism and compliance-cost claims, then makes the retail information-gap, fraud, market-discipline, and cost-of-capital arguments.

name: Brian
rationale_evidence: 'Keep transparency and regular reporting... Protect investors!!' (IP).
summary: Short opposition. Transparency + investor protection framing.

name: Christian Kritas
role: Licensed investment banking representative and Associate - Model Risk
rationale_evidence: 'Put those without access to be expensive private market data at a disadvantage' (IA); 'alternatives available such as Reg D for companies where going fully public does not make sense' (AL — alternative structural framing).
summary: Investment-banking rep opposing. Argues quarterly filings level the playing field for retail investors and students who lack paid private-market data. Suggests Reg D as an alternative path for issuers that do not want public-cadence disclosure.

name: James
role: Activist, business owner, tutor and student
rationale_evidence: 'It keeps things transparent and fair' (IP).
summary: Short opposition. Transparency + fairness framing.

name: Joseph d suh
rationale_evidence: 'Please protect us.' Three-word plea — no substantive argument (NR / IP inclusive only).
summary: Three-word plea. No substantive argument.

name: Matthew Jubinsky
rationale_evidence: Position only ('Reporting should be kept to quarterly'); no substantive argument (NR).
summary: Short opposition. No substantive argument.

name: Paul Bussman
rationale_evidence: 'Quarterly reports help build confidence... encouraging investment' (MF); 'the average citizen would be inclined to avoid investment' (IA, retail-focused).
summary: Argues quarterly reporting underpins investor confidence; losing it would push retail investors away from markets.

name: Richard Thompson
rationale_evidence: 'Do not hide companies financial visibility' (IP); 'super well informed (billionaires) more advantages and the uninformed (retail) more disadvantages' (IA); 'volatility will greatly increase around those six month earnings' (MF).
summary: Information asymmetry, jump-volatility, and downside-scenario framing. Worries about undeserved bankruptcies or bailouts driven by 6-month opacity.

name: Timothy A. Mann
rationale_evidence: Explicit rebuttal of short-termism: 'decoupled from reality' (ST); 'rely on financial advisors, analysts, and market pricing that all depend on the 10-Q as a ground truth' (RI, IA); 'reduces management accountability' (IP); 'Fraud Deterrence' heading (FR); 'volatility around reporting events' (MF); 'US markets are the global gold standard' (US).
summary: Structured three-point opposition: (1) rebuts short-termism, (2) information-supply-chain reliance for retail, (3) fraud deterrence. Frames US markets as global gold standard.

name: Gary Kirian
rationale_evidence: 'more time for corruption to flourish' (FR); 'we do not want more corruption in this beautiful country' invokes investor protection by implication (IP, inclusive only).
summary: Short retail Oppose tying longer reporting cycles to increased corruption risk. Plain anti-fraud framing without invoking institutional or market-function mechanics.

name: Grandpa Trades
rationale_evidence: 'modern technology allows for near immediate reporting at lower costs than ever before' — directly rebuts the CB premise for the proposal.
summary: Retail Oppose that rebuts the cost-burden rationale on its head: modern tech makes frequent reporting cheaper than ever, undercutting the SEC's $330K vs $132K cost-savings argument.

name: Jay Cai
role: Jay Cai, Associate Professor of Finance, Drexel University; Elizabeth Demers, Deloitte Professor of Accounting, University of Waterloo; Tu Nguyen, Associate Professor of Finance, University of Waterloo
rationale_evidence: EX: 'net information spillovers to peer firms are 70-99% higher on earnings announcement dates' / 'semi-public good' / 'underproduction of socially valuable information'. MF: 'price discovery and efficiency' / 'capital markets as a whole'. CMP: 'comparability, capital allocation, and market efficiency'. AL: Recommendation 4 — 'Consider allowing optional semi-annual reporting only for smaller firms'. CB: 'cost of quarterly reporting is also likely to be more burdensome for smaller firms'. IP (inclusive only): 'socially valuable information' framing as implied investor-protection mandate.
summary: Academic finance letter from Cai (Drexel), Demers and Nguyen (Waterloo) attaching their working paper. Central evidence: quarterly earnings announcements generate net peer-firm information spillovers of 70-99% — a 'semi-public good' externality the SEC's economic analysis understates. Recommends scaled relief for smaller firms only (AL).

name: John Beaty
rationale_evidence: SG: 'one brave lad or lassie will do it right and thump the competition' — the SG mechanism that firms maintaining quarterly reporting differentiate themselves. RI (inclusive): 'the investment consumer will go elsewhere' implies investor reliance on the existing cadence.
summary: Short Oppose with garbled prose but a recognizable SG (signaling) mechanic: firms that keep quarterly reporting will out-compete those that switch to semiannual.

name: Luke Galluccio
rationale_evidence: Five-word letter: 'This is an unfair proposal'. Position without engagement.
summary: Five-word Oppose. No substantive rationale.

name: Rohan Sharma
summary: OFF-TOPIC: letter addresses 'Docket No. SEC-2026-2839-0001 - Technical Comments on Proposed Amendments to Regulation S-T and Form 10-K'. It does not engage S7-2026-15 (semiannual reporting). Filed under the S7-2026-15 docket by submission error. Excluded from public site, regression, and rater statistics.

name: Allen Raevsky & Company LLC CPAS
rationale_evidence: IP: 'Accountability is paramount to transparency for investors concerns' (explicit transparency framing). AU (inclusive only): CPA letterhead + accountability framing implies audit-verification concern.
summary: Short CPA-firm letter (Allen Raevsky CPA, ABV, CVA, CFE) opposing the rule on transparency-and-accountability grounds.

name: Ashley Ramirez
rationale_evidence: FR: 'Why would insiders need one more advantage?' — insider trading framing.
summary: 15-word retail Oppose. Insider-advantage framing.

name: Charles J. Gallo
rationale_evidence: IA: 'uneven playing field among investors' — surface IA framing. AU (inclusive only) on CPA letterhead.
summary: One-line CPA opposition. 'Uneven playing field among investors' (IA framing).

name: Fevered Fisherman
rationale_evidence: IP: 'More transparentcy. Not less. More accountability.' — explicit transparency framing. IA (Primary/Inclusive): 'reckoning for the ruling class' + 'middle class has been abused' invokes the retail-vs-institutional distinction.
summary: Populist 39-word Oppose. Transparency-and-accountability framing with class-warfare overtones.

name: Lee Lin
rationale_evidence: IP: 'Transparency is important to outside investors'. IA (Primary/Inclusive): 'outside investors' framing distinguishes retail from insiders.
summary: Short retail Oppose. Transparency for 'outside investors' framing.

name: Philip Birsh
rationale_evidence: FR: 'insiders having advantages over normal individuals' + 'cloak of non-reporting'. IA: 'normal individuals' vs 'insiders'; 'majority of investors... not in the top tiers'. ICc: 'The Chinese stock market does not require GAAP accounting. This terrible idea is similar in practice'. IP: 'inform the public... investors will be damaged'. RI: 'guardians of pension funds protect our people's retirement funds'. US (inclusive only): implicit US-vs-China contrast.
summary: Substantive 175-word retail Oppose with multiple rationale codes. Cites the Chinese stock market as a cautionary international comparison (ICc — new). Frames pension fund oversight (RI) as the constituency harmed.

name: Colleen Wold
role: Finance and governance executive (public-company reporting, IPO readiness, SOX/internal controls, Audit Committee engagement, operational finance leadership)
rationale_evidence: OP: 'I support the Commission's effort to provide flexibility in reporting cadence'. CB: 'quarterly reporting can create significant organizational burden... Management attention, finance resources, auditor coordination'. ST: 'this cadence can unintentionally reinforce short-term decision-making and near-term performance management at the expense of longer-term strategic execution'. IA: 'reducing reporting frequency may further widen the information gap between institutional and non-institutional investors'. IP: 'retail and non-institutional investors rely more heavily on periodic SEC disclosures as a primary source of transparent, comparable, and decision-useful information'. CMP: 'reduced comparability between issuers'. AL: explicit eligibility-criteria framework — 'no material weaknesses in ICFR... no significant restatements... independent Audit Committee oversight'. AU: 'no material weaknesses in internal control over financial reporting' + 'auditor coordination'. CC: 'debt covenants... market discipline'. RI: 'investor expectations... analyst coverage'. MF + OV (inclusive only): 'broader market transparency' + 'providing conditional optionality may particularly benefit issuers'.
summary: Substantive 800-word governance-executive letter supporting flexibility with explicit gate-keeping eligibility (no recent ICFR material weaknesses, timely filing history, independent Audit Committee, etc.). Conditional unanimous, 10-code letter — the most rationale-rich letter in the corpus.

name: Cynthia Foster
rationale_evidence: Three-word letter: 'Keep it quarterly'. Position without engagement.
summary: Three-word Oppose. No substantive rationale.

name: Damon Schwartz
rationale_evidence: FR: 'unfair advantage for corporate insiders... increase the presence and likelihood of insider trading'. OP: '"optional" statement doesn't fix the problem because companies that opt in are often the ones whose investors most need frequent updates' — substantively engages and rebuts the OP rationale. IA (Primary/Inclusive): 'I rely on... longer gaps create an unfair advantage'. RI (Primary/Inclusive): personal-reliance framing on quarterly cadence.
summary: Substantive 119-word Oppose. Notable for explicitly rebutting the proposal's optionality argument — opt-in self-selection makes the rule worse, not better.

name: David Lesh
rationale_evidence: IP: 'Investors need transparency so as to make wise decisions'.
summary: Short retail Oppose on transparency grounds.

name: Garen Sarafian
role: Vice President, Investor Relations, Bausch Health (BHC)
rationale_evidence: OP: 'We support the Commission's effort to provide greater flexibility'. CB: 'reduce administrative burden and modestly lower external auditor quarterly review procedures and related fees'. AL: 'broader structural changes would likely be necessary, such as extended filing deadlines and/or streamlined reporting requirements similar to certain European reporting frameworks'. ICs: 'similar to certain European reporting frameworks' — international evidence read as a constructive supportive model. CC: 'Investor expectations, lender requirements... continue to drive quarterly reporting discipline'. SG: 'peer companies may also elect to continue quarterly reporting practices, reinforcing market expectations for quarterly transparency and comparability' — peer-signaling mechanism. CMP: 'reinforcing market expectations for quarterly transparency and comparability'. AU (primary/inclusive only): 'external auditor quarterly review procedures and related fees'. RI (primary/inclusive only): 'Investor expectations... analyst coverage' framing.
summary: First NYSE-listed public-issuer letter in the corpus. Bausch Health Companies (NYSE:BHC) supports the flexibility goal but argues the proposal as drafted will not deliver meaningful cost savings — investor expectations, lender requirements, Board oversight, and peer comparability will keep quarterly close cycles intact. Calls for extended filing deadlines + streamlined reporting (European-style) as the real reform. First substantive ICs invocation in the corpus.

name: Gavin Barrett-Hayes
rationale_evidence: FR: 'allow greater amounts of insider trading'. MF: 'make accurately valuing companies harder' — explicit price-discovery framing.
summary: Three-mechanism Oppose in 23 words: bad for investors, more insider trading, harder valuation.

name: GEM
rationale_evidence: FR: 'The Mice play when the Cat is away' + 'hide what they are Doing' — fraud/manipulation framing on reduced disclosure. IP (Primary/Inclusive): 'Without Responsibility... bailing them out' invokes investor-protection mandate.
summary: Short Oppose with mice-and-cat metaphor. Reduced disclosure invites concealment.

name: Kenneth Fuller
rationale_evidence: MF: 'security prices adjust quickly to new information' + 'full and fair price discovery will be negatively impacted'. IA: 'benefit institutional investors at the expense of individual investors' — explicit. IP: 'mission of the SEC is to protect investors'. LE: invokes 'mission of the Commission' and lists the Section 3(f) and 23(a)(2) statutory factors verbatim ('protect investors; maintain fair, orderly, and efficient markets; and facilitate capital formation').
summary: Substantive retail Oppose grounded in efficient-markets framing and the SEC's statutory mandate. Unanimous 4-code letter.

name: Prakash Shah
rationale_evidence: IP: 'let public [access] company's quarterly report so [they] can make investment decision' — transparency framing despite broken syntax.
summary: Twelve-word Oppose. Public-access framing for investment decisions.

name: Jarek Erznoznik
rationale_evidence: FR: 'More opportunity for fraud and corruption'. IP: 'their numbers should be PUBLIC' (transparency). IA (P/I): 'public retail investors' framing.
summary: Retail Oppose. Fraud/corruption framing tied to less public oversight of public companies.

name: Olivia Gunter
rationale_evidence: Gestural opposition: 'A lot can go wrong... Companies should checked in more often'. No substantive mechanism invoked.
summary: Short retail Oppose. No substantive rationale.

name: Paul R. Dittrich
rationale_evidence: Eleven-word position statement, no mechanism.
summary: Eleven-word Oppose. No substantive rationale.

name: Russell Edward Watrous
rationale_evidence: Fourteen-word position statement, no mechanism.
summary: Fourteen-word Oppose. No substantive rationale.

name: Thomas Kelley
rationale_evidence: FR: 'ripe for allowing fraud to fester'. IA: 'everyday investors who have little insight'. IP (P/I): 'kneecapping American investors' invokes investor protection.
summary: Retail Oppose. Fraud-fester framing + retail-vs-insider asymmetry. Notes 'Corporations aren't even asking for this'.

name: Adam Clark Andrews
rationale_evidence: Three-word position: 'Keep quarterly reporting'.
summary: Three-word Oppose. No substantive rationale.

name: Andrew Wager
rationale_evidence: FR: 'people who seek to deceive or mislead shareholders'. IP (inclusive): 'Six months in the dark is unacceptable for investors' invokes transparency.
summary: Short retail Oppose. Deception/insider framing.

name: Ashr Khan
rationale_evidence: FR: 'encouraging more insider trading' — explicit insider-trading framing. IP (P/I): 'main window into the financial health' transparency framing.
summary: Short retail Oppose. Insider-trading framing.

name: Clay Tezel
rationale_evidence: Nine-word position: 'Publicly traded companies should have to report every quarter'.
summary: Nine-word Oppose. No substantive rationale.

name: Craig Cutler
rationale_evidence: FR: 'companies misrepresenting their financial performance... much easier to mislead'. CMP: 'much harder for an investor to compare companies with half the information'. CB: 'save money or remove pressure' — engages and rebuts the cost-burden rationale. IP: 'undeserving gift to corporations at investor expense'.
summary: Retail Oppose with 4-code argument. Notable for explicit CMP invocation: 'much harder for an investor to compare companies with half the information'.

name: Douglas Duncan
rationale_evidence: FR: 'We have enough corruption as it is'.
summary: Twenty-one word Oppose. Anti-corruption framing.

name: James Silva
rationale_evidence: FR: 'Bernie Madoff' + 'fraudulent and immoral reporting by banks' + 'Jeffrey Epstein... hidden from public view'. IA: 'individual American citizens and small investors' vs 'large businesses'. IP: 'strong transparency measures should be mandatory'.
summary: Substantive 235-word Oppose. Historical fraud anchors (Madoff, 2008, 2000, Epstein) used as cautionary evidence. Argues democratic-society framing for transparency primacy.

name: Jan Charvat
rationale_evidence: IA: 'average/small investors' vs 'corporations and the rich' — explicit retail-vs-institutional framing.
summary: Sixteen-word Oppose. Pure IA framing.

name: Jaret Holden
rationale_evidence: IP: 'Please consider not taking transparency away'. IA: 'retail investors... at a disadvantage'. RI (P/I): 'our window into making smart financial decisions and planning where to best allocate our money' — personal reliance framing.
summary: Forty-one word retail Oppose. Personal-reliance framing on quarterly cadence.

name: Jennifer Simpson
rationale_evidence: FR: 'insiders to quietly sell their shares... before the bad news tanks the stock' + 'Enron. WorldCom. Madoff. The run-up to 2008'. OP: '"Optional" is a trick' + 'What started as a choice quietly becomes the new normal' — explicit rebuttal of the OP rationale. EX: 'Once large companies start filing twice a year, smaller competitors face pressure to follow' — explicit spillover/externality mechanism. CB: 'supposed savings are a miniscule... Companies still have to keep their books. They still have to brief their boards' — engages and rebuts the CB rationale. IA: 'you, the person whose 401(k), IRA, or college fund holds the stock'. RI: '(or your ETF)' — explicit ETF-reliance language.
summary: Substantive 307-word retail Oppose. Four-point structured argument: six-month-blindness + fraud growth (Enron/WorldCom/Madoff/2008) + optional-is-a-trick (with EX spillover mechanism) + savings-are-miniscule. Closes with 'We the People in America, not We the Corporations'.

name: Mark Simpson
rationale_evidence: Near-identical template to #218 Jennifer Simpson. Same 6-code rationale set. Wording variant: 'supposed savings are a nothing burger' instead of 'miniscule'. Signs as 'Mark Simpson, Arcadia, CA'.
summary: Substantive 305-word retail Oppose. NEAR-IDENTICAL TEMPLATE to #218 Jennifer Simpson — same 4-point structured argument with one-word substitution ('miniscule' → 'nothing burger'). Looks like coordinated household or template advocacy.

name: Michelle Phelps
