rationale_evidence: IA (P/I): 'regular American investors' — gestural retail framing. Literalist skips; no explicit retail-vs-institutional contrast.
summary: Short retail Oppose. Gestural 'regular American investors' framing.

name: Neal Cohen
rationale_evidence: FR: 'make insider trading and fraud easier'. AL: 'Make reporting monthly' — maximalist alternative cadence proposal.
summary: Short Oppose with monthly-reporting maximalist alternative (AL). Central thrust is Oppose despite the AL invocation.

name: Philip-Michael Enix
rationale_evidence: IP: 'reduces transparency'. IA: 'individual or retail investors who do not have access to the same tools or resources as institutional investors' — textbook IA framing. OP: 'I am against allowing any opt-in to an alternative reporting' — explicit rebuttal of optionality. ST: 'Companies should be focused on long term strategy regardless'.
summary: Four-code retail Oppose. Textbook IA framing + explicit OP rebuttal + short-termism counter.

name: Sandra K.
rationale_evidence: IA: 'Retail investors are already at a huge disadvantage compared to institutional investors' — textbook IA framing.
summary: Short retail Oppose. Explicit retail-vs-institutional IA framing.

name: William Bishop
rationale_evidence: IP: 'reduces transparency'. IA (P/I): 'hurts individual investors' framing.
summary: Short retail Oppose. Transparency + individual-investor framing.

name: William Keisler
rationale_evidence: IP: 'reduce corporate transparency' + 'erode investor confidence'. CB: 'marginal savings companies may find from this proposed change' — engages and rebuts the CB rationale.
summary: 55-word retail Oppose. Transparency framing + engages and rebuts the cost-savings rationale ('marginal savings').

name: Anonymous
rationale_evidence: Ten-word position statement: 'Please keep the quarterly reporting as it is now!'.
summary: Ten-word retail Oppose. No substantive rationale.

name: Barbara Myers
rationale_evidence: Five-word position: 'This is really bad idea!'.
summary: Five-word retail Oppose. No substantive rationale.

name: Daniel Platt
rationale_evidence: RI (P/I): 'My life savings are tied up in investments & I base my portfolio partly on those quarterly reports' — personal-reliance framing. Literalist needs institutional reliance (analyst/ETF/credit) which is not invoked here.
summary: Retired AT&T employee writing in personal-investor register. Entity disagreement: Primary reads Individual on substance, Self-described and Letterhead read Issuer-former on the role-field credential. Majority Issuer-former.

name: Donnie Eckhoff
summary: OFF-TOPIC: letter reads 'Thank you for all the education I've received about crypto and Bitcoin and the rest very interesting'. Does not engage S7-2026-15. Looks like the commenter clicked the wrong form or filed a misdirected thank-you note. Excluded from public site, regression, and rater statistics.

name: Gregory Valentine
rationale_evidence: CB: 'I have never worked for a company where providing this information to investors was a burden' — engages and rebuts CB. IP: 'regular quarterly reports... easily accessible'. IA: 'no matter the size of the investment'. RI: 'I have personally used this information in the past to decide whether or not to buy, sell, or hold'.
summary: Substantive 100-word retail Oppose. Rebuts the CB rationale on first-hand grounds ('never worked for a company where providing this information was a burden').

name: Justin Schultz
rationale_evidence: FR: 'hide all their shady business dealings'. IA: 'lower to middle classes of American people'. IP: government oversight to 'protect' the public.
summary: Populist 60-word Oppose. Anti-corporate-protection framing.

name: K. Webster
rationale_evidence: FR: 'hide bad news, stock buybacks and more from investors and analysts'. IA: 'Public means transparency for everyone, not just insiders'. IP: 'Public means transparency'.
summary: 39-word Oppose. Three-mechanism critique: fraud-window, retail-vs-insider, transparency.

name: Kevin Montalti
rationale_evidence: AU: 'As an external auditor, the quarterly reporting requirement acts as a disruption to our overall audit procedures' + 'More time to focus on the audit will allow for better audit quality'. CB: 'gives back audit teams two additional months'. Pro-proposal AU invocation — unique direction in the corpus.
summary: FIRST pro-proposal Support letter from an external auditor (CPA). Frames the proposal as freeing up audit-team bandwidth for year-end work, improving audit quality. Inverts the typical anti-proposal CPA framing seen elsewhere in the corpus.

name: Kevin W. Alexander
rationale_evidence: US: 'US markets are far and away the largest, most liquid and most attractive in the world' + 'Other lower tier markets (London, Hong Kong, etc.) will certainly gain'. ICc: 'Look at European markets for the results that will follow'. MF: 'weaker, smaller and less liquid'. CB: 'time commitment of quarterly reporting for public companies is minimal'. OP: 'private tech companies are choosing to stay private longer' — engages the proposal's stated optionality goal. IA (P/I): 'Public investors absolutely need the information'. IP (P/I): 'make informed investment decisions'.
summary: Substantive 279-word retail Oppose grounded in US-capital-market-leadership framing. Argues US markets' dominance depends on quarterly reporting; semiannual will hand share to London / Hong Kong. Engages and rebuts the proposal's policy theory.

name: Larry Overman
rationale_evidence: CB: 'We have internet, should be quicker not longer' — rebuts the cost-burden premise of the proposal.
summary: Fourteen-word Oppose engaging the CB rationale via 'modern technology' rebuttal.

name: Margaret Rosenfeld
role: Margaret Rosenfeld, Founder, Zukunft Advisory, LLC (27-year corporate/securities lawyer; prior partner roles at Freshfields Bruckhaus Deringer, Paul Weiss Rifkind Wharton & Garrison, K&L Gates, Smith Anderson; prior senior in-house CLO roles)
rationale_evidence: Stance: 'I do not oppose the proposal' + 'The Commission's policy rationale...is defensible'. Three specific recommendations + four-component framework = clear Conditional. AL: four-component framework for what semiannual filers should be expected to implement. AU: extended PCAOB AS 4105 / AU-C 930 / AICPA interim-review analysis. CB: 'cost-reduction objective is real' + 'cost savings...are at least partly illusory' if auditor review continues. CC: 'Lenders incorporate financial covenant certification and reporting requirements into credit agreements that often track the Form 10-Q schedule'. FR (L/I): Rule 10b-5 securities class action analysis + Rule 10b5-1 trading-window discussion. IP: 'investor protection objectives the Commission has articulated as central to its mission'. LE: Sections 302 + 906 SOX, Item 307 + 308 Regulation S-K, Section 18 filed status, Section 11, PCAOB AS 4105, AU-C 930, Rule 10b-5, Regulation FD, Rule 10b5-1. MF (P/I): capital-markets-access and 135-day staleness rule. OP: 'regulatory flexibility' + 'preserves the existing quarterly framework as the default'. OV (P/I): 'preserves the existing quarterly framework as the default for issuers for whom it remains appropriate'. RI: 'Analyst coverage of U.S. public companies is built around quarterly models. Institutional investors maintain valuation frameworks that require quarterly revenue, earnings, and guidance updates. Lenders incorporate...'
summary: Nine-page legal memo from Margaret Rosenfeld (Founder, Zukunft Advisory, LLC), a 27-year corporate/securities lawyer with partner experience at Freshfields, Paul Weiss, K&L Gates, Smith Anderson, and senior in-house CLO roles. Conditional: does not oppose but proposes a four-component operational framework + three specific recommendations to address the 'disclosure liability gap' the proposal creates for issuers that elect semiannual and continue quarterly market communications (the substantial majority, in her view). 11 rationale codes — new corpus record. Substantial LE invocation: cites Sections 302/906 SOX, Items 307/308 Regulation S-K, Section 18, Section 11, PCAOB AS 4105, AU-C 930, Rule 10b-5, Reg FD, Rule 10b5-1. Notable framing: bifurcation between large-cap quarterly filers (overwhelmingly non-electing) and smaller semiannual electors, and the 'on-ramp question' for emerging issuers — neither maps cleanly to existing codes.

rfc_questions.note: Securities lawyer (Rosenfeld). Section 'Responses to specific requests for comment' engages numbered RFCs by topic without citing numbers: voluntary quarterly disclosure (18/22), internal controls and review (23/26), insider trading (31/32), capital-markets access (37/57).

name: Sami Aji
rationale_evidence: FR: 'help fraud and insider trading grow because there is more time to hide things'.
summary: Software Engineer writing in retail-investor register. Entity disagreement: Primary reads Individual on substance, Self-described / Letterhead read Industry practitioner on the role-field credential. Majority Industry practitioner.

name: Shirley Williams
rationale_evidence: AL: 'first report of the year for four months... after 7 months instead of semi-annual... another report after 10 months' — explicit every-4-months alternative cadence (3 reports/year). AU: 'extra month to recover from annual and year end audits' — engages auditor-burden framing.
summary: Conditional with explicit alternative cadence proposal (every 4 months, 3 reports/year) framed around audit-team workload smoothing. Unusual AL invocation — most AL letters propose scaled relief or longer 10-S deadlines; Williams proposes a different middle-ground cadence.

name: William Irvine
rationale_evidence: FR: 'too much potential for abuse'. IP: 'accountability and transparency to shareholders'.
summary: 29-word retail Oppose. Accountability-and-transparency framing + abuse potential.

name: A. D.
rationale_evidence: Six-word position: 'SEC should stick to quarterly reporting'.
summary: Six-word Oppose. No substantive rationale.

name: Alan LePage
rationale_evidence: IA: 'As a small investor... Anything less leaves me disadvantaged' + 'Market influencers and trading algorithms leave me in a situation'. RI (P/I): 'Quarterly reports are needed for me to keep up to date'.
summary: Small-investor Oppose. Algorithmic-trading-disadvantage framing.

name: Alexander
rationale_evidence: IP: 'keep the companies in dark not creating transparency'.
summary: 19-word Oppose on transparency grounds.

name: Beth Carnegie-Kent
rationale_evidence: FR + IA: 'another way to keep insider advantages to insiders'.
summary: Twelve-word Oppose. Insider-advantage framing.

name: Charles Labianco
rationale_evidence: FR: 'gov insiders would have more of an advantage... become more corrupted'.
summary: 40-word Oppose. Insider corruption framing.

name: Denise Orton
rationale_evidence: FR: extensive Enron retelling — 'Kenneth Lay... knew they company was going bust, all given the heads up to sell off all their soon to be totally worthless stocks' + 'corruption to flourish'. IA: 'single mother working diligently... WE THE CONTRACTORS hired by Enron' vs 'top tier employees, politicians in the know, as well as other elite billionaires'. RI (P/I): personal-financial-reliance via lost commissions and stolen 401k framing.
summary: 263-word retail Oppose anchored in personal Enron experience. First-person account of losing $41,743 in unpaid Enron commissions and watching 401(k)s emptied while insiders sold. Framed as evidence that reduced reporting frequency enables exactly this fraud pattern.

name: Frederick Brown
rationale_evidence: FR: 'Fraud can easily be hidden from shareholders for a long time' + 'The real beneficiaries... are going to be the fraudsters'.
summary: 53-word retail Oppose. Fraud-hidden-from-shareholders framing.

rationale_evidence: FR: 'Keep the quarterly report to avoid fraud'.
summary: Seven-word Oppose. Pure FR framing.

name: Jim Perkins
rationale_evidence: CB: 'savings are actually minimal' — rebuts CB rationale. IA: 'only benefit the company and the executives, not the shareholders or the public'. IP: 'a matter of transparency'.
summary: 55-word retail Oppose. Rebuts the cost-savings rationale on distributional grounds.

name: John Koloen
rationale_evidence: IA: 'retail investors such as myself would have access to less timely information'. IP: 'verify the information via quarterly financial statements'. OP: 'naive to believe that making this policy "optional" is nothing more than a smokescreen' — explicit OP rebuttal. CB: 'notion that it will generate significant savings for the corporation' — CB rebuttal. FR (P/I): 'close the information window on investors'. RI (P/I): 'no notion of whether to buy or sell' — personal reliance.
summary: 164-word retail Oppose. Explicit OP rebuttal ('optional is a smokescreen') + CB rebuttal in one letter — full structural critique of the proposal's stated benefits.

name: John Ranyard
rationale_evidence: FR: 'permission to grift'. IA: 'big business operate in the dark, hiding from the small investors'.
summary: 31-word retail Oppose. Anti-deregulation framing.

name: John Toupalik
rationale_evidence: AL: 'Quarterly filing should be monthly' — maximalist alternative. FR: 'more chance for manipulation' + 'Less chance of fraud and deception'. IA: 'Us little guys are getting smoked now'. CB: 'There truly is little savings by going every six months' + 'at very little cost' — CB rebuttal.
summary: 83-word Oppose with maximalist AL alternative (monthly). Engages CB and FR rationales explicitly.

name: Kirby Wells
rationale_evidence: IP: 'reduces transparency'. IA (P/I): 'competitive advantage for large public companies' (small-business vs large-issuer framing). LE (P/I): 'contrary to the purpose of the SEC' — gestural statutory-mission framing.
summary: Small-business owner (Southwest Missouri OPE LLC) Oppose. Competitive-advantage-for-large-companies framing + SEC-mission framing.

name: Anthony Langenstein
rationale_evidence: IA: 'reducing reporting frequency directly increases information asymmetry'. MF: 'degrade market efficiency, elevate cost of capital'; 'continuous, incremental price discovery'; 'idiosyncratic volatility and severe price gaps'. CMP: 'diminishes comparability across peers... cognitive processing burden on market analysts'. OP: Form 8-K rebuttal — 'structurally flawed', 'inadequate'. US: 'U.S. capital markets are globally recognized as the gold standard'; foreign direct investment framing. LE: 'Exchange Act Rules 13a-13 and 15d-13'. IP: 'individual retail investors do not' have proprietary data streams. EX: 'negative externalities imposed on capital allocation efficiency, market transparency, and global competitiveness'.
summary: Substantive Oppose PDF from a self-described Individual Investor. 6-vector technical argument (US gold standard, operational risks, IA, price discovery, Form 8-K critique, agency costs). Structurally near-identical template to #259 Rei Takashi (coordinated advocacy). 8 rationale codes — among the most rationale-rich letters in the corpus.

name: Brad Deaner
rationale_evidence: IP: 'wealth gap... hurts normal Americans while helping businesses and private investors' — distributional investor-protection framing.
summary: One-line Oppose. Wealth-gap framing.

name: Jermiah Dockery
rationale_evidence: IA: 'retail investor are going to be the last to know about a company if this law pass' — retail-vs-institutional information-timing framing.
summary: One-line Oppose. Retail-vs-institutional information-asymmetry framing.

name: Kenneth Fahndrick
rationale_evidence: IP: 'abandoning investors (the OWNERS) needs', 'Corporate Non-transparency Commission' sarcasm. IA: 'needs for CURRENT, timely, and accurate corporate information'.
summary: Sarcastic emphatic Oppose. Investor-as-owner framing plus appeal to modern instant-information context.

name: Lance Graven
rationale_evidence: IA: 'Increased Information Asymmetry' section header; 'retail investors at a severe disadvantage'. IP: same section, retail-investor focus. MF: 'Heightened Market Volatility'; 'Reduced Management Accountability'. OP: 'Ineffectiveness of Voluntary Updates' — Form 8-K critique. CMP: 'difficult for investors to accurately compare performance across companies or sectors'.
summary: Substantive 4-vector Oppose: information asymmetry, market volatility, management accountability, Form 8-K inadequacy. Closing appeal to SEC mission.

name: Owen Murphy
rationale_evidence: IP: 'cutting the window the general public has into Wall Street in half', '99% of Americans'. PP: 'Full blown corruption in broad daylight... hurtful to 99% of Americans.'
summary: Short profanity-laced Oppose from a Data Analyst writing in retail-public register. Entity contested: Primary reads personal voice as Individual; Self-described + Letterhead follow the role field. Substantive PP-watch invocation ('corruption in broad daylight').

name: Rei Takashi
rationale_evidence: Near-identical template to #253 Langenstein (minus US-gold-standard and operational-risk vectors). IA: 'reducing reporting frequency directly increases information asymmetry'. MF: 'degrade market efficiency, elevate cost of capital'; 'idiosyncratic volatility'. CMP: 'diminishes comparability across peers'. OP: Form 8-K rebuttal. LE: 'Exchange Act Rules 13a-13 and 15d-13'. IP: retail investors disadvantaged. EX: 'negative externalities imposed on capital allocation efficiency and market transparency'.
summary: Substantive 4-vector technical Oppose with explicit information-asymmetry, price-discovery, Form 8-K, and agency-cost framing. Near-identical structural template to #253 Anthony Langenstein — coordinated advocacy pattern, similar to Simpson pair at #218/#219.

name: Richard Norelli
rationale_evidence: IP: 'small guy', 'retirement funds', 'I rely on the fairness of the SEC'. FR: 'corporate manipulation and privileged executives'. CB: 'does very little to reduce costs' — direct CB rebuttal. PP: 'this rule revision reflects corporate/government cronyism... I don't have powerful attorneys or lobbyists at my disposal.'
summary: Retiree Oppose with substantive 3-code engagement: investor protection, fraud/manipulation, CB rebuttal. Substantive PP-watch invocation ('corporate/government cronyism').

name: Robin Moylan
rationale_evidence: NR: no substantive rationale; pure venting. PP: 'If the sec attempts to do this, it proves how corrupt this country really is!'
summary: Short Oppose, no substantive rationale. Venting register with 'corrupt this country' framing (weak PP-watch).

name: Tyler Dirks
rationale_evidence: IP: 'As a retail investor, quarterly earnings are my only real data point'. IA: same plus 'asks for more data, not less' — retail data-access framing.
summary: Retail-investor Oppose. AI-boom volatility framing argues for more data, not less.

name: Scott Parker
rationale_evidence: IA: 'information flowing to all investors in a timely manner'. CB: 'actual savings... are modest legal and accounting fees' — direct minimization. IP: 'cost of worse information falls on the investors'.
summary: Brief Oppose. Quarterly preserves timely information flow (IA); argues cost-savings claim is overstated because internal recordkeeping and board reporting remain (CB); distributional framing — savings flow to companies and executives, information costs fall on investors (IP). Backfill from 2026-05-15 — Action skipped twice via CDN staleness on page 2.

name: Andrew Dempsey
rationale_evidence: IP: 'Investors deserve more transparency, not less'. IA: 'allows investors to monitor patterns and keep a pulse on how the company is performing'. MF: 'share price volatility on announcement days' — direct jump-volatility framing.
summary: Brief Oppose. Three-code engagement: transparency / investor protection, monitoring access, and announcement-day jump volatility.

name: Anonymous
rationale_evidence: NR: 'Reporting every 6 months is insane.' Generic anti-corporate venting; 'accountability and controls' is too thin to count as substantive IP engagement.
summary: Two-line Oppose with no substantive argument engaged.

name: Benjamin Wynne
rationale_evidence: IA: 'How's an investor supposed to make good decisions if they don't know how their investment is doing?' IP: 'Corporations shouldn't be able to hide how they're doing'; investor decision-making focus.
summary: Brief Oppose. Investor-decision-quality framing plus anti-corporate-opacity framing.

name: Michael S. Cook
rationale_evidence: IP: 'maintain an informed investor community'. FR: 'reduce the potential for securities fraud' — direct fraud framing.
summary: Two-sentence Oppose. Informed-investor framing plus explicit securities-fraud invocation.

name: Robert Dunning
rationale_evidence: FR: 'potential for inside dealing will substantially increase'; 'potential for abuse, subsequent turmoil and scandal'. IA: 'smaller investors will be making decisions with much less information'. IP: 'Fair market trading will be severely harmed as smaller investors will be making decisions with much less information'.
summary: Substantive 3-code Oppose. Insider-dealing risk, smaller-investor information disadvantage, fair-market-trading framing.

name: William T. McKinzie
rationale_evidence: ST: 'Semi-annual reporting is adequate for the long-term investor' — short-termism framing. ICs: 'data from foreign markets supports this assertion' — international evidence supportive read. CB: 'reduce the burden of being a public company with the hoped for salutary effect of increasing the number of public companies available'.
summary: Brief Support. Three-code endorsement: long-term-investor adequacy (ST), foreign-markets supportive evidence (ICs), and reduced-burden framing tied to increasing the number of public companies (CB). Ninth Support letter in the corpus.

name: Walter
rationale_evidence: IP: 'greater performance transparency', 'investment democratization', 'Hiding information feels very third world'. MF: 'efficient markets, price stability'. AL: alternative-regime proposal — 'live reporting information daily', 'Loosen the legal requirements and allow companies to publish easy to understand metrics'. CB: 'Companies already have live data reporting dashboards' — argues low marginal cost of more-frequent reporting. Skeptic flips to Conditional on the alternative-regime proposal; Primary and Literalist read Oppose (wrong-direction framing).
summary: Distinctive Oppose arguing the opposite of the proposal: technology should enable live/daily reporting, not less-frequent. Pro-transparency, pro-efficiency framing with a live-reporting alternative regime and a low-marginal-cost (CB-rebuttal) argument.

name: Thomas Bourveau
role: Professor of Accounting, Saïd Business School, University of Oxford
rationale_evidence: IA: engages and rebuts the information-asymmetry market-failure argument (Grossman/Milgrom unraveling; own RFS 2025 streetcar paper, 72%->95% voluntary disclosure). EX: engages and rebuts the externality market-failure argument (Breuer JAR 2021, European private firms). CB: compliance/preparation costs, higher for smaller high-growth issuers; modest aggregate savings. IP: 'must balance investor protection with the goal of facilitating capital formation'; 'does not necessarily mean diminished investor protection'. AL: hybrid reporters (voluntary Q1/Q3 info); incorporate reporting frequency into risk-based oversight framework. OP: central theme — 'potential benefits of a flexible reporting regime', 'allow issuers to reveal their preferences'. ICs: European (Breuer) and Chinese comply-or-explain (Bourveau et al JAE 2025) international evidence, supportive direction. Stance: Primary=Support (dominant pro-flexibility position); Literalist=Conditional (no explicit endorsement verb); Skeptic=Conditional (risk-based-oversight refinement is a qualification) -> majority Conditional. Classified by the 3-rater algorithm without human override per project methodology.
summary: Substantive academic letter from an Oxford accounting professor. Argues the two market-failure justifications for mandatory quarterly disclosure (information asymmetry, externalities) are empirically weak, citing his own RFS 2025 streetcar-industry paper, Breuer JAR 2021, and a Chinese comply-or-explain study. Concludes the optional regime has benefits (revealing issuer preferences, capital formation for small high-growth firms) and that reduced frequency need not diminish investor protection if the SEC folds reporting-frequency choice into risk-based oversight. Algorithm majority Conditional (Primary Support / Literalist + Skeptic Conditional). Strong OP and EX invocations; ICs in the supportive direction.

name: Susanna Maria Harper
rationale_evidence: FR: 'Past bad acts would have taken much, much longer to discover and prosecute'; Enron and 2008 housing-bubble fraud-discovery anchors. IP: 'an attempt to hide activities from the public's eye'.
summary: Oppose with historical-fraud anchors (Enron, 2008 housing bubble) arguing semiannual would have delayed discovery and prosecution of bad acts. Public-transparency framing.

name: Theresa winbush
rationale_evidence: NR: one-line opposition ('Stop letting big companies of the hook... against this') with no substantive argument engaged.
summary: One-line Oppose, no substantive rationale.

name: Zachary Luzier
rationale_evidence: IP: 'must keep Quarterly Reports available for traders'. IA: access-equity framing — 'We have daily reports available to our State and Federal Representatives, we MUST keep this available at the very least' (representatives get daily; investors should keep at least quarterly).
summary: Brief Oppose. Argues traders must keep quarterly reports, drawing an access-equity comparison to the daily reports available to government representatives.

name: Chris Kearns
role: Self-directed retail investor and software developer (RigidBody Tech LLC)
rationale_evidence: IA: 'Institutional Information Asymmetry' — institutions proxy company health via satellite imagery/credit-card scrapes/supply-chain tracking; retail relies on SEC filings as the only 'level playing field'. IP: 'unfair advantage to institutions and hedge funds', 'American retail investor'. CB: 'The Cost Savings Fallacy' — same accounting systems, audit teams, compliance staff regardless of frequency; savings negligible. MF: 'The Risk of Information Shocks' — 180-day windows cause destabilizing gap-downs/gap-ups (jump volatility).
summary: Substantive 4-point Oppose from a self-directed retail investor / software developer (RigidBody Tech LLC). Institutional information asymmetry (alternative-data proxying), cost-savings-fallacy rebuttal, and information-shock/jump-volatility framing. Entity contested: Primary+Self-described read the retail-investor speaking register (Individual); Letterhead follows the LLC/technologist credential.

name: Bonnie Sears
rationale_evidence: Inclusive reads 'No changes to government oversight. Checks and balances are essential.' as a one-line oversight/protection gesture (IP); Primary/Literalist treat it as a bare position statement (NR).
summary: Ten-word civic Oppose: 'No changes to government oversight. Checks and balances are essential.' No engagement with the rule's specifics.

name: David N. Abernathy
rationale_evidence: IP: 'Do not undermine the public's access to information', 'Do not put investors in the dark!'. Inclusive adds IA on 'Investors need as much information as possible' (broad access framing).
summary: 22-word retail Oppose. Investor-information-access framing: 'Do not put investors in the dark.'

name: Fred Scialabba
rationale_evidence: Bare position: 'I would like to see reporting on a quarterly basis.' No substantive argument.
summary: Ten-word retail Oppose stating a preference for quarterly reporting. No rationale.

name: James Parker
rationale_evidence: IA: 'structural gap between institutional and retail investors'; small investors 'locked out of the information loop' while large funds gain private insights. FR: management gets 'an unacceptable window to delay the disclosure of negative material news'; six-month MNPI 'blackout' with private 'color' to favored parties. MF: 'extreme price volatility'; options 'pricing and liquidity are deeply tied to the regular arrival of quarterly financial data'. IP: 'prioritizes corporate convenience over investor protection... erodes transparency'. Inclusive adds CB ('corporate convenience'/compliance costs) and RI (options-market reliance on quarterly cadence).
summary: Substantive 410-word retail Oppose (PDF). Four points: institutional-vs-retail information asymmetry, delayed-bad-news / six-month MNPI blackout, options-market liquidity dependence on quarterly data, and corporate-convenience-over-investor-protection. Signs from Charlotte, NC with no institutional affiliation.

name: Shawn Lyon
rationale_evidence: MF: 'Quarterly reports create volume trading most. This creates volatility and confidence to invest in dividend based stocks.' Trading-volume / volatility framing. Skeptic flips Conditional on the muddled, non-one-directional prose.
summary: 34-word retail letter valuing quarterly reports for trading volume and market dynamics. Muddled prose; Skeptic flips Conditional, majority Oppose.

name: Daniel Flatley
role: Retired Managing Director DLJ and Credit Suisse, Retired Partner Masthead Investment Partners, Retired Chairman, Lumeta Holdings
rationale_evidence: AL: 'optional, significantly abbreviated, interim monthly reports' — alternative cadence. CB: 'without overburdening reporting companies'. IP (P/Inc): 'investors should have as much information as is reasonably possible'. Inclusive adds LE ('Congressionally approved shareholder strike suit immunization') and OP ('optional' reports). Skeptic flips Conditional on the alternative proposal; majority Oppose.
summary: Retired investment-bank MD (DLJ/Credit Suisse), investment partner (Masthead), and corporate chairman (Lumeta). 'Absolutely against' the proposal but proposes optional abbreviated monthly reports plus shareholder strike-suit immunization. Entity contested: Primary reads Individual on the personal investor register, Self-described/Letterhead read Investment professional on the credentials.

name: Dr. Fahim Mazumdar
rationale_evidence: FR: Certified Fraud Examiner; 'detect... fraudulent activity in a timely manner', 'conceal adverse financial conditions'. IA: 'heighten information asymmetry' (explicit). IP: 'undermining investor protection, market transparency', 'protect investors'. MF: 'fair, efficient markets', 'capital market integrity', 'capital allocation decisions based on current... information'. Inclusive adds AU (misstatement detection), CB ('not an administrative burden' rebuttal), US ('U.S. disclosure regime', 'step backward for U.S. capital markets').
summary: 283-word Oppose from a Professor of Accounting (Yavapai College) and Certified Fraud Examiner. Fraud/governance framing: semiannual reporting expands informational blind spots, weakens fraud deterrence, and is incompatible with the SEC's investor-protection mandate. The 7th Academic researcher in the corpus.

name: Josh Miller
rationale_evidence: RI (P/Inc): 'less accurate information on companies that I have invested my money in' — personal investor reliance (same pattern as #228 Platt, #230 Valentine; Literalist drops it for lack of institutional-reliance surface). CB: 'A few extra dollars for the C level' — rebuts the cost-savings premise. Inclusive adds IP on the distributional/'fellow citizens' framing.
summary: 55-word retail Oppose. Personal-reliance argument plus a cost-savings rebuttal: the only beneficiary is 'a few extra dollars for the C level.'

name: Russ Nickel
rationale_evidence: Four-word position: 'Please keep quarterly reporting.' No substantive argument.
summary: Four-word retail Oppose: 'Please keep quarterly reporting.'

name: William Martin
rationale_evidence: MF: 'essential for efficient markets'. IP: 'transparency'. AU: 'accounting controls'. Unanimous three-code letter.
summary: 20-word retail Oppose. Three mechanisms in one sentence: efficient markets (MF), transparency (IP), accounting controls (AU).

name: Dan Jamieson
rationale_evidence: RI: 'I am a retired investor and rely on periodic reports to stay abreast of company financial reports'; 'Many market participants rely on 10Qs as well'.
summary: 40-word Oppose from a retired retail investor. Reliance on periodic reports plus broader market-participant reliance on 10-Qs.

name: Michael Castleberry
role: Former Chief Accounting Officer; active investor
rationale_evidence: AL: 'the traditional 10-Q could be greatly streamlined as follows' — detailed Form 10-Q content-streamlining proposal (eliminate Significant Accounting Policy boilerplate, Fair Value boilerplate, refer to most recent 10-K for Commitments/Contingencies, drop Controls note if unchanged). CB: streamlining frames burden reduction. Stance Conditional: prefers quarterly but proposes a modified-quarterly regime.
summary: Active investor and former Chief Accounting Officer. Conditional: prefers companies keep quarterly reporting but proposes a detailed Form 10-Q content-streamlining alternative (eliminate boilerplate, cross-reference the 10-K). Entity contested: Self-described reads 'active investor' (Individual); Primary and Letterhead read the substantive former-CAO voice (Issuer/Corporate - former).

name: Mike Scherer
rationale_evidence: IA (P/Inc): 'Keep small guy having a chance' — small/retail-investor disadvantage framing. Literalist sees no surface code (colloquial slogan) -> NR.
summary: Six-word populist Oppose: 'Keep small guy having a chance.' Small-investor framing.

name: Marshal Shichtman
rationale_evidence: MF: markets 'more efficient, more orderly', real-time trading. US: 'gives foreign markets a competitive advantage', 'new U.S. issuers at a low point', 'American capital markets... reputation'. CB: 'skeptical that issuers will see an aggregate material savings'; auditors 'still going to go through the same amount of material'. AU: 'two less looks by not reviewing the Form 10-Qs allowing items to take root or fester'. CMP: 'uniformity creates an apples-to-apples effect... regularity'. CC: 'provisions in financing agreements for defaults based upon regular reporting', creditors getting info less regularly. LE: 'Rule 144 adequate public information', 'delayed listing... delisting determinations'. IP: 'builds investor confidence', 'consumer confidence'. Inclusive adds EX (collateral market-mechanics effects) and IA (asymmetric non-public information).
summary: Substantive 514-word Oppose from a securities attorney (Marshal Shichtman & Associates, P.C.; NY bar + England & Wales solicitor). Eight rationale codes: efficiency/orderly markets, US competitiveness, cost-savings skepticism, lost auditor reviews, apples-to-apples uniformity, debt-covenant/Rule-144/listing mechanics, and investor confidence. Skeptic flips Conditional on the 'not insurmountable' concession; majority Oppose. NOTE: PDF filed under docket S7-2026-18 URL but engages our Form 10-S proposal directly.

name: Alan Maloney
rationale_evidence: IP: 'lack of transparency is bad for investors AND bad for democracy'.
summary: One-line retail Oppose: keep quarterly; less transparency harms investors and democracy.
stance_evidence: 'Keep reporting quarterly - lack of transparency is bad for investors'

name: Amber Koel
rationale_evidence: IP: 'Nothing about this helps investors'; (Inc) FR/concealment: 'benefits those who seek to hide information'; IA: harms investors.
summary: Short retail Oppose: semiannual only benefits those who want to hide information from investors.
stance_evidence: 'Keep reporting quarterly, 6 months only benefits those who seek to hide information'

name: Dilan Paranagama
rationale_evidence: IA: 'increase information asymmetry', institutional/insiders vs ordinary investors; IP: 'reduce investor protection', market transparency; MF: 'volatility'; (Inc) CB: concedes 'reducing compliance burdens may be a valid goal'.
summary: Substantive retail Oppose: a six-month cycle widens the information gap between institutions/insiders and ordinary investors, weakening transparency, investor protection, and market efficiency; compliance relief should not come at that cost.
stance_evidence: 'I strongly oppose any proposal to reduce public company reporting from quarterly to semiannual'

name: Levonnie Hunter
rationale_evidence: None; non-substantive.
summary: Non-substantive comment ('I am very excited') with no position on reporting frequency. Filed under the new 'No position' category (held out of the public split, like Off-topic).
stance_evidence: 'I am very excited' — engages the topic but states no Support/Oppose/Conditional position.

name: Lewanda Walker
rationale_evidence: None; off-topic.
summary: Off-topic: appears to misunderstand the docket as a payment channel ('When is the money is coming in').
stance_evidence: 'When is the money is coming in' — not about the proposal.

name: Mayra Rodriguez Valladares
role: Managing Principal, MRV Associates (financial-regulation consultancy); congressional witness
rationale_evidence: SG: 'signaling trap' — opting into semiannual reads as a lower-transparency signal; stronger banks stay quarterly. MF: six months of deterioration lands as a single price shock. IA: information gap leaves analysts/lenders/investors unable to forecast accumulating risk. EX: SVB-style contagion to peer (esp. semiannual) banks. IP: 'information in banking is infrastructure'. (Inc adds RI: analyst/lender/credit-rating reliance.)
summary: Financial-regulation consultant (MRV Associates). Her SEC letter links to her Forbes article; this is a summary of that article. Bank-specific Oppose: banking is a confidence business, so opacity is dangerous. Mid-sized regional banks ($20-150B) are the cohort most likely to opt into semiannual and exactly where transparency is most fragile. Semiannual compresses gradual deterioration into a single shock, inviting SVB-style runs and contagion among peer (especially semiannual) banks. And self-selection becomes a signaling trap that stratifies banks by perceived transparency.
stance_evidence: Bank-focused Oppose (via her linked Forbes article): 'Anything that increases opacity in banking weakens the system'; urges stakeholders to comment. Skeptic flips Conditional on the 'depending on how it is adopted' qualifier.

name: Hayden Ramsey
rationale_evidence: CB: repetitive, resource-intensive quarterly burden on smaller issuers; OP: 'optional rather than mandatory', market-driven choice; ST: frees teams for 'long-term operational execution'; (Inc) OV/access: lowers barriers to going public; MF: 8-K continuous disclosure preserves material-event flow. Skeptic flips Conditional on the 'transparency remains critically important' concession.
summary: Substantive Support for the optional semiannual framework: cuts repetitive compliance burden on smaller issuers, frees management for long-term execution, lowers barriers to public-market entry, and preserves material-event disclosure via Form 8-K; optionality keeps the choice with companies and investors. Skeptic reads the transparency concession as Conditional; majority Support.
stance_evidence: 'I am writing in support of the Commission's proposed optional semiannual reporting framework'

name: John Horne
rationale_evidence: NR: bare position, no substantive argument.
summary: Two-word Oppose: 'Report quarterly.'
stance_evidence: 'Report quarterly'

name: John Woodriff
rationale_evidence: IP/transparency: 'Shareholders need to know what's happening'; (Inc) IA: shareholder information access.
summary: Short retail Oppose: keep quarterly so shareholders can see what is happening.
stance_evidence: 'Keep companies reporting quarterly. Shareholders need to know what's happening.'

name: M.A.
rationale_evidence: MF: 'more volatile'; FR: 'encourage even more bad acting'; IA: 'the retail investor suffers'; US: 'discourage foreign investment', harm to US markets' standing. (political aside not coded)
summary: Vehement retail Oppose: semiannual would raise volatility, enable bad actors, hurt retail investors, and discourage foreign investment in US markets.
stance_evidence: 'This is a HORRIBLE idea, will only make the market more volatile... while the retail investor suffers'

name: Michael F Holmes
rationale_evidence: IA: '98 percenters' vs '2% with inside access... unfair, unequal info'; FR: troubled companies get 'too much time to adjust the public picture'; MF: insiders 'jump the market'.
summary: Retail Oppose framed as the 98% vs the 2%: semiannual hands insiders unequal information to jump the market and gives troubled companies time to massage the picture.
stance_evidence: '6 month reporting... is a negative for us 98 percenters'

name: Taylor King
rationale_evidence: IP: 'accountability', 'transparency and clarity'; MF: 'increase uncertainty, speculation, and misinformation', 'volatile market'; IA: 'informed decisions instead of relying on rumors'.
summary: Substantive retail Oppose from a self-described 17-year-old: quarterly reports give accountability and clarity, curb speculation/misinformation, and have been an educational tool. FLAG: states he is 17 but no enrollment claim, so kept Individual (not Student).
stance_evidence: 'Keeping quarterly reporting is crucial in an already speculative and volatile market'

name: Todd Gloria
rationale_evidence: IP/transparency: 'do not delete investors ability to see or learn how investments are doing'; (Inc) IA: investor information access.
summary: Retail Oppose: keep quarterly so investors can see how their investments are doing; explicitly asks the SEC to vote the proposal down. (Name matches a public figure, but no indication in the letter; classified as Individual.)
stance_evidence: 'Please KEEP QUARTERLY reporting... do not change to semi-annual reporting. Please deny... S7-2026-15.'

name: Jeff Zehner
rationale_evidence: IP/transparency: 'Regular transparency'; US: 'why the US stock market is appealing to the world... investors the world over will look to other markets'; (Inc) MF: 'too difficult to analyze' (price-discovery/analyzability).
summary: Retail Oppose on a US-competitiveness frame: regular quarterly transparency is what makes US markets attractive globally, and loosening it would push investors toward other markets.
stance_evidence: 'Please do not allow this rule to be passed... Keep it transparent quarterly!'

name: Rebecca Lank
rationale_evidence: FR: 'a lot more corruptness'; IP/accountability: 'Filing 4 times a year keeps accountability much more contained'.
summary: Short retail Oppose: twice-a-year filing invites more corruption; quarterly filing keeps accountability contained.
stance_evidence: 'This would allow a lot more corruptness if they only have to file twice a year. Not a good idea.'

name: Robert Reed
rationale_evidence: FR: 'more fraud', 'bad actors will get away with their bad acts for longer'; IP/accountability: 'better for accountability to stakeholders'; (Inc) IA: 'investors holding the bag'.
summary: Retail Oppose: less frequent reporting means more fraud and lets bad actors run longer; quarterly reporting protects accountability and leaves investors less exposed.
stance_evidence: 'Less regulation will mean more fraud, plain and simple. Quarterly reporting is better for accountability to stakeholders.'

name: John DiCintio
rationale_evidence: FR: 'shifts more power to the corrupt'; IA: 'not good for the average person' (disadvantages the ordinary investor). Literalist surface-codes only the explicit 'corrupt' (FR).
summary: Brief retail Oppose: the change disadvantages the average person and hands more power to the corrupt.
stance_evidence: 'I feel this is not good for the average person. It shifts more power to the corrupt.'

name: Steve Carman
rationale_evidence: MF: 'The market is already manipulated and operating like the wild west', 'the casino'; FR: 'create more fraud'; IA: 'losses for the little guy'.
summary: Vehement retail Oppose: an already-manipulated market would see more fraud and losses for small investors under semiannual reporting; threatens to close his accounts if it passes.
stance_evidence: 'This is an incredibly bad idea... Lowering reporting to twice a year will just create more fraud and losses for the little guy.'

name: Anonymous
rationale_evidence: None; non-substantive.
summary: Single word ('Thanks'); no position on the proposal. Held out of the public split (No position).
stance_evidence: 'Thanks' — one word, no position on reporting frequency.

name: David
rationale_evidence: NR: bare opposition, no substantive argument.
summary: Bare retail Oppose: 'Terrible terrible idea. Don't do this.'
stance_evidence: 'Terrible terrible idea. Don't do this'

name: Eduardo Cardenas
rationale_evidence: FR: fraud, 'bad actors', criminals shielded from the law; (Inc) IA: the 'predator class' / rich escape while ordinary people are harmed. PP: 'those in charge of protecting us from bad actors, are receiving kickbacks from these same criminals while they point out new targets and shield them from the law.'
summary: Vehement, politically charged retail Oppose: argues lax compliance protects fraud and a 'predator class', alleging regulators are captured. On-topic core opposes weakening reporting; the rest is political venting (uncoded). FLAG: PP-watch (regulatory capture).
stance_evidence: Opposes laxer compliance: 'this compliance has become more lax even after all the fraud we have seen'.

name: James Prevo
rationale_evidence: IP: transparency/honesty, SEC mission to promote them; MF: information vacuum -> conjured, manipulable info -> instability; IA: investors 'abhor an information vacuum and tend to make up the information to which they lack access'; (Inc) CMP: value of a 'uniform set of reporting requirements'.
summary: Reasoned retail Oppose: uniform, timely quarterly disclosure serves the SEC's transparency mission and market stability; an information vacuum breeds inaccurate, manipulable, destabilizing guesswork. Firms wanting less transparency can stay private.
stance_evidence: 'making quarterly reports optional would be counter to this [SEC] mission'

name: Judith Stanton
rationale_evidence: IP: monitoring/transparency of her investments; IA: a retiree on a fixed income relies more heavily on periodic reports (less-resourced investor reliance).
summary: Retail Oppose from a fixed-income retiree: quarterly reporting lets her watch her investments closely.
stance_evidence: 'Keep the quarterly reporting... quarterly reporting allows me to keep a very close eye on my investments.'

name: William Oldright
rationale_evidence: NR: bare opposition; if anything wants more-frequent (not less) reporting, but no developed argument.
summary: Bare retail Oppose: leave it alone; quarterly is already infrequent enough.
stance_evidence: 'Leave well enough alone, it is bad enough that it is three months now.'

name: Alejandro
rationale_evidence: IA: retail investors 'blind for long stretches' while institutions/insiders keep timely info (paid research, alternative data, ad-hoc disclosures); FR: insiders 'trade ahead of bad news', 'fraud to grow undetected longer'; IP: 'weakening transparency would make accountability harder'; (Inc) CB: frames compliance savings as shifting to executives, not retail savers. PP: 'We do not need further corruption to infiltrate our institutions of justice.'
summary: Substantive Oppose from an attorney: semiannual reporting widens the retail-vs-institutional information gap, lets insiders trade ahead and fraud fester, and shifts modest compliance savings to executives at retail savers' expense. Entity Industry practitioner (signs 'Attorney').
stance_evidence: 'I oppose SEC Rule S7-2026-15... Please keep quarterly reporting.'

name: Alexandre J Vautrin
rationale_evidence: IA: quarterly reporting framed as 'democratizing' the market (leveling retail access). Literalist reads the rest as pure rhetoric (NR); (Inc) US: 'the greatest nation on earth'. Apocalyptic framing otherwise uncoded.
summary: Apocalyptic retail Oppose: removing quarterly reporting destroys a 'democratizing aspect of the free market' and hastens national decline. The substantive core is a market-access/leveling claim.
stance_evidence: 'If you do this, you will destroy a democratizing aspect of the free market.'

name: Anonymous
rationale_evidence: IP: quarterly filings as investor protections; (Inc) IA: 'for individual investors'.
summary: One-line retail Oppose: quarterly filings protect individual investors.
stance_evidence: 'Please do not make the mistake of removing quarterly filings and the protections they provide for individual investors.'

name: Arnold Bass
rationale_evidence: IP: transparency, accountability, oversight; IA: individual vs institutional investors with alternative data, quarterly disclosures 'level the playing field'; MF: 'market volatility driven by speculation rather than facts', longer uncertainty; (Inc) FR: 'problems to go unnoticed'; (Inc) CB: names the regulatory-burden tradeoff and rejects it.
summary: Reasoned retail Oppose: semiannual reporting widens information gaps, disadvantages individual investors against institutions with alternative data, invites speculation-driven volatility, and weakens accountability; the compliance-burden savings do not outweigh the loss of transparency.
stance_evidence: 'I am writing as an individual investor to express my strong opposition... such a change would meaningfully weaken market transparency.'

name: Bradley Lawson
rationale_evidence: IA: explicit retail information disadvantage that fewer filings would deepen.
summary: Brief retail Oppose: halving quarterly filings deepens the disadvantage retail investors already face.
stance_evidence: 'Cutting in half the number of financial quarterly filings... would put us at a greater disadvantage than we already experience.'

name: Cheryl Shaw
rationale_evidence: IP: private investors rely on disclosures to make informed decisions; IA: AI and algorithm-driven trading already reduce 'private, unsophisticated investors' ability to achieve profits; semiannual reporting would make intelligent retail decisions impossible.
summary: Retail Oppose: private, unsophisticated investors already face algorithmic-trading barriers; cutting to semiannual would make informed retail decisions impossible. In an age of AI the case is for more reporting, not less.
stance_evidence: 'I oppose semi-annual reporting. Quarterly reporting does not interfere with investment decisions...'

name: Chris Parisi
rationale_evidence: IP: audited, personally signed quarterly filings let him track earnings and spot souring investments; IA: 'Major investment firms can weather losses caused by gaps' but an individual investor needs the extra data; benefits 'the top 1%'. (Inc) AU: values the audited/signed nature.
summary: Retail Oppose: audited, personally signed quarterly filings let an independent investor catch deteriorating positions that large firms can ride out; the change benefits the top 1% and hurts individuals. Notes the SEC abandoned the same change in 2018-2019 for reasons that still hold.
stance_evidence: 'Please do not change SEC requirements that corporations file quarterly reports. The current scheme is extremely beneficial to independent investors like me.'

name: Christi Powell
role: CFP, IAR — Falcon Financial of Oklahoma, LLC
rationale_evidence: AU: opposes dropping audit certification and management legal jeopardy; IP: without them, reporting would be 'rife with obfuscations'. Skeptic reads the 'without audit certification' framing as a condition (Conditional). (Inc) MF: 'impossible for investment managers to accurately assess the company'.
summary: Investment-professional Oppose (CFP/IAR): semiannual reporting stripped of audit certification and management legal accountability would be rife with obfuscation and impossible to assess. The skeptic rater reads the audit-certification framing as conditional.
stance_evidence: 'I strongly oppose the idea of semi-annual reporting without requiring audit certification and the inclusion of legal jeopardy attached to management.'

name: Christopher Reynolds
rationale_evidence: NR: bare opposition; the aside that AI efficiency could support monthly reporting is not a taxonomy argument.
summary: Bare retail Oppose: keep quarterly, and AI efficiency could even support monthly.
stance_evidence: 'I oppose such proposal and vote to keep earning calls quarterly.'

name: Dan Gordner
rationale_evidence: IP: timeliness of disclosure for decision-making; (Inc) IA: a retiree relies more heavily on periodic reports.
summary: Retiree Oppose: timely information drives quick financial decisions, and six months is not timely.
stance_evidence: 'Being of retirement age, I need to make financial decisions quickly, based on timely information. 6 months is NOT timely.'

name: Daniel Krzysiak
rationale_evidence: NR: bare opposition in slang. Self-described rater tags the 'crypto guru' signoff as Industry practitioner / technologist; majority Individual.
summary: Bare retail Oppose in slang: six months is too long, keep quarterly. Signs 'crypto guru'.
stance_evidence: 'Bruh wtf every 6 months yo this can't be real cuh. Need my financial reports quarterly ya heard.'

name: David A Miller
rationale_evidence: IP: free, full, timely disclosure; (Inc) IA: relies on investing to support retirement without charity.
summary: Retail Oppose: free, full, timely disclosure supports a self-funded retirement and should not change.
stance_evidence: 'It is very important that the free, full and timely disclosure of information on companies be maintained. I support that the system not be changed.'

name: David Hayden
rationale_evidence: IP: relies on SEC filings for 'real data instead of the spin' of press releases; wants decades-old certified standards held. The technology-is-cheap point rebuts compliance burden but maps to no anti-code.
summary: Retail Oppose: cheaper technology argues for the same or more frequent reporting, not less; SEC filings give real data rather than corporate spin.
stance_evidence: 'As technology gets better and cheaper, there's no reason to permit less frequent financial reporting.'

name: Dene M Alden
rationale_evidence: IP: quarterly data shows company direction for income and growth, monitoring; IA: 'doesn't assist the day-by-day investor', the SEC is 'vital in securing data for the retail investor'; lowering standards does not help shareholders.
summary: Retail Oppose: quarterly data lets a fundamental retail investor track company direction; cutting frequency and lowering standards fails the day-by-day investor.
stance_evidence: 'As a fundamental retail investor, quarterly reporting is vital to my retirement.'

name: Diane Wheeler
rationale_evidence: IP: lost accountability leaves investors uninformed and at risk; (Inc) FR: companies 'hide' problems. The employee-vulnerability point has no taxonomy code.
summary: Retail Oppose: removing quarterly reporting lets companies hide behind lost accountability, leaving investors uninformed and employees, who learn employer health from financials, more vulnerable.
stance_evidence: 'Changing the rules to get rid of the quarterly reporting rule is a disastrous idea! Companies will hide behind the lack of accountability!'

name: Donna Wong
rationale_evidence: IP: 25-year investor relies on quarterly reports to track each company's growth.
summary: Retail Oppose: a long-term investor of 25-plus years relies on quarterly reports to track company growth.
stance_evidence: 'I oppose the proposal to stop the quarterly reports. I am an investor... I rely on the reports for information and investing strategy.'

name: Elke Doddridge
rationale_evidence: NR: bare request to keep quarterly reporting.
summary: Bare retail Oppose: keep three-month reporting.
stance_evidence: 'Please Keep 3 month reporting.'

name: Ellen Burgeson
rationale_evidence: AU: the 10-Q is reviewed by independent auditors and signed by CEO and CFO under legal accountability, while the 'voluntary corporate communications that would replace them carry none of that'; IP: needs current, certified information to make buy/sell decisions; (Inc) IA: 'small investor'.
summary: Investment-club Oppose: a 30-year club uses quarterly reports for buy/sell decisions; halving them removes independently audited, personally signed filings that voluntary communications cannot replace.
stance_evidence: 'By dropping our information down to twice a year our decision making would be severely affected... As a small investor, I request that you DO NOT approve this proposal.'

name: Gloria Gunn
rationale_evidence: IP: relies on quarterly filings across 50-plus holdings to hold/buy/sell; frequent reporting keeps numbers reliable and transparent; 'investor protection'. (Inc) MF: 'market integrity'; (Inc) IA: individual investor.
summary: Retail Oppose: an individual holding 50-plus companies relies on quarterly filings to monitor positions; reducing frequency undercuts reliability, investor protection, and market integrity.
stance_evidence: 'I am writing to express my strong opposition to the proposal of changing the 10-Q reporting requirement from a quarterly to a semi-annual basis.'

name: H Michael Bradley
rationale_evidence: IP: quarterly reports support timely ownership decisions.
summary: Brief retail Oppose: quarterly reports matter for timely decisions on holdings and prospects.
stance_evidence: 'I consider quarterly reports for the companies I own stock in or am considering owning as important in timely decisions.'

name: Jacques Laufer
rationale_evidence: NR: no substantive disclosure argument. PP: 'Another Trump directed cop-out to the rich and influential!'
summary: Political retail Oppose: dismisses the proposal as a 'Trump directed cop-out to the rich and influential'. No substantive disclosure argument; FLAG: PP-watch (capture sentiment).
stance_evidence: 'The is crazy proposal. Who do you think this would help? Another Trump directed cop-out to the rich and influential!'

name: Jacques Nguema
rationale_evidence: IP: transparency and accountability for regular analysis and financial decisions.
summary: Brief retail Oppose: keep quarterly reports for transparency and accountability.
stance_evidence: 'Please let's keep providing Quarterly reports for transparency and accountability.'

name: Jeff Gorman
rationale_evidence: IP: quarterly reports analyze investments and spot problems early; (Inc) ST: surface mention that management should focus on long-term company health rather than stock prices.
summary: Retail Oppose: quarterly reports are essential to analyze investments and spot problems; management should still prioritize long-term company health over stock prices.
stance_evidence: 'I think quarterly reports are essential for analyzing potential investments. They are also essential to spot problems with an investment...'

name: Jennifer Adair
rationale_evidence: IP: monitors financial health and emerging risks between annual reports; CMP: compares the current quarter's balance sheet, income, and cash-flow statements 'against the same period from the previous year to spot trends' (granularity/comparability across time).
summary: Retail Oppose: a long-term investor uses 10-Q filings to monitor risks and to compare each quarter against the prior-year quarter for trends; semiannual aggregation would erase that granularity.

name: Julia Hartfelder

rationale_evidence: IA: frames the change as favoring the wealthy over private investors. PP: 'Rich keep getting richer. What about a government for the common people and not the 1%?'
summary: Political retail Oppose: the change favors the rich over ordinary private investors. FLAG: PP-watch.
stance_evidence: 'Rich keep getting richer. What about a government for the common people and not the 1%.'

name: Matthew Namendorf
rationale_evidence: IP: frequency is part of accountability; FR: less frequent reporting increases risk and susceptibility to fraud.
summary: Retail Oppose: reporting frequency is part of accountability; reducing it increases susceptibility to fraud.

name: Ravi Shah
rationale_evidence: CMP: cites the release's own comparability concern about comparing quarterly vs semiannual filers; IA: institutions/insiders retain access while retail depends on filings; IP: timely, standardized, comparable information and liability discipline; (Inc) AL: targeted relief for smaller issuers instead of a broad option; (Inc) ST: distinguishes quarterly reporting from quarterly earnings guidance; (Inc) MF: standardized vs fragmented messaging.
summary: Comprehensive Oppose: semiannual reporting erodes comparability and standardization and widens the retail information gap; if relief is needed, target smaller issuers rather than weakening the quarterly cadence for all.
stance_evidence: 'I respectfully urge the Commission to retain mandatory quarterly reporting on Form 10-Q.'

name: Shane Short
rationale_evidence: FR: longer gaps give scam and fake companies more time to forge and avoid delisting.
summary: Retail Oppose: a longer cadence gives scam and fraudulent companies more time to forge results and dilute American investors.
stance_evidence: 'Twice a year instead of quarterly will make scam companies have plenty more time to forge.'

name: Steve Thurston
rationale_evidence: FR: semiannual reporting makes it easier to manipulate and hide issues and aids insider manipulation; IA: less accountability favors executives and insiders over investors; (Inc) AL: scale reporting up for larger companies and require monthly top-line revenue; (Inc) AU: as a former CPA, notes modern systems can soft-close books anytime, so the burden claim is overstated. Skeptic reads the scaling fallback as Conditional.
summary: Retired-CPA Oppose: semiannual reporting eases manipulation and insider gaming; modern systems make near-real-time reporting feasible, so larger companies should report more, not less.
stance_evidence: 'This is giving the fox the henhouse.'

name: Vikram Pillai
rationale_evidence: IP: transparency into company health for informed decisions.
summary: Brief retail Oppose: quarterly reporting preserves transparency into company health.
stance_evidence: 'Please keep the reporting requirements to be quarterly to keep the existing transparency into company health.'

name: W. Smith
rationale_evidence: FR: cites First Brands and its private creditors as a case where lack of transparency let fraud reach industry-threatening scale; (Inc) MF: systemic economic vulnerability.
summary: Retail Oppose: public disclosure keeps fraud from becoming systemic, as the First Brands collapse showed; reducing reporting creates an economy-wide vulnerability.
stance_evidence: 'Public disclosure prevents fraud from becoming systemic.'

name: Aaron
rationale_evidence: IP: relies on quarterly calls to decide hold/sell; a 6-month gap means learning of trouble too late; (Inc) IA: private investor.
summary: Retail Oppose: quarterly reporting lets a private investor catch a floundering stock before it is too late.
stance_evidence: 'A lot can happen in 6 months, and at that point by the time i find out a stock is starting to flounder it will likely be too late.'

name: Anonymous
rationale_evidence: IP: quarterly financial results are the key factor in buy/trim/eliminate decisions; a 6-month wait adds risk.
summary: Long-term investor Oppose (since 1997): quarterly results are the critical input to hold/trim/sell decisions, and a six-month wait adds unnecessary risk.
stance_evidence: 'The quarterly SEC filings currently required are critical to my success.'

name: Anonymous

name: Brad

name: Dave

summary: Reasoned retail Oppose: halving disclosure widens the institutional information advantage and lets governance and accounting problems stay hidden longer; accountability should rise with public ownership.

name: David Desnoyers

name: Jeff Dyke

name: Seth Adamd

name: Stephen MacMinn
rationale_evidence: IP: six months with no information is too long.

name: Anonymous

name: Brian Zee

PP: 'Reducing the reporting requirements is reckless at best, and flat out corrupt at worst... stand up for the American people instead of paving the way for financial malfeasance.'
summary: Retail Oppose: reducing reporting paves the way for financial malfeasance at scale. FLAG: PP-watch.
stance_evidence: 'Reducing the reporting requirements is reckless at best, and flat out corrupt at worst.'

name: Cherian

name: Corporate Governance Class
rationale_evidence: IP: mandatory reporting engenders trust (board-oversight assurance, negative assurance of no material change avoids an information vacuum); AU: verification processes inspire confidence in interim reports; (Inc) CB: cost savings may be overstated since boards/auditors must engage regularly regardless (Caremark duties).

name: Joan

name: Lyle Downing

name: Matt Norris

name: Nasser Habeeb
rationale_evidence: IP: timely public access to information.

stance_evidence: 'The public should have access to timely information. Do not allow semiannual reporting.'

name: Robert Kirkpatrick
rationale_evidence: IP: investors deserve more information, not less; (Inc) FR: opens the door for more corruption. PP: 'You were hired to drain the swamp not facilitate it.'
summary: Retail Oppose: investors deserve more information, not less; increase reporting frequency rather than open the door to corruption. FLAG: PP-watch.
stance_evidence: 'Investors deserve more information not less. This change opens the door for more corruption.'

name: S Rosenberg
rationale_evidence: MF: timely reporting helps investors shape the economy toward productive outcomes; FR: reducing reporting makes insider manipulation and outright deception easier and more likely.
summary: Retail Oppose: timely reporting underpins a healthy, productive market; reducing it makes insider manipulation and deception likelier.

name: Robin Moylan

stance_evidence: 'Do the job with integrity. Follow the rules. Stop showing favoritism.'

name: Charles D
rationale_evidence: IP: investors, like patients on continuous vital-sign monitoring, need frequent reporting to make timely decisions; (Inc) IA: retired physician relying on his own monitoring.

stance_evidence: 'When critical decisions need to be made, more information is always better than less.'

name: Andrew Parker
rationale_evidence: AL: the operative position is a carve-out — bar companies with recent material revisions/misstatements/ICFR period-end failures (and lingering significant deficiencies) from semiannual reporting for at least three years, while permitting it for mature, clean issuers; AU: ICFR / material-weakness detection (PCAOB AS 2201; the early-warning intent defeated in practice); FR: heightened risk that material misstatements due to error or fraud go undetected (RFC 26); IP: investor-protection concerns (RFC 18); IA: significant deficiencies 'disclosed exclusively to institutional investors'; CMP: he names decreased comparability/consistency as a cost; (Inc) MF. Cites EGC material-weakness rates and SOX-404 adverse-ICFR data.
summary: Substantive Conditional (canonical Andrew Parker; this amended PDF replaces the original #99, which Tzachi held out as a duplicate). Same position as #99, expanded: he supports OPTIONAL semiannual reporting but only for mature, clean issuers, and would bar companies with recent ICFR/material-weakness conditions for three years and add disclosure conditions. Engages RFC 18 and RFC 26 with ICFR / SOX-404 detail. Tzachi's 2026-05-31 dedup call kept #462 over #99; the 3-rater stance stays Conditional (the amended letter is longer, not a different position).
stance_evidence: 'semiannual reporting should be restricted to mostly mature companies that have consistently avoided [material revisions / misstatements / ICFR failures]'; 'narrowing the scope of who will be eligible for semiannual reporting... will increase capital markets efficiency and decrease the administrative burden'; 'I would like to see conditions on the proposed semi-annual reporting rule'.

rfc_questions.note: Andrew Parker amended PDF comment; replaces #99. Names and answers Request for Comment 18 (investor-protection concerns) and 26 (ICFR / timely detection of material misstatements and control deficiencies under semiannual reporting).

name: Eileen Sickler
rationale_evidence: IA: 'I use the 10-Qs' to evaluate companies. IP: 'transparent and provides information that investors use'. (Inc) MF: transparency framing on market function. Letterhead reads 'Stock Club Member, CPA, Retired' but the investor capacity leads the text, so Primary + Self-described land on Individual; majority Individual.
summary: Retail Oppose from a stock-club member and retired CPA: she uses 10-Qs to evaluate companies and to update her holdings, and asks the Commission to preserve quarterly required disclosures.
date_note: SEC docket tagged 2026-06-27 (likely typo — every surrounding entry is 05-27); recorded as 05-27.

name: Homer Hill

(Inc) FR: accountability framing extends to fraud-prevention; (Inc) AU: certified-filing accountability sub-frame. Per-code majority lands IP only.
summary: One-line retail Oppose: keep quarterly so big corporations stay accountable.
date_note: SEC docket tagged 2026-06-27 (typo — PDF body itself reads 'May 27, 2026'); recorded as 05-27.

summary: Reasoned retail Oppose: individual investors rely on quarterly reports especially for small/mid-cap companies that lack Value Line and Morningstar analyst coverage; cutting to twice a year would severely limit retail knowledge.
date_note: SEC docket tagged 2026-06-27 (typo — surrounding entries are 05-27); recorded as 05-27.

name: Barbara Stanley
rationale_evidence: IP: 'Financial decisions are based on these quarterly reports' — investor reliance. Literalist reads as too generic (NR). (Inc) IA. Per-code majority IP only.

name: Bill Kraynak
rationale_evidence: AU: explicit 'the 10-Q is reviewed by independent auditors, and the CEO and CFO sign it personally and can be held legally accountable... the voluntary corporate communications that would replace them carry none of that' — the recurring campaign template. IA: 31-year Investment Club member tracking quarterly to time buys and sells; 'we need current information'. IP: investor decisions. (Inc) MF.
summary: Substantive retail Oppose from a 31-year Investment Club member: he leans on the certified-10-Q-versus-voluntary-communications template (CEO + CFO sign and are legally accountable; the voluntary disclosures carry none of that) and on a club-level workflow that uses quarterly reports to time buys and sells.

name: Bobby Hill
rationale_evidence: IP: investor reliance on disclosure. Literalist reads the single line as too generic (NR). (Inc) IA: 'informed decisions' as retail information argument. Per-code majority IP only.
summary: One-line retail Oppose: he relies on quarterly disclosures to make informed decisions.

name: Carl Williams
rationale_evidence: Bare withdrawal request, no rationale supplied.
summary: Two-line retail Oppose: he urges the Commission to withdraw the proposal, no rationale given.

name: Christopher Craft
role: Sales Director and Financial Literacy Coach
rationale_evidence: IA: 'agile, informed decisions'; 'Form 10-Q every single quarter'. IP: 'individual investor's ability to make informed decisions'. MF: 'six-month window creates a dangerous information vacuum'; 'market volatility and erosion of trust'. AU: 'weakens our ability to keep tabs on C-suite behavior'; 'executives would face far less immediate accountability'. FR: 'opens the door to unethical corporate behavior'. (Inc) US: 'active individual investor' framing as broader US-markets argument. Letter reads as AI-drafted (he labels it 'a draft of the comment paragraphs').
summary: Substantive retail Oppose from an active individual investor and financial literacy coach. The letter (drafted in a paragraph-by-paragraph format, likely with AI assistance) frames the six-month gap as an information vacuum that degrades transparency, weakens C-suite accountability, opens the door to unethical behavior, and erodes market confidence.

name: Ed. Brill

MF: calendar-cyclic businesses, seasonal variations, comparability across half-years. (Inc) AL: the rhetorical 'why not monthly?' and 'why not once a year?' would be Alternative-cadence proposals if serious, but read as sarcasm — Inclusive carries them; Primary + Literalist do not. Skeptic stays Oppose because the alternatives are explicitly sarcastic (capped by 'let's go back a hundred years... abolish the SEC altogether and save money in a mattress').
summary: Retail Oppose with a quietly sophisticated argument about calendar-cyclic businesses (seasonal variation reduces comparability of half-year reports), wrapped in a closing rhetorical sarcasm about abolishing the SEC. Argues investors need more reporting (every 2 months, even monthly), not less.

name: Elizabeth  Hill
rationale_evidence: Bare opposition statement, no rationale supplied.
summary: One-line retail Oppose: strong opposition, no rationale given.

name: Ginger Riddle
rationale_evidence: AU: explicit 'a 10-Q is reviewed by independent auditors, and the CEO and CFO sign it personally and can be held legally accountable for its contents. The voluntary corporate communications that would replace them carry none of that' — the recurring template, same wording as #331/#338/#344/#467. IA: 'individual investors like me own the majority of shares in smaller public companies, the ones most likely to take the new option'. IP: 'accountability would be decreased'. (Inc) CB: she frames the reduction as net cost to investors. (Inc) LE: she cites the 2018-2019 record (CFA Institute, Council of Institutional Investors representing ~$4 trillion) as a regulatory-history argument, the same CFA-CII parallel as #382 Keith DeSonier.
summary: Substantive retail Oppose from a retired teacher: deploys the certified-10-Q-vs-voluntary-communications template (CEO + CFO sign and are legally accountable), cites the 'individual investors own the majority of shares in smaller public companies' framing the SEC itself uses, and walks through the 2018-2019 record (CFA Institute, $4T Council of Institutional Investors, the empirical research) to argue the Commission has been here before.

name: lori carroll

stance_note: Skeptic flips to Conditional on the alternative-proposal language ('make reports less burdensome and more useful'); Primary + Literalist stay Oppose because the conclusion is unambiguous ('should not go backwards'). Majority Oppose (2-of-3).

name: Anthony Calime Jr.
rationale_evidence: IA: 'rely on timely, standardized, and comparable company information to evaluate risks'. FR: 'Less frequent reporting may make it easier for problems to remain hidden longer'.
summary: Substantive self-directed-investor Oppose: 401(k) + 529 retail investor argues quarterly reports give individual investors the only reliable checkpoint against six-month information gaps, increased information inequality, market-volatility surprise risk, harder portfolio rebalancing, and reduced management accountability. Recommends preserving quarterly reporting, with a fallback investor-protection package if semiannual is allowed.

(timely quarterly financial updates, explanation requirement, prompt material-change disclosure, equal-access protection). Same hedge pattern as #486 Anderson. Primary + Literalist stay Oppose because the headline recommendation - 'preserve quarterly reporting requirements' - is unambiguous. Majority Oppose (2-of-3).

name: Anonymous

rationale_evidence: IA: transparency / 'in the dark' framing. (Inc) IP, FR. Body also asks 'Who initiated this S7-2026-15? What do they want to hide?' — generic concealment-suspicion, NOT a PP / capture frame (no attribution to political pressure or regulatory capture).

name: Anonymous

name: Kali Lawson

name: Karen Steige

name: Lauren Gaunt
rationale_evidence: AU: the recurring template - 'a 10-Q filing is reviewed by independent auditors, and the CEO and CFO sign it personally and can be held legally accountable for its contents. IP: 'individual investors own the majority of shares in smaller public companies'. (Inc) MF.
summary: Retail Oppose carrying the certified-10-Q-vs-voluntary-communications campaign template (CEO + CFO sign and are legally accountable; voluntary communications carry none of that); cites SEC data that individual investors own the majority of shares in smaller public companies; cites the 2018-2019 SEC review where the empirical record opposed reducing reporting frequency.

name: Marshall McDade Jr.

name: Patricia A McGarrity

name: Patricia Ann Murray

summary: Retail Oppose: existing 30-60 day delay would extend to 7 months total; daily-action justification in the market would suffer; management accountability for turnarounds and strategic developments depends on timely reporting.

name: Ronald Axler

summary: Substantive retail Oppose: decades-of-precedent argument plus a counter-proposal that the right fix is stronger personal liability for officers, not less-frequent reporting; computers have reduced the reporting burden, not increased it.

name: Susan Kelty

name: Vernon Ramsey

CB: 2018-2019 SEC history + UK 2014 study ('where 90% of companies continued Reporting by choice').
summary: Substantive multi-paragraph retail Oppose with the AU template (CEO/CFO signatures eliminate plausible deniability) + 2018-2019 SEC history + UK 2014 90%-voluntary-continuance citation + TTM analysis + retirement-account framing across stock funds, 401(k), 457(b), Thrift Savings, Roth, 529, individual portfolios. One of the most substantive letters in the batch.

IA: 'Individual shareholders like myself would lose the most information'. IP: 'Commission's own data show that individual investors own the majority of shares in smaller public companies'. CB: 2018-2019 SEC history (CFA Institute, CII opposed). ICc: UK 2014 'more than 90 percent of UK public companies continued to file quarterly reports on their own when they had the choice'.
summary: Substantive retail Oppose carrying the AU template (10-Q reviewed by independent auditors / CEO+CFO sign / voluntary corporate communications carry none of that); cites SEC's own data on individual-investor ownership in smaller companies; cites the 2018-2019 SEC review (CFA Institute + CII opposed); cites UK 2014 90%-voluntary-continuance.

name: Gestalt Fund

summary: Substantive retail Oppose written under the alias 'Gestalt Fund': US markets are a global example; quarterly cadence has worked for decades; AI accelerates information pace; delayed economic releases produce volatile surprises; semi-annual brings less transparency, less inter/intra-sector comparability, more fraud potential.
