name: Joseph Wagner
rationale_evidence: AU: 'Quarterly reporting is one of the few mechanisms that ensures standardized, timely, and accurate financial information, according to US GAAP'. IA: 'standardized, timely, and accurate'. FR: 'Semi-annual reporting, on the other hand, would allow press releases, spin, and false narratives to drive valuations much longer, and corrections would be much steeper'. CMP: 'the proposed rule treats all companies equally - from a longstanding, trillion dollar brand to a new, million dollar startup. Treating such disparity equally is a fundamental flaw'. AL: explicit alternative-cadence proposal - 'A better approach would have been to make this rule available for smaller companies, such as those traded Over-The-Counter (like OTCQX and OTCQB), but unavailable for larger companies'. MF: market-discipline frame.
summary: Substantive Oppose with an alternative-cadence proposal: GAAP-standardized timely reports prevent press-release spin from driving valuations; rule treats trillion-dollar and million-dollar issuers identically (fundamental flaw); better approach would scale semiannual to smaller / OTC-traded issuers only.
stance_note: Skeptic flips to Conditional per the pattern in #486 Anderson / #492 Calime: Wagner offers a concrete alternative-cadence proposal (scaled relief for smaller / OTC issuers only) and writes 'rework or abandon the proposal'.

summary: Substantive retail Oppose: relies on company reports for portfolio updates; Value Line, Morningstar, and AI tools depend on company reports; reducing 'public' part of publicly traded reduces public trust in companies and in the SEC.

name: Dan T
rationale_evidence: IA: 'timely transparency, giving investors and regulators a frequent, unfiltered view'. MF: 'strengthens market discipline by reducing the information gaps that can lead to volatility, speculation, or mispricing'. AU: 'quarterly cadence enforces consistent management accountability... evaluated in real time rather than hidden within six-month reporting windows'.
summary: Substantive retail Oppose: quarterly preserves timely transparency before small problems become large; market discipline reduces volatility, speculation, mispricing; enforces consistent management accountability in real time.

name: CINDY L MEIER
rationale_evidence: No engaged rationale; the letter is an expression of outrage. PP: 'we are dealing with the worst corruption ever and SEC wants to protect itself and other corrupt entities.'
summary: Brief retail Oppose: outrage at corruption; SEC wants to protect itself and other corrupt entities. PP-watch instance (capture attribution).

name: Portia Catania
rationale_evidence: Bare preservation request, no rationale supplied.
summary: Two-line retail Oppose: wants quarterly reports; twice a year is not enough.

name: Cherry Nichols
rationale_evidence: FR: 'executives that want to hide illegal or inappropriate behavior' (concealment frame).
summary: Two-line retail Oppose: not appropriate to stop oversight to satisfy executives that want to hide illegal or inappropriate behavior.

name: Bob Patton
rationale_evidence: IA: 'a lot can change in 6 months'.
summary: Brief retail Oppose: investors depend on quarterly reports; a lot can change in 6 months.

name: Dave Potter
rationale_evidence: IP: 'Small investors managing our own retirement accounts'. IA: 'minimal company knowledge and protection from losing our money'. US: 'keeping cap gains taxes flowing to US Treasury'.
summary: Retail Oppose: small investors with retirement accounts need quarterly reports for minimal company knowledge and protection; also helps keep capital-gains taxes flowing to US Treasury.

name: John Yopps
rationale_evidence: IA: 'cannot afford to wait 6 months for information to make portfolio changes'. FR: 'Has Enron taught us nothing?' (historical fraud reference).
summary: Brief retail Oppose from a retired investor: cannot afford to wait 6 months for portfolio-change information; has Enron taught us nothing?

name: Anonymous
rationale_evidence: FR: 'everywhere you see biannual reporting you will find fraud'. US: 'not a world standard'. PP: 'everywhere you see biannual reporting you will find fraud but hey thats the United States way, right. Well at least under a republican administration.'
summary: Brief Anonymous retail Oppose: not a world standard; biannual reporting is associated with fraud; the United States way under a republican administration. PP-watch instance (political attribution).

name: Anonymous
rationale_evidence: Bare exclamation of opposition, no engaged rationale.
summary: Three-line Anonymous retail Oppose: are you all out of your minds; terrible idea; NO.

name: Mark Halverson
rationale_evidence: IP: level-playing-field framing.
summary: One-line retail Oppose: need sufficient time to ensure a level playing field.

name: Janki McDermott
rationale_evidence: IP: 'cause for concern for the ordinary investor'.
summary: Brief retail Oppose: drop-10-Q is unethical, sketchy, should be illegal; huge cause for concern for the ordinary investor.

name: Archer Petersen
rationale_evidence: AU: 'Accountability'. IA: 'transparency'. FR: 'institutional bad actors'. MF: 'weaken our economy'.
summary: Brief retail Oppose: accountability and transparency should be a priority; do not weaken the economy in favor of institutional bad actors.

name: William Michael Cunningham
rationale_evidence: MF: financial-crisis frame. Letterhead Creative Investment Research; author has published books on minority-owned business and the JOBS Act.
summary: Investment-professional Oppose from William Michael Cunningham (Creative Investment Research, author of books on minority-owned business and the JOBS Act): will lead to another financial crisis.

name: Cody Cole
rationale_evidence: FR: 'allow malfeasance to go undetected... hide problems within the business'.
summary: Brief retail Oppose from an engineer: reduced reporting could allow malfeasance to go undetected longer and hide problems within the business.

name: Charles Nestell
rationale_evidence: FR: 'more insider trading opportunities'.
summary: Two-line retail Oppose: less reporting means more insider trading opportunities.

name: Joshua Wright
rationale_evidence: IA: trends visibility. RI: non-investor downstream-workflow reliance - 'trends in materials manufacturing for construction pricing' - construction-industry use case for quarterly company data.
summary: Brief Oppose from a licensed architect/contractor: keep quarterly mandatory, even monthly; needs trends in materials manufacturing for construction pricing.

name: Dalvyn
rationale_evidence: FR: 'rug pull' / fraud framing. MF: 'economic crash like 2008'. PP: 'The regime is already doing what they can to weaken the US economy, why would you guys volunteer to take the fall for them??'
summary: Retail Oppose from a civil engineer: feckless CEOs will pull the same rug Elon is attempting with SpaceX; another 2008-style economic crash; the regime is weakening the US economy. PP-watch instance (regime attribution).

name: Denny
rationale_evidence: CB: implicit counter to cost-burden argument - 'now that we have computers'. AU: 'The SEC is supposed to be the financial Watch Dog, so, you set the standards and Companies Comply. Don't let the Fox Guard the Hen House'.
summary: Brief retail Oppose: now that we have computers, the cost-burden argument fails; SEC is the financial Watch Dog and sets standards; don't let the fox guard the hen house.

name: Louis Lehot & Patrick Daugherty, Foley & Lardner LLP
rationale_evidence: OP: explicit optionality / flexibility frame - 'decision whether to report quarterly or semiannually is one that boards, audit committees, and investors are best positioned to make'. AL: explicit alternative-cadence / OP-on-AL frame - the proposal is itself the alternative, lawyers endorse the optional Form 10-S structure. MF: explicit acknowledgment - 'Cutting two quarterly reports could increase the cost of capital. But that is a business judgment, not a regulatory one'. Cross-filed letter (filed on S7-2026-18 docket, lists S7-2026-15 + S7-2026-17 + S7-2026-18 + CLL-16 in Re).
summary: Substantive Support from Louis Lehot and Patrick Daugherty (Foley & Lardner LLP, Silicon Valley + Chicago, capital-formation lawyers with 70+ combined years advising founders, boards, underwriters, and investors); explicit OP/AL/MF framing - choice should rest with boards, audit committees, investors; cutting two reports could raise cost of capital, but that is a business judgment, not a regulatory one; expect few to elect semiannual in the near term; cross-filed with S7-2026-17, S7-2026-18, CLL-16.
stance_note: Cross-filed at S7-2026-18 docket URL but addresses S7-2026-15 substantively in Part I.C. The 8-bucket entity taxonomy does not yet have a Trade-association / Advocacy or Legal-practitioner bucket; coded as Industry practitioner-technologist for now. Re-bucket after the 2018 rubric fold-in ships.

name: Justin K
rationale_evidence: IP: 'Retail investors should not be forced to buy, sell, or hold stocks using stale information while executives and large institutions have a clearer picture'. IA: 'stale information'. FR: 'gives insiders more time to act on information before the public sees it'.
summary: Retail Oppose from a Chicago software developer (Justin Kobylarz): six months without required reporting gives insiders more time to act on information before the public sees it; retail should not be forced to trade on stale information while executives and institutions have a clearer picture.

name: Brink Williams
rationale_evidence: IA: 'less transparency' framing. US: 'American investor's best interests'. Does not qualify as PP - 'unless they do not prioritize the American investor's best interests' is motive-questioning without political-attribution.
summary: Retail Oppose from a Realtor: quarterly must be preserved; companies could report monthly with a click of a mouse; no idea why SEC would want less transparency unless they do not prioritize the American investor's best interests.

name: Ron Salsbury
rationale_evidence: MF: 'Factors affecting stock price happen much too quickly with computerized trading'. IP: 'essential for individual investors'. IA: timeliness frame. Signs as VP Quality TYRX Inc. - mixed entity (Letterhead: Issuer-current at a Medtronic subsidiary; Self-described + Primary: Individual since substance is investor-side, not issuer-side).
summary: Brief Oppose from Ronald Salsbury (VP Quality, TYRX Inc.): quarterly essential for individual investors; computerized trading makes stock prices move too quickly for semiannual cadence. Letterhead-mixed entity (Individual majority, Issuer-current letterhead).

name: Patrick McCabe - McCabe Fine Woodworking
rationale_evidence: IP: 'two-tiered system... company insiders and institutional investors know critical news months before everyday investors'. FR: insider-information-gap framing. CMP: explicit divestment threat - 'everyday/individual investors will choose to remove their capital from the market' (same CMP usage as #507 Hoffman, #532 Casabona).
summary: Retail Oppose from a small-business owner (McCabe Fine Woodworking, West Chester PA): two-tiered information system would push everyday investors to remove their capital from the market; we expect our regulatory bodies to REGULATE.

name: Angela Correa
rationale_evidence: MF: 'Quarterly reports are essential to market integrity.'
summary: One-line Oppose: quarterly reports are essential to market integrity.

name: Alejandro Perez
rationale_evidence: NR: 'I urge the Commission to withdraw S7-2026-15.'
summary: One-line Oppose urging withdrawal of the rule; no substantive argument.

name: Angelo Genco
rationale_evidence: RI: 'I use the 10-Q filings to determine the current value of investing'. CMP: 'compare other quarterly data verses their peers'. AL: 'Reporting should rather increase to every sixty days'.
summary: Retail Oppose who uses 10-Q filings for valuation and peer comparison; proposes increasing frequency to every 60 days. Skeptic flips to Conditional on the alternative-cadence proposal.
stance_note: Skeptic flips to Conditional on an alternative-cadence proposal (increase to 60-day reporting); majority stays Oppose (#486/#492/#593 pattern).

name: Nancy Fritz
rationale_evidence: IA: 'Investors need to see current information in order to make informed decisions.' AL: 'maybe the process and paperwork could be streamlined'.
summary: Oppose changing the reporting timeline; investors need current information. Concedes the paperwork could be streamlined, so Skeptic flips to Conditional.
stance_note: Skeptic flips to Conditional on the streamlining concession; majority stays Oppose.

name: Anonymous
rationale_evidence: RI: 'eliminate half the data points from curves we use to evaluate our investments'. IA: 'the most reliable method we have for determining the risks of investing'.
summary: Retired investor: eliminating quarterly statements removes half the data points used to evaluate investments and raises investing risk, especially for young companies and newly-held positions.

name: Jesus Gomez
rationale_evidence: FR: 'Quarterly disclosure deters securities fraud. More disclosure, not less.'
summary: Vehement one-line Oppose: quarterly disclosure deters securities fraud.

name: Beulah
rationale_evidence: IP: 'The proposed rule weakens investor protection.'
summary: One-line Oppose: the rule weakens investor protection.

name: Bubba Trammell
rationale_evidence: IA: 'This proposal would harm retail investors.'
summary: One-line Oppose: the proposal harms retail investors.

name: Emilia Kilpatrick
rationale_evidence: IP: 'Semiannual reporting reduces transparency.'
summary: One-line Oppose: semiannual reporting reduces transparency.

name: Diland Funez
rationale_evidence: NR: 'Please vote against the proposed rule.'
summary: One-line Oppose; no substantive argument.

name: Farryn Walker
rationale_evidence: NR: 'I am against this reduction in disclosure frequency.'
summary: One-line Oppose to the reduction in disclosure frequency.

name: Hayward Cole
rationale_evidence: NR: 'I don't consent to your unfavorable change or Rule.' Generic manipulation accusation, no political-pressure/capture attribution (not PP).
summary: Rambling Oppose; refuses consent to the rule and accuses the Commission of manipulation. No substantive economic argument.

name: Freddie Simmons
rationale_evidence: NR: '...write in opposition to the proposed reduction in reporting frequency' (preceded by keyboard noise).
summary: One-line Oppose (garbled prefix) to the reduction in reporting frequency.

name: Edward Dinel
rationale_evidence: US: 'The transparency of the US financial system is WHY the dollar is the reserve currency of the world... it is why we are where the world invests.'
summary: Emphatic Oppose: US disclosure transparency underpins the dollar's reserve-currency status and global investment in US markets; do not reduce it.

name: Ellen Harvey
rationale_evidence: IP: 'Reducing the number of times public companies report earnings reduces the transparency investors have'. CMP: 'changes in their earnings and strategies needs to be visible' (Allbirds pivot example).
summary: Investment professional: less frequent reporting cuts transparency into fast-moving companies; cites Allbirds' pivot from sustainable shoes to AI as an example of changes investors would miss.

name: Jacob Wiebe
rationale_evidence: IA: 'this rule directly affects the retirement security of ordinary Americans. Side with Main Street.'
summary: Oppose: the rule affects the retirement security of ordinary Americans; side with Main Street.

name: Jesus Olmeda
rationale_evidence: IA: 'The corrupt corporation that is America had BETTER remember the 99%'. Ordinary 99%-vs-powerful populist framing (excluded from PP per the #559/#589 standard).
summary: Angry Oppose on behalf of his own household; populist 'remember the 99%' framing. Not a substantive economic argument; not PP under the narrow definition.

name: José Pérez Leon
rationale_evidence: IP: 'The proposed rule weakens investor protection.'
summary: One-line Oppose: the rule weakens investor protection.

name: Pedro Gómez
rationale_evidence: NR: 'Please reject this proposal.'
summary: One-line Oppose; no substantive argument.

name: Marina Cruz
rationale_evidence: IA: 'This rule benefits insiders at the expense of ordinary investor... people.' (the ordinary insiders-vs-retail framing — IA, not PP).
summary: One-line Oppose: the rule benefits insiders at the expense of ordinary investors.

name: Miguel Torres
rationale_evidence: NR: 'I oppose S7-2026-15.'
summary: One-line Oppose; no substantive argument.

name: MK Leeds
rationale_evidence: NR: 'I am against this reduction in disclosure frequency.'
summary: One-line Oppose to the reduction in disclosure frequency. (First of two distinct MK Leeds letters; different text from #645, kept separately.)

name: MK Leeds
rationale_evidence: NR: 'Keep the reports to SEC at 3 months intervals!!!'
summary: One-line Oppose: keep reporting at three-month intervals. (Second of two distinct MK Leeds letters; different text from #644, kept separately per the Homer Hill / Moylan rule.)

name: Muthanna Almesri
rationale_evidence: NR: 'i strongly disagree with this rulemaking.'
summary: One-line Oppose; no substantive argument.

name: Ryan Breneisen
rationale_evidence: IP: 'The public access to information should not be diminished or limited by design.'
summary: Oppose: public access to information should not be diminished; bad legislation that does not help citizens.

name: Osmel Suarez
rationale_evidence: MF: 'Higher required returns means higher cost of capital for US companies.' (cost-of-capital, MF).
summary: Oppose: a substantive cost-of-capital point — higher required returns raise the cost of capital for US companies.

name: La Tortuga Moreno
rationale_evidence: NR: 'Citadel, Fidelity, Two Sigma, and D.E. Shaw have all warned the SEC against this rule. Who does this rule actually help?' Generic motive-questioning (excluded from PP per the #620 standard).
summary: Oppose: cites major investment firms' opposition and asks who the rule helps. Generic motive-questioning, not a substantive SEC-framed argument; not PP.

name: Martha Cain
rationale_evidence: NR: 'I am writing to oppose the semiannual reporting proposal.'
summary: One-line Oppose; no substantive argument.

name: Jilian Sipes
rationale_evidence: FR: 'Quarterly filings... dramatically shorten the window in which [fraud] can fester... Enron. WorldCom. Madoff.'
summary: Oppose: longer reporting gaps give fraud more time to grow; cites Enron, WorldCom, Madoff and the run-up to 2008 (keepitquarterly.org campaign).

name: Josephine Laki
rationale_evidence: CC: 'Underwriters require quarterly data to issue comfort letters for capital market transactions.' IP: 'Choose transparency over opacity.'
summary: Oppose: underwriters require quarterly data for comfort letters in capital-market transactions; choose transparency over opacity.

name: Jilian Sipes
rationale_evidence: Verbatim re-submission of the same keepitquarterly.org / Enron-WorldCom-Madoff text by the same commenter (Jilian Sipes); superseded by #651 (doc 2403667).
summary: Duplicate of #651 (Jilian Sipes) — same body text, different doc-id (2403746). Held out of the public count.
stance_note: Verbatim re-submission by Jilian Sipes; superseded by #651 (doc 2403667). Held out as Duplicate.

name: Robert Richard
rationale_evidence: IA/FR: 'opens the doors to outside influence and pilfering by insiders and well connected friends.' Insiders-vs-retail framing (IA), not explicit political-pressure/capture attribution (not PP).
summary: Oppose: reducing public-company disclosure opens the door to pilfering by insiders and the well-connected; companies that want secrecy should not incorporate and take the public's money.

name: Colene Walden
rationale_evidence: IA: 'Without the transparancy of regular company reports, retail investors are at a disadvantage in making investment decisions.'
summary: NAIC investment-club member and retiree: opposes any change; without regular reports retail investors are at a disadvantage. Credits club participation for a comfortable retirement.

name: Shem Alhassan
rationale_evidence: No position: 'I'm interested' — states no Support/Oppose/Conditional position on reporting frequency.
summary: Two-word submission ('I'm interested') stating no position on the proposal. Held out as No position.

name: Scott James
rationale_evidence: No position: 'I'm interested' — states no position on reporting frequency.
summary: Two-word submission ('I'm interested') stating no position on the proposal. Held out as No position.

name: Karen Lewis
rationale_evidence: IA: retail deprived of info insiders have; MF: volatile economy changes 'on a dime'.
summary: Retired Sr. Business Analyst (ex Disclosure/Primark/Thomson Financial): quarterly financials are necessary for all investors in a volatile economy; semiannual would endanger all but insiders.

name: Nick Yeager
rationale_evidence: IA: 'increases the information gap between corporate insiders and everyday investors'; IP: SEC mission to protect investors.
summary: Marine veteran, construction manager, retail investor: opposes; quarterly reports are one of the few tools retail has; reducing frequency widens the insider information gap and weakens investor protection.

name: Ramesh Doraiswamy
rationale_evidence: IA: insiders have continuous access; CB: AI makes a 10-Q cheap for large caps; AL: proposes semiannual only below a $75M float threshold.
summary: Retail investor (2 decades, options strategies): a 180-day blind spot disadvantages retail; proposes semiannual optional only for companies under ~$75M float, quarterly mandatory for large/mid caps. Skeptic flips to Conditional on the threshold proposal.
stance_note: Skeptic flips to Conditional on an alternative-cadence proposal (semiannual only below a $75M float threshold); majority stays Oppose.

name: Ryan Reuther
rationale_evidence: MF: UK/EU 2014 shows liquidity dries up and spreads widen; FR: adverse selection — Form 10-S used by distressed issuers; IA: 180-day void favors institutional alt-data.
summary: Substantive Oppose: three mechanisms — liquidity reduction (UK/EU 2014 evidence), systemic adverse selection (distressed issuers use 10-S), and sanctioned information asymmetry.

name: Sandra Setti
rationale_evidence: IP: reports 'essential for ensuring transparency and accountability'; IA: relies on them to avoid losing retirement savings.
summary: Energy compliance manager who trades to supplement income: keep quarterly reporting — essential for transparency and to avoid pitfalls.

name: Theresa Condict
rationale_evidence: IA: six-month gap leaves retail 'flying blind' while insiders have full visibility; FR: Enron/WorldCom cautionary tales.
summary: Retail investor: opposes; information asymmetry is unfair, skeptical the 'voluntary' framing will hold, and longer gaps raise fraud risk (Enron, WorldCom).

name: Charlie Klein
rationale_evidence: IP: 'Quarterly reporting is important to a fair and transparent open market.'
summary: Pilot: quarterly reporting is important to a fair and transparent market and must be kept.

name: Douglas Reed
rationale_evidence: IA: insiders/institutions keep current info while retail is 'last to find out'; FR: Enron/WorldCom/2008 — opacity lets problems fester.
summary: Retail investor: cutting reporting in half leaves ordinary investors blind for six months; raises insider advantage and fraud risk; 'optional' will become de facto standard.

name: Elizabeth Scott
rationale_evidence: IA: insiders gain advantage from earlier access; SG: only struggling/opaque firms opting in becomes a red flag.
summary: Three concerns: decreased transparency, increased insider/information-asymmetry risk, and a 10-S 'red flag' signaling effect.

name: Fresnel Phan
rationale_evidence: US: US markets are the global standard of transparency and stability; ICc: other countries' semiannual markets are smaller and show no benefit.
summary: Opposes: US markets are the world's largest and the global standard for transparency; no evidence semiannual reporting helped smaller foreign markets.

name: Piyush Shah
rationale_evidence: IP/oversight: Boeing 737 MAX analogy — reduced independent oversight lets issues go undetected; ST: 3->6 months won't change managerial incentives; CB: AI automation makes savings modest.
summary: Assistant Professor (PhD): independent, timely oversight matters (737 MAX analogy); the long-termism rationale is unpersuasive; automation makes the cost savings modest.

name: Susan Corman
rationale_evidence: ICc: Wirecard (EU), UK 2014 analyst-coverage loss, Singapore reconsidering; IA: businesses/lawyers know problems before shareholders.
summary: Citizen/investor: the 'global standard' is not working where tried — Wirecard, the UK's 2014 shift, Singapore reconsidering; detrimental to shareholders.

name: Landon McCoy
rationale_evidence: IA: 'Letting companies sit on that information while insiders trade on it... tilting the table further toward Wall Street.'
summary: Small-business founder and retail investor: quarterly filings let him spot red flags; six months is an eternity; 'optional' becomes standard; savings accrue to companies, costs to retail.

name: Laura Bowen
rationale_evidence: IA: structural disadvantage for those without a seat at the table; FR (accountant lens): Enron/WorldCom/2008 — quarterly cadence limits how long fraud goes undetected.
summary: Accountant and retail investor: information gap disadvantages retail; as an accountant, the fraud implications concern her most — semiannual doubles the window for undetected fraud.

name: Mark Klein
rationale_evidence: IA: institutions afford alt data (satellite/credit-card); MF: bigger surprises and volatile swings; CMP: biannual filings disrupt backtesting/data models.
summary: Trader/backtester: less transparency, bigger surprises, insider alt-data advantage, harder fraud detection, and disrupted historical data models.

name: Paul Bartelt
rationale_evidence: IP: points to the SEC's three-pillar mission — none of which is acquiescing to corporate interests. Generic motive-questioning (not PP).
summary: Notes comments are overwhelmingly opposed and asks whose interests the proposal serves; cites the SEC's mission statement.

name: Stephanie Micinski
rationale_evidence: ICc: UK/EU research finds no long-term-decision improvement; AU: quarterly reporting maintains strong internal controls; CB: savings overstated (firms already do monthly closes).
summary: CPA: international research does not support the long-termism premise; quarterly reporting sustains internal controls; cost savings are overstated and weaker-control firms may cut oversight.

name: Christopher Warson
rationale_evidence: FR: Citadel's hedge-fund + market-maker conflict; semiannual lets them 'cook the books twice a year instead of four'.
summary: Opposes: does not help retail; flags Citadel's market-maker/hedge-fund conflict of interest; halving reporting would be disastrous.

name: Kenneth Mucalo
rationale_evidence: IA: 'Investors need timely information to make sound decisions.'
summary: Site engineer: against the proposal; investors need timely information and transparency.

name: Robert Fox
rationale_evidence: IA: semiannual 'serves those with insider knowledge'; MF: would reduce confidence and market participation.
summary: Electrical engineer: quarterly reporting supports long-term stability; semiannual serves insiders and would reduce confidence and market participation.

name: Timothy Boyer
rationale_evidence: AL: 'in today's digital age we should have mandatory monthly reporting.'
summary: Information-security forensic analyst: reject the shift; if anything reporting should be monthly. Skeptic flips to Conditional on the alternative-cadence (monthly) proposal.
stance_note: Skeptic flips to Conditional on the alternative-cadence proposal (mandatory monthly); majority stays Oppose.

name: Victor Espericueta
rationale_evidence: IA: information gap favors institutions/insiders; MF: efficient price discovery and investor confidence depend on disclosure.
summary: Individual investor: timely information is one of the few tools to compete with institutions; quarterly reporting supports transparency, price discovery, governance, and confidence.

name: Alex Paransky
rationale_evidence: NR: 'Keep it quarterly. I want to know what I am investing in.'
summary: One line: keep it quarterly — wants to know what he is investing in.

name: Alexandre C
rationale_evidence: FR: 'Quarterly reporting deters fraud and discourages manipulation.'
summary: Opposes: quarterly reporting deters fraud and manipulation and supports a transparent, growth-positive market.

name: Anonymous
rationale_evidence: IA: wants to see what is happening for his 401k; CC: 'They need to keep their books anyway.'
summary: Opposes: information should be available more than twice a year; companies keep their books anyway and should keep disclosing.

name: Anonymous
rationale_evidence: IA: quarterly reporting 'helps retail investors close the gap with powerful institutional investors'.
summary: Retail investor: opposes lengthening the reporting period; quarterly cadence closes the gap with institutional investors.

name: Dan Downer
rationale_evidence: FR: 'Making it 1/2 a year would allow companies to fleece investors in new ways.'
summary: Calls the rule insane; longer gaps let companies fleece investors; suggests (rhetorically) weekly reporting.

name: Harp
rationale_evidence: FR: removing the requirement 'will create more opportunities for fraud'; AU: cash flow can be manipulated more without quarterly reporting.
summary: Quarterly reporting forces transparency; removing it creates fraud opportunities and lets cash flow be manipulated.

name: Jarrett Homan
rationale_evidence: IA: executives get six months of silence to sell; FR: doubling the window helps bad actors conceal ruin; ICc: international attempts caused volatility and frauds, forcing reversals.
summary: Retail investor: opposes; six-month silence aids insider selling and fraud concealment; international attempts failed; 'voluntary' will become the norm.

name: Jeff Wickizer
rationale_evidence: FR: 'everywhere you see biannual reporting you will find fraud'; PP: '...at least under a republican administration' — explicit partisan attribution (same template as #607).
summary: Opposes: not a world standard; associates biannual reporting with fraud and attributes the direction to the current (republican) administration. PP-watch flag.

name: Juan Bran
rationale_evidence: NR: 'This is lunacy. Please reject the proposed reduction in reporting frequency.'
summary: Site reliability engineer: brief Oppose — reject the reduction in reporting frequency.

name: Levester Best
rationale_evidence: AU: '10-Q is reviewed by independent auditors, and the CEO and CFO sign it personally... voluntary corporate communications carry none of that' (AU template); RI: 31-year investment club relies on quarterly reports.
summary: 31-year investment-club member: carries the AU template (auditor-reviewed, CEO/CFO-certified 10-Q vs unaccountable voluntary communications); the club needs current certified filings to make decisions.
stance_note: AU-template carrier.

name: Laura
rationale_evidence: NR: 'You literally are saying you agree with corporate greed and corruption if you vote Yes.' Generic greed/corruption outburst — no political-pressure/capture attribution (not PP).
summary: Opposes: the SEC should increase disclosure, not reduce it; voting yes endorses corporate greed and corruption. No substantive economic argument.

name: Liam Bruce
rationale_evidence: FR: semiannual 'will greatly increase the ability of those who are not dealing in good faith to hide their actions.'
summary: Licensed HVAC technician: opposes; semiannual will not change short-sighted management but will help bad-faith actors hide their actions.

name: Michael Giraldo
rationale_evidence: IA: ordinary investors lack the access of executives/insiders/institutions; six months is too long to go dark.
summary: Retail investor: opposes; quarterly reports matter because ordinary investors lack insider access; 'optional' becomes standard.

name: Neha Garg
rationale_evidence: MF: 'keep it quarterly otherwise you may see a total market crash!'
summary: Brief Oppose: keep reporting quarterly or risk a market crash.

name: Rodrigo Valiente
rationale_evidence: IA: 'it will benefit insiders more than anyone else'; AL: conditional — 'if you do indeed apply it, then... enforce that insiders can only sell company stock twice a year'.
summary: Opposes; if adopted, proposes a parallel rule locking insiders (and politicians) to twice-a-year stock sales after earnings. Skeptic flips to Conditional on the conditional proposal.
stance_note: Skeptic flips to Conditional on a conditional alternative (insider-sell restriction if the rule is adopted); majority stays Oppose.

name: Sara Brantley
rationale_evidence: No position: 'Sounds cool' — states no Oppose/Support/Conditional position on reporting frequency.
summary: Two-word submission ('Sounds cool') stating no position. Held out as No position.

name: Shane & Jamie Trez
rationale_evidence: NR: 'Is it NOT the idea to be as transparent as possible!' — pro-transparency thread amid unrelated tangents.
summary: Rambling submission opposing reduced transparency ('be as transparent as possible'); the rest digresses into unrelated topics. No substantive economic argument.

name: Shawn Grona
rationale_evidence: IP: 'reduces transparency'; MF: 'may increase volatility'.
summary: Brief Oppose: semiannual reporting reduces transparency and may increase volatility.

name: Clayton Braden
rationale_evidence: IA: 'We don't get enough [transparency] compared to insiders and large investors, and therefore are at a disadvantage.'
summary: Retail trader: keep quarterly earnings; transparency is key and retail is already at a disadvantage to insiders and large investors.

name: Richard Biffl
rationale_evidence: IA: public filings are his primary source vs insiders; CB: the 'burden' is a pretext (an accountant produces it with a few clicks); AL: reports should be more frequent (monthly).
summary: Individual investor: public filings are his primary information source; the compliance 'burden' is a pretext to increase information disparity; disclosures should be more frequent, e.g. monthly. Skeptic flips to Conditional on the alternative-cadence proposal.
stance_note: Skeptic flips to Conditional on the alternative-cadence proposal (more frequent / monthly); majority stays Oppose.

name: Linda Thomas
rationale_evidence: IA: 'investors are kept in the dark about stock performance'; retiree managing funds in a downturn.
summary: Retiree: reject the proposal; investors should not be kept in the dark about stock performance, especially retirees managing funds in a downturn.

name: Dennis M. Kelleher & Benjamin L. Schiffrin, Better Markets, Inc.
rationale_evidence: No position (procedural): a comment-period EXTENSION request — Better Markets asks for 60 more days (120 total), noting the proposal is 279 pages with 58 RFCs while the shorter 2018 request got 90 days; it explicitly defers its substantive position ('looks forward to providing its own substantive comments'). Argues at length for quarterly reporting's value to retail (IA/IP) but takes no Support/Oppose/Conditional stance.
summary: Better Markets (Dennis Kelleher, Cofounder/President/CEO; Benjamin Schiffrin, Director of Securities Policy) — a non-profit financial-reform advocacy organization — requests a 60-day extension of the comment period (to 120 days total), arguing the 279-page, 58-RFC proposal needs more time and that the far shorter 2018 request got 90 days. It defers its substantive position to a later filing. Classified No position (procedural extension request); entity Trade association / advocacy organization.
stance_note: Procedural comment-period extension request; defers a substantive stance. Held out as No position. First letter in the Trade-association / advocacy entity bucket.

name: Anonymous
rationale_evidence: FR/IA: 'You simply cannot assume individuals will act in the best interest of their countrymen with their personal compensation on the line'; SEC filings demand accountability for public-company leaders.
summary: Equity-compensation expert: SEC filing obligations protect investors and demand accountability from public-company leaders whose pay is on the line; reducing visibility hurts the investing public.

name: Anonymous
rationale_evidence: IA: quarterly reporting informs shareholders of company health; delay can devastate investment assets in a fast-moving world.
summary: Opposes: quarterly reporting informs shareholders; in a fast-moving world delay can devastate investors' assets.

name: Anonymous
rationale_evidence: IP: 'The SEC was created to ensure full and fair disclosure'; FR: '6 months would allow longer grifts.'
summary: Reject: a 3-month requirement is not burdensome; six months would allow longer grifts; the SEC exists for full and fair disclosure.

name: Charles Dignazio
rationale_evidence: IA: reports let him make changes sooner than six months later; FR: longer gaps make it 'seem like... companies are hiding their losses and profits' and harder to hold them accountable.
summary: Former active investor: quarterly reports let investors act sooner and hold companies accountable; longer gaps look like companies hiding losses, profits, and mistakes.

name: Elizabeth Haines
rationale_evidence: IA: 'My investment information is much more current if the SEC reports are quarterly.'
summary: Objects: investment information is much more current under quarterly reporting; keep the quarterly system.

name: Ethel Brown
rationale_evidence: RI: 'As an individual invested the quarterly reports are very important to me. I watch my holdings carefully.'
summary: BetterInvesting individual investor: quarterly reports are very important for watching holdings closely, including large positions; opposes semiannual.

name: James R Windgassen
rationale_evidence: MF: removing quarterly reporting 'will discourage individual investors... and drive them towards alternative assets,' damaging public markets.
summary: Consulting engineer (Northrop Grumman), writing as an investor: quarterly reporting is essential; removing it would push individual investors toward alternative assets and damage public markets.

name: Joe Y
rationale_evidence: IA: 'it will put retail investors at a significant disadvantage.'
summary: One line: opposes; the rule puts retail investors at a significant disadvantage.

name: John Chu
rationale_evidence: IA: 'More information for investors is critical. Less timely information is BAD.'
summary: Brief Oppose: more, timelier information for investors is critical.

name: John Greco
rationale_evidence: MF: 'Less disclosure means slower price discovery.'
summary: Opposes: less disclosure means slower price discovery.

name: Kem Patterson
rationale_evidence: NR: 'How is only semi-annual reporting a good thing?? Keep the quarterly reporting!!'
summary: Brief Oppose: keep quarterly reporting.

name: Ken Berg
rationale_evidence: IA: retail relies on public disclosures while institutions have private research/alt-data; CMP: optional semiannual makes companies harder to compare; Form 8-K is not an adequate substitute for the 10-Q.
summary: Substantive Oppose: quarterly 10-Qs underpin market trust; retail relies on public disclosures; Form 8-K is no substitute; optional semiannual reduces comparability across peers.

name: Michael George
rationale_evidence: IA: institutions buy alt-data/channel checks while retail relies on the 10-Q; MF: stale data and 'unpriced stock shocks'; FR: a 180-day window 'exponentially increases the risk of insider trading' (cites Trump-administration pandemic-briefing controversies as a fraud-risk analogy, not political-pressure attribution).
summary: Strong Oppose: a six-month cadence exacerbates information asymmetry and volatility and lengthens the window for insider trading; cites pandemic-era insider controversies as a cautionary example.

name: Omar Ziadeh
rationale_evidence: IP: 'The SEC was created to ensure full and fair disclosure. Reducing reporting is a net negative for all investors.'
summary: Software engineer: opposes; the SEC exists for full and fair disclosure and reducing reporting is a net negative for all investors.

name: Paul Knife
rationale_evidence: IP: 'Average investors should not be kept in the dark. We need transparency.'
summary: Brief Oppose: average investors should not be kept in the dark; transparency is needed.

name: Richard Leonard
rationale_evidence: MF/RI: 'The loss of quarterly reporting raises the risk threshold for me as an investor.'
summary: Individual investor (mentions Two Rivers Investment Group): losing quarterly reporting raises his investment risk threshold; urges disapproval.

name: Robert Shaw
rationale_evidence: IA: 'I rely on the quarterly reports to track... how well management is doing... A lot can go wrong in 3 months and even more could go wrong in 6 months.'
summary: Relies on quarterly reports to track management performance; six months would impair his ability to monitor the companies he invests in.

name: Robert Ulm
rationale_evidence: IA: 'Information asymmetry harms ordinary investors most'; FR: 'Less reporting means more insider trading opportunities.'
summary: Opposes: information asymmetry harms ordinary investors and less reporting means more insider-trading opportunities.

name: Saul Rosenberg
rationale_evidence: MF: 'Every six months is way too long. Situations and competition makes major changes faster and faster these days.'
summary: Retired investor (Saul Rosenberg): keep quarterly reporting as has been done for half a century; six months is too long given how fast competition and conditions change.

name: Samuel Neves
rationale_evidence: IA: 'Quarterly reporting is essential for investors to understand the health and direction of publicly traded companies, now more than ever.'
summary: Brief Oppose: quarterly reporting is essential to understand company health and direction.

name: Susan Bahr
rationale_evidence: RI: quarterly reporting is 'the best tool' to monitor public companies; CB: 'With AI tools this should present a hardship to these companies' (i.e. automation makes it cheap, rebutting the burden claim).
summary: BetterInvesting individual investor: quarterly reporting is the best tool to monitor public companies, and AI tools mean it should not be a hardship for issuers.

name: Todd Mathews
rationale_evidence: IA: 'Removing quarterly reports tilts the playing field toward institutions'; AL: 'the SEC should increase the reporting to once every two months.'
summary: Small investor: opposes; the rule tilts toward institutions; proposes increasing reporting to every two months. Skeptic flips to Conditional on the alternative-cadence proposal.
stance_note: Skeptic flips to Conditional on the alternative-cadence proposal (every two months); majority stays Oppose.

name: Will Lyons
rationale_evidence: FR: less public information 'increase[s] pressure for certain types of professional investors to get information through unofficial/illegal channels'; IA: harder for honest investors.
summary: Opposes: reducing public information makes investing harder for honest investors and pressures professionals toward unofficial/illegal information channels.

name: Gary Peterson
rationale_evidence: IP: 'the public needs to know legal detail about any company traded publicly to make a reasoned investment decision.'
summary: Opposes: the public needs detailed disclosure on any publicly traded company to make reasoned investment decisions.

name: Andrea Horbinski
rationale_evidence: IA: quarterly reporting keeps individual investors informed; FR: 'duped by corporations' / books secret for six months.
summary: PhD retail investor: quarterly reporting keeps individuals informed about corporate risk; do not let companies keep their books secret for six months.

name: Anonymous
rationale_evidence: IA: 'I'm not buying what I can't research.'
summary: One line: will not buy what cannot be researched; keep quarterly reporting.

name: Chuck Schultz
rationale_evidence: IA: retiree needs frequent updates on his financial situation.
summary: Retiree: frequent updates are imperative to avoid becoming 'a ward of the state'; keep quarterly reporting.

name: Colton Growney
rationale_evidence: MF: semiannual reporting 'will reduce available information... making our capital markets less efficient and more prone to manipulation.'
summary: Investment advisor (10 years, CFA): semiannual reporting reduces information, makes capital markets less efficient and more manipulable — bad outcomes for issuers and investors alike.

name: Farhad Dhabhar
rationale_evidence: FR/IA: 'pave the way for more corruption and grift from insiders at the expense of retail investors.' Generic motive-questioning ('In whose interest is the SEC acting?') — not PP per the #620/#673 standard.
summary: Opposes: less frequent reporting enables insider grift at retail's expense; if quarterly reporting costs money, companies can trim executive pay.

name: Janet Porter
rationale_evidence: IA: 'I cannot make informed decisions about investing if I don't have up to date information.'
summary: Nurse practitioner: keep quarterly reporting; cannot invest informed without current information.

name: Julie Green
rationale_evidence: IA: 'I'm not an industry insider... there will be market slippage while insiders take positions... before the public knows.'
summary: Investor since the 1970s: relies on contemporaneous audited financials; insiders would trade ahead of the public during the gap; enforcement after the fact does not undo retail losses.

name: Lorna Pater
rationale_evidence: RI: 'I count on those quarterly reports to help me with my decisions I make with my retirement accounts.'
summary: Retail investor: counts on quarterly reports for retirement-account decisions; keep quarterly reporting.

name: Richard Parmelee
rationale_evidence: IP/IA: the rule 'will remove transparency about how our retirement funds are reported to us.' Generic 'grift on the American people' framing — no political mechanism named, not PP per the narrow definition.
summary: Opposes 'this new grift on the American people': the rule removes transparency and the ability to manage retirement investments with current performance information.

name: Rome
rationale_evidence: IA: 'granting insiders an unfair advantage over retail investors'; MF: 'increase market volatility... trade on speculation rather than timely data.'
summary: Structured Oppose: six-month windows exacerbate information asymmetry and increase volatility as investors trade on speculation.

name: Theresa Duperon
rationale_evidence: FR: 'a lot of shady things are going on in the market'; PP: 'we have criminals working in the White House. That's impactful.' — explicit attribution to the political regime (qualifies per the #607/#616 precedent).
summary: Opposes: keep quarterly reporting because of market 'shady things' and — explicitly — 'criminals working in the White House.' PP-watch flag.
stance_note: PP-coded: explicit political-regime attribution ('criminals working in the White House').

name: Tracie McCutchen
rationale_evidence: IP: quarterly reporting is 'open, and accessible to investors. Keeps you fellas honest.'
summary: Brief Oppose: quarterly reporting is open, accessible, and keeps companies honest; hands off.

name: Gabriel Gardner
rationale_evidence: IA: six-month gap widens the insider/public divide; CMP: 8-K releases lack financials/MD&A and mixed cadences destroy comparability; CC: lenders and debt covenants still require quarterly tracking; ST: short-termism is a governance issue, not a disclosure issue.
summary: Substantive Oppose: information asymmetry, the inadequacy of voluntary 8-K releases (fragmented disclosure, lost comparability), speculative cost savings given covenant requirements, and a rebuttal of the short-termism rationale.

name: Joseph Corcoran & Raymond Mosca, SIFMA
role: Managing Director & Associate General Counsel; AVP, Asset Management Group
rationale_evidence: No position (procedural): SIFMA + SIFMA AMG request a 60-day extension of the comment period. Reasons: underwriter due-diligence questions, timeliness of investor information, comparability across mixed cadences, two overlapping offering-reform rulemakings (S7-2026-17, S7-2026-18), and the July 6 deadline coinciding with the 250th anniversary of American independence. Defers substantive comments.
summary: SIFMA (Joseph Corcoran, MD & Associate General Counsel) and SIFMA AMG (Raymond Mosca, AVP) — the leading broker-dealer/asset-manager trade association — request a 60-day extension of the comment period, citing the proposal's complexity (underwriter due diligence, investor-information timeliness, comparability), two overlapping offering-reform proposals, and the July 6 deadline's collision with the U.S. semiquincentennial. The second formal extension request on the docket (after Better Markets #701). Classified No position (procedural); entity Trade association / advocacy organization.
stance_note: Second comment-period extension request (after Better Markets). Held out as No position; Trade-association bucket.

name: Lord Tofu
rationale_evidence: US: 'These quarterly reports are what gives the world faith to invest into US markets'; CB: companies should 'invest in better infrastructure' rather than report less.
summary: Opposes: quarterly reports underpin global faith in US markets; if reporting is hard, companies should invest in better infrastructure.

name: Darlene Futch
rationale_evidence: FR: 'Reducing reporting frequency increases the risk of market manipulation.'
summary: Brief Oppose: withdraw the proposal; less frequent reporting raises manipulation risk.

name: Adam Luthman
rationale_evidence: IP: 'The SEC was created to ensure full and fair disclosure'; FR: 'With so much rampant fraud... reducing visibility of financials would only encourage more.'
summary: Opposes: the SEC exists for full and fair disclosure; reduced visibility encourages more fraud.

name: Daniel Silvers
rationale_evidence: IA: 'Information asymmetry harms ordinary investors most.'
summary: One line: opposes; information asymmetry harms ordinary investors most.

name: Dustin Lenz
rationale_evidence: FR: 'Less disclosure means more opportunity for corporate misconduct.'
summary: Brief Oppose: less disclosure means more corporate misconduct.

name: Elaine
rationale_evidence: IP: the shift 'decreases investor transparency.'
summary: Brief Oppose: decreases investor transparency; not the right direction.

name: George Wright
rationale_evidence: IA: 'The game is already rigged. Please don't make it worse!' (insiders-vs-retail framing — not PP).
summary: Brief Oppose: the game is already rigged against ordinary investors; do not make it worse.

name: John Hunter
rationale_evidence: FR: 'Reducing reporting frequency increases the risk of market manipulation.'
summary: Brief Oppose: manipulation risk; do not take information away from the public.

name: Larry Wilson
rationale_evidence: FR: 'We need to reduce fraud in our system, not increase it.'
summary: Brief Oppose: the system needs less fraud, not more.

name: Jean Psmith
rationale_evidence: FR: 'Allowing companies to hide their bad numbers whilst continuing to offer shares' is 'buying a pig in a poke'; MF: 'This action would absolutely chill our stock market. Serious investors will turn their backs on US companies.'
summary: Vivid Oppose: a six-month dark period lets companies hide bad numbers while selling shares; serious investors would shun US companies and the market would chill.

name: Lawrence Schaffer II
role: Small business owner, LLC Serene Distribution (Tennessee)
rationale_evidence: IP: 'the public is protected from losses in their stock portfolios that they rely on for retirement.'
summary: Small business owner (Tennessee): calls the change an atrocity; public companies should keep filing quarterly so the retirement-investing public is protected.

name: Matthew Bernardo
rationale_evidence: IA/FR: timely disclosure prevents 'insiders from slowly dumping stock during a longer blackout from public filings.'
summary: Medical-device sales professional: harmful to consumers and retirement savers; timely transparency prevents insiders from quietly dumping stock during a longer blackout.

summary: Oppose: Zackery Alvarez — retail/individual letter; rationale IA,FR.

name: John Peltier
rationale_evidence: FR: 'Quarterly reports prevent fraud from compounding over months.'
summary: Oppose: John Peltier — retail/individual letter; rationale FR.

name: Anonymous
rationale_evidence: IP: 'Any government agency should practice transparency and act to support the citizenry'; IA: 'how dare the SEC enable corporate America against We the People'; MF: 'less informed about important, timely information upon which to base our investment decisions.'
summary: Oppose: Anonymous — retail/individual letter; rationales IP, IA, MF.

name: Anonymous
rationale_evidence: FR: 'the changes in reporting will allow fraud to go undetected because it could allow companies to “fix their books”.'
summary: Oppose: Anonymous — retail/individual letter; rationale FR.

name: Anonymous
rationale_evidence: IA: 'average people will not have correct or up to date information to make good choices'; FR: 'allow companies insider trading information'.
summary: Oppose: Anonymous — retail/individual letter; rationales IA, FR.

name: Brandon Ross
rationale_evidence: MF: 'Investors need this information to make important decisions'; AL: 'Financial reporting should be moving towards ... MONTHLY reporting'; IP: 'Keep public companies accountable with quarterly reports.'
summary: Oppose: Brandon Ross — retail/individual letter; rationales IP, MF, AL.
stance_note: Proposes MORE-frequent (monthly) reporting; Skeptic flips to Conditional on the alternative-cadence proposal. Primary + Literalist read Oppose; majority Oppose.

name: Carsten McCollum
rationale_evidence: IP: 'Quarterly reporting remains an important mechanism for protecting investors'; MF: 'enhances market efficiency, and helps identify emerging financial risks'; IA: 'reduces information gaps between management and shareholders.'
summary: Oppose: Carsten McCollum — retail/individual letter; rationales IP, MF, IA.

name: Dan Murillo
rationale_evidence: IP: 'Transparency is a cornerstone of investor confidence and market integrity'; MF: 'Public markets function best when investors have the information necessary'; IA: 'everyday Americans who rely on accurate information.'
summary: Oppose: Dan Murillo — retail/individual letter; rationales IP, MF, IA.

name: Elaine Cornacchia
rationale_evidence: IA: 'this sounds like it only benefits the rich who don’t have everything to lose like an average american.'
summary: Oppose: Elaine Cornacchia — retail/individual letter; rationale IA.

name: Fred Chase
rationale_evidence: IP: 'Quarterly reporting requirements are the backbone of the rigorous public disclosure system upon which investors have come to depend.'
summary: Oppose: Fred Chase — retail/individual letter; rationale IP.

name: Gary McClellan
rationale_evidence: IA: 'Not fair! Especially for the small investor!'
summary: Oppose: Gary McClellan — retail/individual letter; rationale IA.

name: Hunter Collins
rationale_evidence: FR: 'Corporate fraud and abuse are at record highs'; MF: 'I have to do my own research and quarterly reporting helps with that.'
summary: Oppose: Hunter Collins — retail/individual letter; rationales FR, MF.

name: Huy
rationale_evidence: IP: 'Removing public oversight'; FR: 'benefits no one but the bad actors.'
summary: Oppose: Huy — retail/individual letter; rationales IP, FR.

name: Jackson Lemp
rationale_evidence: IA: 'Asymmetrical information directly harms investors ... further advantage insiders and large institutions over retail investors'; MF: 'Timely disclosure is one of the foundations of fair and efficient markets.'
summary: Oppose: Jackson Lemp — retail/individual letter; rationales IA, MF.

name: Joanne Baek
rationale_evidence: IA: 'Making financial information MORE hidden ... harms ordinary investors'; FR: 'makes insider trading all the more lucrative.'
summary: Oppose: Joanne Baek — retail/individual letter; rationales IA, FR.

name: John Warner
rationale_evidence: IA: 'It makes it seem like the financial sector is really stacking the deck against individuals'; MF: 'changing how and when institutional indexes need to buy new ipos.' Entity: self-identifies as a data scientist (Self-described + Letterhead → Industry practitioner/technologist); Primary reads him as a retail investor (Individual). Majority Individual? See note.
summary: Oppose: John Warner — data scientist; entity Industry practitioner/technologist (majority); rationales IA, MF.

name: Kerri Nugent-Wells
rationale_evidence: MF: 'There is no way we will keep investing in the stock market without quarterly reports.'
summary: Oppose: Kerri Nugent-Wells — retail/individual letter; rationale MF.

name: Matthew Lunsford
rationale_evidence: MF: 'does not benefit the investment decisions of my pension fund or myself'; IA: 'a big advantage to those inside the company'; ICc: 'Advantages ... are not borne out when you look at other nations ... Many who have tried it appear to be retreating.'
summary: Oppose: Matthew Lunsford — retail/individual letter; rationales MF, IA, ICc.

name: Matthew Russell
rationale_evidence: FR: 'Did we learn nothing from Enron?'; AU: 'How does this follow GAAP guidelines?'
summary: Oppose: Matthew Russell — retail/individual letter; rationales FR, AU.

name: Mercer Pickens
rationale_evidence: MF: 'Timely reporting allows me to evaluate a company's performance ... make informed choices'; IA: 'create an unfair advantage for corporate insiders ... information asymmetries increase'; IP: 'make the markets less transparent.' Entity: writes 'as an individual investor' (Primary + Self-described → Individual); docket affiliation 'Director of Operations, Academy of Dental & Medical Anesthesia' (Letterhead → Industry practitioner). Majority Individual.
summary: Oppose: Mercer Pickens — writes as individual investor (entity Individual, majority); rationales MF, IP, IA.

name: Mike Corn
rationale_evidence: MF: 'Investors and regulators both will not have current data upon which to measure'; FR: 'a wide window is made available for all things troublesome'; CB (rebuttal): 'virtually every corporation will continue to compile the quarterly data, so it will not be an extra burden.'
summary: Oppose: Mike Corn — retail/individual letter; rationales MF, FR, CB.

name: Mike Powers
rationale_evidence: FR: 'Moving away from quarterly reporting would increase the risk of fraud.'
summary: Oppose: Mike Powers — retail/individual letter; rationale FR.

name: Steve Komondorea
rationale_evidence: IA: 'leave the retail trader as the last to know and the first to get hurt'; FR: 'C-suite opportunities to leverage the company, commit additional shares, leveraging then cashing out'; MF: 'Shifts in market trends, changes in financing costs during that long time period.'
summary: Oppose: Steve Komondorea — retail/individual letter; rationales IA, FR, MF.

name: Scott MacLeod
rationale_evidence: IA: 'Stop the enshittification of semi-annual fillings for corporate welfare.'
summary: Oppose: Scott MacLeod — retail/individual letter; rationale IA.

name: Shane Gibeault
rationale_evidence: IA: 'Changing the reporting requirements will rig investments against all common investors and ensure only brokers and corporation's win.' Entity: personal/veteran appeal (Primary + Self-described → Individual); email-signature title 'Director of Information & Marketing Technologies' (Letterhead → Industry practitioner). Majority Individual.
summary: Oppose: Shane Gibeault — entity Individual (majority; letterhead dissent); rationale IA.

name: Tim Trowbridge
rationale_evidence: MF: 'reducing corporate reporting requirements to semi-annually will deprive investors of timely, critical information'; IP: 'the owners of corporations, the shareholders, are provided timely financial performance information.'
summary: Oppose: Tim Trowbridge — retail/individual letter; rationales MF, IP.

name: William Ragan
rationale_evidence: FR: 'an opportunity for companies to obscure their malfeasance'; IA: 'take advantage of little old ladies and the last of their investments'; PP: 'Bad actors and the sorry politicians they bribe.' — explicit political-corruption/capture attribution (qualifies under the narrow PP definition, per the #607/#616/#736 precedent).
summary: Oppose: William Ragan — retail/individual letter; rationales FR, IA, PP.

name: Deborah Craycroft
rationale_evidence: NR: opposes via a (topically confused) tax/IRS argument — 'we should not be giving it away to people who do not want to maintain their books.'
summary: Oppose: Deborah Craycroft — retail/individual (paralegal aside); rationale NR.

name: John Fleck
rationale_evidence: IA: 'so many Americans struggling ... to potentially lose half would be devastating'; concern for elderly parents' savings.
summary: Oppose: John Fleck — retail/individual; rationale IA.

name: Kat Kingsley
rationale_evidence: IP: 'As citizens we must know in a timely manner' — transparency; bulk of letter concerns corporate political influence (tangential).
summary: Oppose: Kat Kingsley — retail/individual; rationale IP (tangential).

name: Kriz Family Mark

name: Matthew Guerrera
rationale_evidence: IA: You need to protect the little guy. MORE DISCLOSURE IS SAFER.
summary: An individual opposes the change, urging the SEC to protect the little guy on the ground that more disclosure is safer.

name: Meera K
rationale_evidence: MF: relies on quarterly filings to make informed decisions on my investments, and need a smaller gap between filings to ensure the info is relevant; IA: insiders have less time to gain an advantage
summary: An individual brokerage investor opposes both mandatory and optional semiannual filing, arguing quarterly data keeps information relevant and limits insider advantage.

name: Michael Ceyko
rationale_evidence: ST: Quarterly reporting prioritizes short term benefits over long term thinking. Companies cannot innovate when beholden to quarterly reports; CB: while also bearing the burden of constant reporting
summary: An engineer at Meta fully supports eliminating quarterly reporting, arguing it forces short-termism and burdens companies that should focus on long-term strategy.

name: Michael McMearty
rationale_evidence: IA: make markets more prone to manipulation by insiders as a result of the public having less relative insight; EX: most companies will face high pressure to only report the minimum amount, especially as larger gold standard companies only report semiannually; CB: The burdens of reporting can be heavy especially for smaller companies; AL: A middle ground might be struck where there could be a simplified quarterly reporting that is less burdensome but still highly informative, with full reporting happening once or twice a year
summary: An individual proposes a hybrid middle ground of simplified quarterly reporting with full reports once or twice yearly, citing both insider-manipulation risk and competitive pressure on smaller companies to under-report.

name: Noah W.
rationale_evidence: MF: you would lose a great deal of fidelity into how the business is actually functioning. Keep quarterly reports.
summary: A systems architect and investor who reads the reports opposes the change, saying semiannual reporting would sacrifice fidelity into how a business is actually functioning.

name: Parker Berg
rationale_evidence: FR: insider trading extravaganzas? Fraud, corruption, no accountability are earmarks of this policy change... the fox is telling the hen house to stand down for six months.
summary: An individual opposes the rule, arguing it invites insider trading, fraud, and corruption by leaving the fox to guard the henhouse for six months.

name: Patricia Boykins
rationale_evidence: IA: Help protect us smaller, private investors before we are forced out of the market. We do not have vast financial resources; MF: need to be able to make informed investment decisions
summary: An operations manager opposes the change, arguing quarterly reporting protects smaller investors who lack vast resources and need timely information to make informed decisions.

name: Porter Gardiner
rationale_evidence: IP: If I own part of a company, the company ought to be obligated to provide reporting on a regular basis. Quarterly is plenty of time for a public company
summary: A startup CTO and cofounder opposes the proposal, arguing companies should be obligated to report regularly and quarterly cadence is ample for public firms.

name: R. Koop
rationale_evidence: FR: crimes of fraud and theft prosper over the longer amounts of time that it can happen without someone checking books; IA: protecting me and the little guys out here
summary: An individual with her life savings in the market and former bank-teller experience opposes the change, arguing longer reporting gaps enable fraud and the SEC should protect small investors.

name: Rick Christie
rationale_evidence: IP: Transparency is non-negotiable. With more retail investors than ever; this proposal seems to be missing the mark
summary: An individual opposes the change, arguing transparency is non-negotiable and the proposal misses the mark given record numbers of retail investors.

name: Sarah Browne
rationale_evidence: MF: I have a very small business and need information frequently to successfully monitor the little money I have.; CB: This proposal is minimally positive for businesses( creating small gains likely to be paid to executives); IA: This proposal seems to be intended to benefit the wealthy and injure the everyday American.
summary: A small-business owner opposes the change, arguing it offers only small executive-bound gains for companies while harming millions of low-income Americans who need frequent information to manage their investments.

name: Shakir
rationale_evidence: MF: Less reporting will not help retail investors make educated decisions.
summary: A self-described concerned investor opposes the rule, arguing less reporting will not help retail investors make educated decisions and would have a negative impact.

name: Shannon Brinkman
rationale_evidence: MF: We need quarterly reporting - to understand if our money is being invested well! Without it is investing blindly.
summary: A small photo business owner opposes the change, arguing quarterly reporting is needed to know whether one's money is invested well, without which investing is blind.

name: Shayn Delph
rationale_evidence: FR: Reduced reporting frequency can only serve to hide information from investors; MF: hinder the natural price-discovery process; AL: If any change should be made, it should be made to be monthly instead of quarterly
summary: An individual strongly opposes semiannual reporting as benefiting corporate bad actors and, if anything, proposes increasing frequency to monthly.

name: Stephen Carlisle
rationale_evidence: IA: There is a term in the industry regarding individual small investors. Dumb Money. It has been used by Wall Street to dehumanize retail investors; IP: the SEC would develop policies that will make publicly held companies more accountable for misuse of investor funds
summary: A construction worker, small business owner, and veteran opposes the change, arguing it would erode public trust and accountability and harm emotionally invested small retail investors.

name: Steven Zuber
rationale_evidence: IP: I would like to keep SEC reporting quarterly because transparency is important to me. Less frequent reporting means less transparency
summary: A self-described investor opposes the change, arguing that transparency matters to him and that less frequent reporting means less transparency.

name: Tek Consult
rationale_evidence: MF: This ensures that public shareholders have access to timely financial information; IA: and not at a disadvantage to insiders and analyst
summary: A self-identified vice president opposes the change, arguing quarterly reporting ensures public shareholders get timely information and are not disadvantaged relative to insiders and analysts.

name: Terry Bohaning
rationale_evidence: AL: Require them to post the raw unaudited results each month with full reporting as is currently provided every third month.; IA: It reduces the amount of good data available to the small investor.
summary: A retired individual currently trading stocks opposes reducing reporting and proposes the alternative of monthly raw unaudited results plus full quarterly reporting, harming the small investor if reduced.

name: Will B Nelson III
rationale_evidence: MF: they should have current accurate information
summary: A citizen opposes the change, arguing anyone who invests their income should have current, accurate information.

name: William Moore
rationale_evidence: NR: This is not how business is done. I can't image any other meaningful organization, with such potential dire consequences, holding (2) two meeting per year
summary: A retired commenter opposes the change, saying no meaningful organization with such consequences would report only twice a year.

name: Al FENGER
rationale_evidence: MF: As an individual investor I need them for my research since they are the top of my list to decide what to invest in
summary: An individual investor opposes the change, stating that quarterly 10-Q filings are essential to his investment research.

name: Anand
rationale_evidence: MF: make it to difficult find timely financial information about companies and make it harder for average retail investor to arrive at investment decisions; IA: this will only incentivize companies to share information internally to select few
summary: An individual opposes the change, arguing it would deny retail investors timely financial information and incentivize companies to share information internally with a select few.

name: Anonymous
rationale_evidence: MF: Quarterly earnings are critical to investors in order to provide insight into companies financial health.
summary: An anonymous commenter opposes the change, saying quarterly earnings are critical for insight into companies' financial health.

name: Anonymous
rationale_evidence: NR: Please withdraw the proposed rule. Companies that don't want to report quarterly can stay private.
summary: An anonymous individual asks the SEC to withdraw the rule, arguing companies that don't want to report quarterly can stay private.

name: Anonymous
rationale_evidence: IP: Companies should report quarterly. It's safer for investors.
summary: An anonymous individual opposes the change, stating companies should keep reporting quarterly because it is safer for investors.

name: Ghst Rydr
rationale_evidence: IP: Public companies need to report to shareholders, and quarterly reporting is extremely important. To move this to 6 months would be a crime
summary: An individual stresses that public companies must report to shareholders and calls moving to a six-month cadence a crime.

name: Art
rationale_evidence: IA: This proposal is an attack on the individual investor. Another David vs Goliath; MF: the last thing I need is less information on the companies we own or are looking to invest in PP: 'The current administration is pushing for these changes' — explicit administration attribution (per #854/#997 precedent).
summary: An individual investor opposes the change as an attack on retail investors that reduces needed information and increases volatility, noting the current administration is pushing it.
pp_candidate_note: The current administration is pushing for these changes. Please, stop them.

name: C. Yu
rationale_evidence: FR: It would incentivize insider trading and securities fraud; IP: sunshine is the best disinfectant. This rule change casts us all back into the Dark Ages of regulation
summary: A retired attorney with past securities-regulation exposure who is also a retail investor opposes the change, arguing it would incentivize insider trading and securities fraud and reduce the disinfecting effect of disclosure.

name: catherine mcginnis
rationale_evidence: IP: I want to continue with quarterly reporting, we need that transparency!!!
summary: An individual opposes the change, urging continuation of quarterly reporting for the sake of transparency.

name: Chris

name: Claudette Lopez
rationale_evidence: MF: I am a retail investor and quarterly reports inform me how to make my decisions

name: Daniel Cooper

Corporations have a duty to report and we the people have a vested interest in their transparency
summary: An individual from South Carolina opposes the change, invoking Enron-like scandal risk and the public's interest in corporate transparency.

name: David Sturgeon
rationale_evidence: IA: Removing quarterly reports tilts the playing field toward institutions... we need quarterly reports to keep them honest

name: Eric Patterson

name: Jacob G

name: james johnson
rationale_evidence: NR: No. No. No.

name: Jeff Heynen

name: John Hummel

name: Joseph Ford

name: Lucas Frisch

name: Melanie Bieder

name: michael bailey

name: Mike

name: Mike Moore

name: Nick Selby

name: Ryan Finch
rationale_evidence: MF: I prefer more frequent company reporting in order to make better investment decisions for my retirement and financial livelihood

name: Satyam C Patel

summary: An average retail investor opposes the change, arguing retail investors rely on quarterly updates and earnings calls, that less frequent reporting helps fraudsters hide, and that the cost to corporations is negligible.

name: Christina Passafaro

name: Burwood Nowicki-McFarland

name: Doris Youngman

It's important both for transparency and trust; AU: Compliance Reporting is part of ethical due diligence and has historically been a safe guard for the public good
summary: A financial controller and accounting/finance consultant opposes the change, arguing compliance reporting is part of ethical due diligence and a historical safeguard for the public good, transparency, and trust.

name: Ben Zink

summary: A technical specialist opposes the change, arguing quarterly reporting keeps investors better informed about the risks they take.

rationale_evidence: NR: We the PEOPLE not we the corporations.

name: Shivaram Rajgopal, Columbia Business School
rationale_evidence: EX: the release counts issuer savings but leaves downstream user costs unquantified; MF: market-quality, liquidity, price-discovery and risk-pricing harms; IA: a two-tier market favoring alt-data institutions over retail; ICc: the UK 2007/2014 natural experiment shows no short-termism or investment effect; ST: halving frequency does not cure managerial myopia; CB: the relieved cost is immaterial (a 19,000-to-1 return on 10-K compliance); AU: fewer interim reviews mean problems surface later; RI: index providers, rating agencies and analysts rely on quarterly data; US: the quarterly clock underpins the most liquid equity market in the world.
summary: Columbia accounting professor Shivaram Rajgopal submits a detailed opposition arguing the release's economic analysis is structurally incomplete, the UK natural experiment refutes the short-termism rationale, the relieved cost is immaterial, and the correct reform is to streamline the 10-Q's contents rather than halve its frequency.

name: Tammey Napier
rationale_evidence: IA: another way to help corporations prosper while the average investor is kept in the dark; IP: quarterly disclosure keeps everyone honest so the little people stay informed.
summary: A retired investor on disability opposes the change, arguing it helps corporations at the expense of average investors who need quarterly disclosure to stay informed.

name: Vincent Colletti
rationale_evidence: MF: detrimental to investors who rely on knowing how companies are performing; opens the way for all kinds of unscrupulous behavior.

summary: A systems analyst opposes the change and argues that if anything reporting should be more frequent for greater transparency.

name: Deepak Rajagopal
summary: (Awaiting Claude classification.)
